children.content: Uzo Z.T.
80,000 words
Oasis
Chapter 1: The Fabric of Fate
The great steppe, sandwiched in wind and sand.
Fu Yuan, the Grand Advisor, sits atop a colossal boulder like a dark, watchful eagle. Behind him, his black cloak — the wings — snaps impatiently.
He gazes at the distant sunset.
“Do you know what fate is?
It is like wind — formless and unseen, yet everywhere at once. It is like desert sand — right under your feet, yet its whole can never be grasped.”
A disciple asks, “If fate is without form, then how do we master it?”

“Master it?” Fu Yuan scoffs. “Fate cannot be mastered! Can you master wind? Can you control sand? Yet you can use wind to fill sails, and sand to build cities. The Fabric of Fate spreads under your feet. Those seemingly useless snags are its clues.”
Another disciple frowns. “But the snags are so small. What use could they be?”
Fu Yuan sweeps over him with his cold gaze.

“Small?” His voice is sharp. “Only because your sight is narrow does its weight escape you. Have you heard of the eagle catching the wind? It soars in the gale because it knows how to borrow the storm’s fury. If it only knew how to hide, it would be nothing but a caged bird.”
“So it is with the fabric of fate. If you are blind to them, you are not a man. You are a speck of dust on that fabric.”
Fu Yuan rises, shaking out his cloak.

“But not every snag should be pulled. Some are the tails of venomous snakes; pull one, and you invite endless suffering. But others are the reins of a heavenly steed; grasp one, and you will see a higher plane of destiny.
How do you tell the difference? That — is your own fate.”
The disciples fall silent.
Fu Yuan gives a humorless smile.

“Very well. I will give you one last truth: These snags are not for the weak. Those destined to be dust will never see the patterns in the fabric. But the truly strong — they require no teaching from me. They will leverage the snags to weave patterns of their own.”
He turns his back on them and walks into the depth of the steppe, leaving only the wind and sand to whisper in their ears.
Chapter 2: The Moon and the Finger
Deep twilight settles.

Fu Yuan stands before the husk of a temple, surrounded by monks and believers, their faces masks of piety. Inside, an idol presides — eyes vacant, face cold. Incense smoke coils toward the rafters as the air thickens with the taste of scorching ash.
An old monk collapses to his knees, his voice trembling.
“Grand Advisor! Your grace is as sacred as this idol. We will honor you as the living truth. We will follow you, we will sustain you!”
The others drop in a wave, foreheads striking the dust. A chant rises: “The Grand Advisor is merciful! The Grand Advisor is boundless!”
Fu Yuan looks down at the prostrate mass, a cold sneer on his lips. He lifts a boot and plants it on the idol’s pedestal.
“You cannot see the moon’s splendor, because you only know how to kiss my toes. And even if one of you looks up, your eyes never leave the finger I point with.”
The crowd freezes. They lift their heads, their eyes blank with incomprehension.
Fu Yuan’s gaze sweeps over them, his voice a roll of thunder.
“Worship is a shackle, not a liberation. The abject soul who kneels before gods and gurus has no right to speak of Enlightenment! Do you think bowing and scraping can be traded for truth?
Truth demands no kneeling! You kneel to your own cowardice. You worship your own fear.”
A young monk flinches, stammering. “But… are the gods not supreme? We are only mortals. How can we compare ourselves to them?”
Fu Yuan gives him a glacial look.
“If you believe you are mortal, then a mortal you will remain. Truth has no pity for slaves. It favors only those who dare to meet the gods’ gaze as equals.”
He says nothing more.
He turns and walks away, leaving the monks paralyzed in the dirt. Before the ruined temple, the wind gusts, and the last of the incense flickers, on the verge of dying out.
It is as if the gods themselves are laughing at their folly.
Chapter 3: The Cycle of the Waves
Deep in the night, the sea glitters under a cold moon, The boat cuts like a blade through the swells.
Fu Yuan stands alone at the prow, his robes snapping in the wind. His gaze is locked on the rise and fall of the waves, his focus so absolute he could be deciphering a celestial script.
At the rudder, the boatman hesitates. Finally, he finds his courage.
“Grand Advisor,” he asks, his voice low, “these waves... you watch them with such intensity. Forgive me, but are you searching for something?”
Fu Yuan does not turn.
“And what do you imagine I am searching for?”
The boatman bows his head. “It is beyond me. It is just...” his voice trembling, “a wave is such a fleeting thing. A moment here, then gone. How can it hold any real meaning?”
A cold laugh escapes Fu Yuan’s lips.
“You call it fleeting because you see only the instant it crests. You fail to see that it never truly vanishes.
Each wave is a thought, given form by the ocean. In the sea’s silence, it is the sea. When it rises, it is the sea’s awareness — for a moment, gathered into a self.”
The boatman looks up, his eyes full of confusion. “But when it breaks, is it not gone forever?”
Fu Yuan turns.
“It returns to the ocean, to await the next cycle. When it rises again, it is both itself and not itself.
So too, is life.”
The boatman grips the rudder, his voice trembling with awe. “Then what is the point of our struggle? If this cycle is all there is, what can any of us truly do?”
Fu Yuan’s gaze is a torch, laying bare the boatman’s terror.
“You are a wave. You cannot escape the ocean. That is true. But you can choose how to crest.
The fool is lost in the terror of the fall. The wise, with each return, carves himself more deeply.
The sea remembers the waves that truly crest. The others are only foam, forgotten even by themselves.”
The boatman stares at the black, churning water below, lost in thought. At last, he lowers his head.
“Grand Advisor,” he whispers, “how do I make the sea... remember me?”
Fu Yuan laughs, a sharp bark of sound against the night. He looks to the horizon, where the sea meets sky.
“First, ask yourself if you can truly see the wave you are in this moment.”
The boatman says nothing more, but the hand that grips the rudder trembles. His gaze falls to the waves, and in their motion, he sees a meaning he has never seen before.
And at the prow, Fu Yuan remains, his gaze still bound to the churning sea, an expression of fierce, profound regard.
The wind passes. The waves surge. And all of creation falls silent.
Chapter 4: The Footprints of the Wild Geese
The sky is scoured clean, the autumn wind like a blade.
A cry from the wild geese rends the heavens. High above, the flock carves a battle line across the sky.
Fu Yuan stands at the precipice, his gaze pushing past the horizon, as if listening for a resonance from the edge of the world itself.
Behind him, an emissary from the Great Yuan kneels, his face carved with earnest pleading.

“Grand Advisor, the realm has descended into chaos, its people cast into misery. If you would but end your seclusion and restore the Great Yuan, you would not only pacify this age of strife, but shelter a world of souls.”
Fu Yuan does not turn.
“What is the Yuan’s fate to me?”
The emissary flinches. “Grand Advisor, was the prosperity of this realm not forged by your own hand? With our world now in ruins, how can you watch and do nothing?”
Fu Yuan’s eyes track the flock’s distant passage.
“Do you know that the geese never look back at their own footprints? Their gaze is always on what lies ahead.”

The wind from the north is a physical blow, yet sweat beads the emissary’s brow. “But... for the geese to fly, they require a vast sky,” he argues, “just as the people require the Grand Advisor’s mantle —“
Fu Yuan cuts him off with a sweep of his sleeve.
“The Mandate is withdrawn.
The prints in the mud are the Yuan’s lesson to learn. The geese have already found their own sky.”
He turns and departs, leaving the emissary to his kneeling.
His disciples press in around him, their faces alight with awe. “Master, your transcendence is absolute!”
One disciple’s gaze is a fire.
“Master, you see through the heart of all things. Why not forge a new teaching, one to surpass all others, to guide every soul in the land?
Your wisdom would echo for ten thousand generations. You would become an eternal beacon for the world!”
By now the flock is a mere thread in the heavens, yet its cry still reverberates between earth and sky. Fu Yuan stops, his eyes circling his followers.
“A goose crosses the mud, leaves a print, and soars again. Does it cross the mud for the sake of leaving a print?”
The disciples can only trade empty glances.
Fu Yuan’s voice is glacial.
“Your ‘religion’ is a scepter. Your ‘eternity’ is a cage.
True freedom is not to make the world gaze up at one beacon. It is to teach each person how to walk with the stars.”
With that, he turns his back, his robes dancing in the autumn wind, dissolving into the vast twilight.
Chapter 5: The Goshawk and Freedom
A spring wind, heavy with the scent of wild grass, sweeps across the steppes.

The grasslands come alive as warblers dart and weave. High above, goshawks ride the currents, their cries echoing in the immense quiet. Yurts scatter the plains like sleeping animals, and herds of horses graze in a deep peace.
Between earth and sky, life itself is an unstoppable tide.

Before the largest of the yurts, Fu Yuan sits on a felt rug. Mutton and wine are laid out before him, but he pours only for himself, drinking with a quiet intensity. To his side, a long-browed monk in grey robes is seated on a cushion, still as stone, eyes shut in meditation.
The silence between them is a world of its own.
Then, suddenly, the monk opens his eyes.
“Grand Advisor,” he begins, his voice smooth and measured, “you believe life possesses a hierarchy, do you not?”
A dangerous light sparks in Fu Yuan’s eyes. He holds the monk’s gaze for a long moment, the barest hint of a smile on his lips.
“I do.”
He glances at the sheep on the hillside, then at the wild horses thundering on the horizon.

“Given the choice, who would be a sheep, born only to kneel for the knife? Why not be the horse, a creature of pure, untamed spirit? Or better still...” His gaze climbs, finding a hawk circling in the deep blue. “...that goshawk, a king in its own sky?”
The old monk’s smile is faint, a mask of pity.

“And you, Grand Advisor, are the great hawk of these plains. While the people of the Central Kingdoms are the sheep. This old monk has journeyed here today to plead with you: stay your talons. Do not throw yourself into the conflicts of the Central Kingdoms.”
One of Fu Yuan’s eyebrows arches, his stare sharp enough to cut.

The monk presses his palms together, his head bowed. “If you would but hold the suffering of the people in your heart,” he murmurs, “in a single moment of compassion, you could spare all sentient beings a great calamity.”
Fu Yuan laughs, a sharp, metallic sound. He raises his wine cup.
“I am the hawk — why should the bleating of sheep concern me?”
The monk shakes his head, his tone soft, yet immovable. “The Enlightened One taught us: all beings carry the Buddha-nature within; rank and station are but shadows on a wall.
The hawk and the sheep alike follow the path karma has laid for them. Grand Advisor, why hold so tightly to this illusion of strong and weak?”
Fu Yuan drains all warmth from his face. In an instant, his gaze turns cold.
“Guards!” he barks.
“Release the lamb. Roast the Master instead.”

Attendants burst from the yurt, slam the monk to the ground, and bind him. The monk’s face turns the color of dust. He struggles against the ropes, his voice cracking as he cries out, “A man of your stature... resorting to such barbarism! This is madness!”
A great, booming laugh escapes Fu Yuan. He waves a dismissive hand, and the guards cut the monk free.
“You preach that all are equal. So tell me, what is the difference between roasting you, and roasting a lamb?”

The monk gasps for air, pushing himself to his knees. The composure is gone, replaced by a chilling uncertainty. “Grand Advisor,” he whispers, “equality is the soul of compassion. It cannot be a weapon for the strong to wield against the weak.”
Fu Yuan’s gaze drives into the monk like a nail.
“Your ‘equality’ is a pittance you grant from on high.
The sheep kneels in the dirt for the sake of survival. The hawk commands the sky for the sake of freedom. The tiers of life are not measured by strength, but by freedom.”
The monk’s expression is a storm of confusion and fear. “Grand Advisor,” he says, his voice heavy, “here on these endless plains, what can possibly limit your freedom?
Who in this world could ever hope to constrain
you
Fu Yuan’s eyes look past him, past the horizon, as if to pierce the veil of the cosmos itself.
“This
world
cannot stop me. But the Heavens can.
The freedom I hunt is not from the shackles of men. It is the freedom from illusion itself —
and from everything they dare to call ‘truth’.”
The monk can only stare, the wind whipping at his grey robes, stealing the unspoken words from his lips. At last, he lowers his head.
“If the Grand Advisor’s mind is set, then this old monk has nothing more to say.”
Fu Yuan gives no reply. He throws back his head and drains his cup, his eyes once again fixed on the goshawk, a solitary speck turning in the vast, silent heavens.
It is as if they are the only two beings in existence, sharing the terrible, beautiful emptiness between the earth and the sky.
Chapter 6: The Farmer and the Wheat Field
Gold light spills across a wheat field at sunset. A breeze sends it rolling in endless waves.
Fu Yuan and his disciples cut a path along its edge, stopping at an old well.
A farmer spies them from afar, drops his hoe, and hurries over, wiping sweat from his brow as he bows.
“Beggin’ your pardon, Grand Advisor. Let this old farmer draw you and the masters a bucket. Please.”
He rolls up his sleeves, wipes the well’s stone slab clean, and gestures for Fu Yuan to sit. Fu Yuan nods, but he casts his gaze out over the wheat, lost in appreciation of its deep, silent swell.
A creak and a groan of the rope, and the farmer offers up a bucket of clear, cool water. Fu Yuan takes the offered ladle. He sips.
“Cold,” he says. “But sweet.”
His eyes remain fixed on the horizon.
The disciples, however, are lost in their own world.
“Brother, the Way is the Prime Mover, formless yet sovereign over the Ten Thousand Things!”
“Precisely! To apprehend its celestial architecture is to transcend this mortal dust!”
Fu Yuan listens, a shadow of a sneer on his lips as he drinks.
The farmer, standing respectfully to one side, is utterly captivated. After a moment, he dares to speak, his voice soft.
“The Way... isn’t it just like this water? Doesn’t matter who you are, if you’re thirsty, you can always draw a scoop.”
The disciples round on him. “Silence! You think a peasant can speak of the Way in the Grand Advisor’s presence?”
The farmer flinches. “I... I spoke out of turn. Forgive an old man’s foolishness.”
Fu Yuan lowers the ladle, his gaze falling upon the farmer with a flicker of genuine interest.
“He’s right.”
He turns on his disciples.
“What the hell does the Way have to do with you? His words have more truth than a thousand volumes of your nonsense.”
They fall silent.
Fu Yuan turns back to the farmer. “You can read the Truth in the well water and the wheat field. Why not walk with me? See what lies beyond them.”

The farmer is speechless. His lips move, but no words come. He just bows his head. “Grand Advisor, I’m a tiller of the earth, nothing more. How could I dare? I’m a simple man. I have no other skills.”
Fu Yuan’s gaze holds him — sharp, but now with a strange warmth. He points toward the fields.
“Does any stalk of wheat in your field stop growing because it is short?”
The farmer shakes his head.
“Does it refuse to ripen because it envies the one next to it?”
Again, he shakes his head.
Fu Yuan’s voice cracks like a whip.
“Then why, because you are a farmer, are you content to be a seed that never breaks the soil?!”
He sees the terror in the man’s eyes and scoffs.
“Transcendence is not a prize for the clever. Those monks and their endless chanting are just sheep circling in the mud.
Better to be a foolish stalk of wheat, always reaching up, than a sheep forever on its knees.”
The farmer stands frozen, the ladle trembling in his hand. Shame and a spark of something new war in his eyes.
Fu Yuan gives him no further glance. He turns and leaves.
The wheat field rolls and surges. Carried back on the wind, his voice echoes.
“Remember. Not every eagle is born knowing the sky, but every seed of wheat knows how to break the earth...”
Chapter 7: The Statue and I
It was the height of summer, yet a pall of dread hung over the city.
The Great Khan was dying. One by one, his chief ministers fell gravely ill. The most learned physicians of the Court confessed their helplessness, and a quiet panic took root in the palace.
Fu Yuan sat by the sickbed, his gaze fixed on the unconscious Khan. After a long silence, he spoke.
“This is sorcery. Medicine is useless here.”

By his order, a house-to-house search was conducted. It ended in a lone cottage on the city’s edge where they found a collection of wooden statues. The craftsmanship was uncanny; each was a perfect, living image of its victim: the Khan, his ministers, and Fu Yuan himself. Fine silver needles bristled from each carving.
Fu Yuan commanded that the needles be plucked out and the statues burned. All but one. He kept his own.
The moment the others were consumed by fire, the Khan and his ministers awakened, the sickness gone as if it had never been. But amidst the relief, a question was whispered: how was Fu Yuan immune?
Later, Fu Yuan holds the statue of himself, a mocking light in his eyes.

“Even the noblest minister, even the invincible Khan — none can escape the fate of being defined by others,” he murmurs. “These definitions are mirrors, and every man mistakes the reflection he sees for his true self.”
He traces the carved face with a long, steady finger, his voice a strange mix of admiration and contempt.
“The man who carved this is a master without equal.”
Then he leans in, his voice dropping to a whisper meant only for the wood.
“Define every man in this world you may. But how could you hope to ever define this man — Fu Yuan?”
He raises his voice to the empty air. “In this age, who truly knows
me
He waves a hand. The candle flames across the room flicker as one, then die.

The attendants take the hint. They bow and retreat, leaving Fu Yuan alone with his wooden double. Moonlight cuts through the ornate window lattice, casting bars of silver and shadow over the two figures.
For the men watching from the doorway, a bizarre illusion takes hold. In the shifting light, they can no longer be sure —
Which is the man? And which is the wood?
Chapter 8: In the Mountains
Fu Yuan walks alone through endless green as twilight deepens.
Ahead, a grass hut. Before it sits a hermit in rags.
“Stranger,” the hermit calls out. “The view ahead is the same as the view here. Why not sit with me”
Fu Yuan stops. He walks to a green stone beside the man and sits.
The hermit does not look at him, speaking as if to the air.
“That stone you sit on is washed by rain day after day, yet it holds its shape. Sometimes, I wonder if the stone is merely enduring the rain, or if the rain is what forges the stone.”
Fu Yuan laughs.
“Then I am like the stone.”
A light flickers in the hermit’s eyes. “A novel claim, stranger. I would hear more.”
“Of the students who come to me,” Fu Yuan begins, his voice even, “those who seek martial skill become the greatest fighters of the age. Those who learn strategy now command armies.

Under my guidance, a prince becomes the ruler of the world. Through my instruction, a commoner becomes a man of terrifying power. They all go forth proclaiming my virtue and my skill, honoring me as the supreme teacher, until the entire world knows the name Fu Yuan.”
He gestures to the empty mountains.
“You see? Is it not they who have made me?”
The stream murmurs, a whisper on the wind, speaking the words Fu Yuan leaves unsaid. After a time, the hermit nods.
“And yet,” he asks, “how do you ‘endure’ them?”
A cold smile touches Fu Yuan’s lips.
“Not a single one is willing to learn what truly brings value to life.

I teach them to let go, to gain a greater freedom. Instead, they scramble to gather what I leave in my wake, using my cast-offs to adorn themselves. They are a swarm of grasping fools who only want to take, and never once consider what they ought to throw away.”
The hermit nods. “So, for all your power and wisdom, you are left with only one choice: to endure.”
A bitter laugh. “Have you any liquor?” Fu Yuan asks.
The hermit shakes his head. “No liquor. Just spring water.”
He offers a ladle, caked with grime.
Fu Yuan takes it without a glance. He drains it in a single motion and lets out a great cry.
“Good wine!”
Then they speak no more. The moon climbs to its zenith, and a night wind soughs through the mountain pass.
They sit. Not another word is exchanged, leaving only the Ten Thousand Sounds of the night.
Chapter 9: The Snowflake
Fu Yuan leads the Khan to a mountain peak, into the heart of a swirling snowstorm. Below, the world is split in two.
On one side, a war camp. Men huddle around flickering fires against the cold. On the other, the enemy city — a black beast of stone, silent, waiting.
The Khan’s fury finally breaks. “Fu Yuan! What the hell is this? You think I’m some damn tourist?”
“I want that city!” he roars. “Or you can just give me your head!”
Fu Yuan pays him no mind, extending a hand to catch the falling snow.
“All you ever see is the world you want to own. I’d wager you’ve never once stopped to look at a snowflake.”
The Khan mutters, “What’s to see? They’re all the same. A little flower, right? Six sides or somethin’.”
Fu Yuan shakes his head, admiring the crystals on his glove.
“No two are ever alike. Each begins with a speck of dust. Its journey through the falling air — the heat, the cold — is what forges its unique shape.”
His gaze cuts through the storm.
“Like a life, shaped by its trials into something singular.”
“And what good is that?” the Khan snarls. “They fall, they turn to mud, and they get in my way!” He stabs his whip toward the heavens —
“Fuck you!”
A ghost of a smile touches Fu Yuan’s lips.
“True. In a crowd, they lose themselves. The heavens can use them to trap your armies. But if you learn to use them, they become a weapon of pure destruction.”
The Khan goes still. A new, sharp light enters his eyes.
Fu Yuan’s voice is ice.

“Those snowflakes are your soldiers. Your people. As a mob, they have one will, one direction. It can be aimed toward good, or toward evil. A ruler’s job is to aim that force, not be swept away by it.”
The Khan’s brow furrows. “You’re always talkin’ about the one man, about freedom. Why tell me to forget the one and rule the mob?”
Fu Yuan’s eyes rake over him, his smile pure mockery.
“Freedom?
The heart of a snowflake is dust. Its destiny is to fall, to be buried in the mass. Good, evil... it has no say. It can only be used. By a man of power. By someone like you.”
He closes his hand.
“To make it free, I would have to make it cease to be a snowflake.”
The next day, the city is gone.
In the command tent, the air is thick with shouts and spilled liquor.
“To the Great Khan! To bury a city in an avalanche — such wisdom will be sung of for a thousand generations!”
The Khan roars with laughter and raises his cup. But across the chaos, his gaze finds Fu Yuan in the corner, hot and intense.
Fu Yuan ignores him. He pushes aside the heavy tent flap and walks out into the cold.

The storm breathes its last. Sunlight breaks through the thick clouds, spilling in great shafts onto the snowfield below. Down where the city stood, there is only a white, level plain, as if it had never been.
He holds out his hand and catches one last snowflake. He watches it vanish in a blink — a flash of water, then nothing. He stares at that last speck of dust in his palm, as if searching for a secret.

An attendant at his side ventures, “Grand Advisor... the flake melts in your hand, as we are liberated when we learn your wisdom and find our freedom. But what of all the others? Must they wait for the sun? For a perfect ruler to come and set them all free?”
Fu Yuan gives a dry, empty laugh.
“And what would they be then? A cloud of mist. The moment the air grows cold, they will freeze again.
They will fall again.”
The attendant stands, silenced.
Under the warm sun, the packed snow begins to thaw. A sense of life returns to the world. When he looks again, Fu Yuan is already far down the path.
The attendant hurries after him.
Chapter 10: The Little Prince
A dreamy midsummer night at the imperial garden.

The air is a strange, intoxicating perfume of lamp oil and exotic blooms. Beyond a sea of treetops, the palace’s golden dome shines like a captive moon. In the spray of a jade fountain, deer that shimmer like living gold play in the water. Seeing Fu Yuan, they scatter with a final, playful bound into the shadows.
Huddled at the base of an old tree, like a sparrow beaten by the rain, is the little prince. He is crying.
He sees Fu Yuan and scrambles to his feet, bowing. “Grand Advisor...” his voice a watery whisper, “My father says fear is for the weak. He says I’m a coward... unfit to be the future ruler.”
Fu Yuan laughs.
“And did he tell you what he fears?”
The prince looks up, confused. “The Khan is the greatest hero under heaven. What could he possibly fear?”
Fu Yuan shakes his head, a note of melancholy in his voice.
“He fears old age. He fears losing control. He fears his name will be forgotten... But those fears, little prince, are the very things that forged your father. They are what guide him still.”
Suddenly, he raises a hand.
A bat swoops from the darkness as if pulled by an invisible string, landing perfectly in his open palm. The prince’s eyes go wide, staring at the impossible sight.
“Come with me,” Fu Yuan says, turning toward a ruined wing of the palace.

Inside, the hall is pitch-black. Faint moonlight filters through the broken lattice of a high window, tracing faint patterns on the dusty floor. Fu Yuan tears a strip from the hem of his robe, wraps it around the bat’s ears, and releases it.
A moment later, a dull thud echoes in the hall. The bat lies on the floor, a crumpled, broken leaf.
“What happened to it?” the prince asks, his voice trembling.

“It lost the ears that hear its fear,” Fu Yuan says. He retrieves the bat, removes the cloth, and releases it back into the gloom. Instantly, it takes flight, carving several perfect, graceful arcs through the darkness.

“Fear,” Fu Yuan explains, “is like the sound the bat makes, a sound humans cannot hear. It is a call it sends out into the world, and the echo that returns tells it everything. The echo tells it where the walls are, and where the path is open. With its fear, it can own the night. Without it, it cannot move an inch.”
In the deep shadows of the hall, Fu Yuan’s silhouette seems to loom larger. His dark robes flow in the draft, and for a moment, he seems to become a great bat himself.
His voice is low and warm.
“The warrior turns fear into fuel. The sage observes it. The Buddha teaches you to let it go, so that it might vanish. But they are all wrong.
Fear is not the enemy. Is a man who fears the dark any different from one who fears fear itself?”
Fu Yuan’s gaze cuts through the darkness and lands on the prince.
“Darkness is not a foe. It is a door. The bat trusts the dark, because it knows the truest boundaries are the ones that echo back.”
The little prince stares at him, his eyes wide and shining in the gloom, reflecting a storm of confusion, curiosity, and awe.
“Fear is the tool of life’s own evolution,” Fu Yuan continues with a soft sigh.

“A man who has lost his fear is like a bat that has lost its ears. He cannot fly; he cannot even survive. Fear is the mechanism by which we transcend ourselves. It shows us our limits, and only by knowing our limits can we ever hope to surpass them.
To wield fear is to be controlled by it. To flee from fear is to be captured by it. In either the obsession or the battle, the chance to understand is lost.”
A commotion echoes from the gardens. A party of men carrying torches can be seen, their voices frantic.
“Little Prince? Your Highness, the Little Prince?!”
“The Great Khan is looking for you!”
“His Majesty says he was wrong! He wishes to apologize to you himself!”
The prince looks up. The tears on his face have dried, leaving shining tracks in the torchlight. “Grand Advisor,” he says, his voice now clear. “Father is looking for me.”
Fu Yuan raises his hand to wipe the tear tracks from the boy’s face.
But the prince takes a small step back and scrubs them away himself with his own sleeve.
“Grand Advisor. I will go now.”
Fu Yuan smiles. “Remember,” he says. “Let your fear be the ladder you climb.”
With a wave of his hand, he dismisses him.
“Go. Don’t make your great father
fear
any longer.”
Chapter 11: The Khan’s Horse
The horn tears through the silence of the great steppe. The sun is new, but the grasslands have already erupted in a chaos of thundering hooves and shouting men.

A pack of generals surrounds the Khan, running down a herd of antelope. Suddenly, a mottled hawk dives, stealing the kill before they can reach it. Amid the curses of his generals, the Khan roars with laughter, spurs his horse, draws his bow into a moon, and puts an arrow clean through its wing.
The bird cries out, tumbling into the grass. The generals cheer.
But the Khan sighs. “Brother, these good days will be over soon, I’m afraid.”
Fu Yuan nods.
“You conquered the world with that bow. Now that the world is yours, perhaps it’s time to put it down.”
The Khan gives a dry laugh, his gaze turning inward.

“Listen to this. This man has a few of those ‘celebrity scholars’ sniffing around him these days... They say an emperor has to civilize the people. Me? I can’t read a word. How am I supposed to do that?”
Fu Yuan turns in his saddle and shouts.
“Your Majesty! What is it that won you this world?”
The Khan points his whip at his men and shouts back.
“My riders of course! Fast as wind, deadly as ghosts!”
“Long live the Great Khan!” The warriors erupt into thunderous roar.
Fu Yuan leans in, his voice for the Khan alone.
“And now that there is peace, do you still need to train more warhorses?”
The Khan’s expression shifts. “No. In peacetime, you need workhorses. Something to pull a cart. Something that obeys.”
“Precisely,” Fu Yuan says. “So, ‘civilizing’ is just raising horses. You must know what kind you want, and why. Did the scholars ever ask you that?”
The Khan stiffens. He snaps his whip in the air. “Useless eggheads!”
They reach a watering hole and dismount, sitting on the earth while their horses drink.
The Khan is still chewing on the thought. “Warhorses for war, workhorses for peace... simple enough. But between you and me... I’d pick a wild stallion like you any day of the week, my brother.”
Fu Yuan just smiles.
The Khan’s brow furrows. “But Fu Yuan... if I raise too many wild, unbroken men like you... won’t I lose my throne?”
Fu Yuan looks out across the misty plains.
“That depends. Are you asking about today, or tomorrow?

Today, you may need horses that only know how to obey. But tomorrow, if the grassland is rich enough, every horse can find its place. Then the wild ones won’t betray you. They’ll expand your borders for you.”
The Khan nods slowly. “So, you’re saying the world today can’t handle too many free spirits?”
“The order of the herd,” Fu Yuan says simply, “is never decided by the horses. It’s decided by the steppe.
If the land is rich, you don’t need to fear the strong. If it’s barren, even the tame ones will bite through their reins.”
Just then, Fu Yuan’s horse shies and bolts, galloping toward a distant herd of mustangs.
“Hey!” the Khan says. “Your horse...”
“Let it go,” Fu Yuan says with a calm smile. “It was always wild.”

The Khan stares, then a slow grin spreads across his face. “I get it now. Here you are, teaching me how to break horses and men, and you ride a wild thing yourself. That’s a different beast entirely.”
Fu Yuan gazes into the distance.
“I have my own way. It’s of no use to you. Maybe in a thousand years, someone will figure out how to tame a horse without breaking its spirit. But this prairie will be a different world by then.”
He lets out a long, sharp whistle. From the distant herd, a mustang breaks away and gallops to him, nuzzling his shoulder.
Fu Yuan swings onto its back. “Enough talk. Before you go back to playing emperor, how about a race?
Let’s see what’s faster — your emperor’s prized champion, or my wild blood!”
The Khan throws his head back and laughs, his voice a playful challenge. “Don’t you dare hold back, my dear Grand Advisor! This one will see you at the next hill!”
He snaps his reins and charges, and the two riders thunder into the morning mist.
Far behind them, a wounded hawk struggles, finds its wings, and climbs back into the sky.
Chapter 12: The White Wolf
Snow falls from a lead-gray sky. Across the boundless steppe, a lone convoy rides.

Ahead, a pack of wolves is running down an old deer. The alpha, a brute, tears at its hind legs, driving it into a corner. Seeing the raw sport of it, some of Fu Yuan’s men begin to cheer. The sight fills Fu Yuan’s face with undisguised contempt.
“The pack may have strength,” he says, “but its blind devotion to a beast like that alpha denies it any knowledge of true freedom.”
While his men stand dumbfounded, Fu Yuan unleashes a thunderous roar: “BEGONE!”
The blast hits the alpha, buckling its legs. It glares back, but a sweep of Fu Yuan’s icy gaze sends it running, tail tucked between its legs, the pack dissolving into the snow behind it.
As the snow falls heavier and the wolves are gone, the party makes camp.
Some time later, a long, clear howl rises from the distance, as if a summons. Hearing it, Fu Yuan steps from his tent.

The sky has cleared. A million pillars of light now stand upon the steppe. On a distant cliff, a great white wolf stands, proud and alone. Its fur glows in the sun, a living creature carved from snow.
As Fu Yuan’s eyes meet the wolf’s, the wolf’s eyes meet his.
A look of profound pleasure appears on Fu Yuan’s face. An attendant, seeing his mood, ventures, “Grand Advisor, why do you favor that lone wolf so?”

“You call it ‘lone’ because you see an outcast,” Fu Yuan corrects him. “I call it ‘sovereign’ because it has chosen its sovereignty. To walk alone is the path of the sage who refuses to be swept up by the crowd, and the warrior with a will so strong he is immune to the group’s temptations.”
He gestures to the empty tracks in the snow.

“A wolf pack is the world of men. The alpha is its leader. He is but an illusion of strength. In truth, his heart is weak; he must suppress others to display his own power, a power that is in truth a dependency on their approval.
The alpha is merely the pack’s ignorance given a face. His rule ensures the pack can never evolve.”
“But surely, Grand Advisor,” the attendant presses, “the pack needs a leader? Surely men need a king?”
Fu Yuan laughs. But the laugh is so cold it makes the men flinch.

“The alpha offers a brief and brutal order, but his greed and selfishness plant the seeds of the pack’s destruction. Every alpha-hero in history has ultimately become a source of mankind’s suffering. They use their power to protect themselves, crushing wisdom beneath the scepter, ensuring the pack can never evolve.”
“Then what of the sovereign wolf?” the attendant asks, lost. “What can its purpose be?”
Fu Yuan’s eyes grow colder.
“Its very existence is proof that another possibility exists.”
He turns and disappears into his tent.
The next morning, a man is brought before Fu Yuan. He kneels, offering up a perfect, white wolf pelt with both hands.
“A gift, Grand Advisor, a token of my devotion!” he proclaims.
An attendant explains that the man, hearing of Fu Yuan’s admiration, trapped and killed the wolf. “This pelt can ward off the most bitter cold. It is a peerless treasure!”
Fu Yuan explodes.
“Guards! Take this wretch out and skin him alive!”
The man screams for his life as he is dragged away. In the tent, the men can only stare at one another, afraid to breathe.
Fu Yuan cradles the pelt to his chest. And weeps.
When he finally stops, he murmurs to himself in his grief, “The sovereign wolf is no more, yet the pack endures.”
He sweeps his dead eyes over the men.
“The sovereign wolf is the orphan of humanity’s ascent... and its seed. Now, that seed has been snuffed out by mediocrity.
The hope for that ascent has just grown that much more distant.”
Chapter 13: The Philanthropist and the Good Tree
“This humble student, Liu Zijing of the Southlands, pays his respects to the Grand Advisor.”

A man in magnificent robes, a person of distinguished bearing, bows deeply to Fu Yuan. At his side, Fu Ce introduces them with boyish enthusiasm. “Uncle, this is Liu Zijing! He is a titan of industry, the greatest philanthropist in all the Southlands!”
Liu Zijing bows again. “You are too kind. But it is true. I have dedicated the greater part of my fortune to charitable works.”
Fu Yuan asks simply, “And you, Zijing. Is the wealth you’ve kept enough for your family?”
Liu Zijing smiles.

“Your concern does me great honor, Grand Advisor. This one secured his wealth two decades ago; the fortune I have amassed would last my family for generations. Besides, material comforts mean little to me. I wish only to spend what remains of my life doing good. And by some blessing of the Buddha, the more I give, the more my wealth seems to grow.”
Fu Yuan is quiet for a moment.
“The weather is lovely. I suspect you have never seen the Imperial Garden. Might I invite you for a stroll?”
Overjoyed, Liu Zijing and Fu Ce hurry to follow.
The garden is another world. As Liu Zijing drinks in the sights, his awe gives way to a profound reverence.
“I have always believed wealth could purchase anything,” he sighs. “Only now do I see the true splendor of power.”
Fu Yuan smiles faintly. “Splendor, yes... but not without its regrets.”

He leads them toward an abandoned courtyard. The moment they pass through the gate, a chill falls upon them, unnatural in the summer heat. A colossal tree stands alone in the center, its canopy a vast, dark stain blotting out the sun. In the oppressive gloom beneath it, only shade-loving things survive—stunted shrubs, mushrooms, moss. Any rare flowers once planted there have long since withered.

But the true horror is the roots. Like a nest of black pythons, they have burst through the flagstones, shattering them and strangling the foundations of the courtyard walls and pavilions, leaving them in a state of ruin.
“How can such a place exist within the Palace?” Liu Zijing stammers, aghast.
Fu Yuan’s voice is calm.

“This tree is called the ‘Good Tree,’ a gift from a king in the Western Deserts. There, amidst the savage winds, it is a tenacious miracle of life. It hoards water from the very air, offering shade from the punishing sun to weary travelers. For this, they named it ‘good’.”
He gestures to the devastation around them.

“Here, however, in this fertile land, its goodness becomes a tyranny. It seeks to shelter all things, but in doing so, it starves them of light, hoards the richness of the earth, and its roots tear down the very structures that surround it.”
Fu Ce ponders this aloud. “So you mean to say, Uncle, that even charity must be done in measure?”
Fu Yuan’s gaze settles on Liu Zijing.

“You have long since secured your own fortune, yet you have never stopped expanding your businesses. What of the merchants whose dreams you have crushed? What of the people whose lives you have changed, who now live as stunted shrubs in your shadow? How do you know that, left to find their own sun, they might not have grown into great trees themselves?”
The blood drains from Liu Zijing’s face. “I... I never conceived of it that way.”
Fu Yuan looks out, beyond the courtyard, to where the rest of the garden grows in vibrant, harmonious life.
“There is a world of difference between doing good and being good. The first is to impose your will upon the world, like this tree. It is an attempt to shelter everything that ends in ruin.
Being good is to become a part of the world’s harmony. To know when to grow, and when to yield.”
“So,” Fu Ce says, the idea dawning on him, “true goodness is not a measure of what one gives, but of one’s alignment with the natural order of things?”
Fu Yuan nods.
“The essence of goodness is to allow all things their proper place. The truly good man does not cast his shadow over the world; he helps clear a space for all things to feel the sun.

Good and Evil are merely tools, born of the narrow lens of human perception. Behind them lies the cosmic dance of order and chaos, a dynamic tension playing out across eons. How can a mere mortal presume to discern its true nature?”

Liu Zijing’s lips tremble, his eyes locked on the “Good Tree.” As the light of the setting sun floods the space between heaven and earth, the great tree dissolves into a monstrous, looming shadow, like a deformed beast.
Fu Yuan’s voice seems to echo in the desolate air.
“Where, then, does goodness lie? Not in the world. Not in your heart.
Goodness... is the state of your being.”
Fu Ce, utterly lost, looks toward Liu Zijing. He has fallen to his knees. A gust of wind whips the collar of his fine robe up, hiding his face.
From Fu Ce’s vantage point, at that very moment, he cannot see the man’s expression.
Chapter 14: The Dream Dancer
Spring warms the air, and all life returns. The Great Khan, however, is struck by sickness and for ten days does not appear.
What sickness? Lovesickness.
The story goes that he has dreamt of an exotic dancer from the West. Some say she has the face of Chang’e, the moon fairy herself; others swear it is Guanyin, the Bodhisattva of Mercy, reincarnated.

The rumor is a fever. From the ministers in the court to the common folk, everyone gossips about her as if they have seen her with their own eyes, describing her beauty, her movements, and speculating on the more intimate details with a heat that makes ears burn.

They say the Khan grows thinner by the day, that he spends all his time staring blankly into the gardens, muttering, “This whole world belongs to this Emperor, but the one thing this Emperor wants most is nowhere in it.”

Some whisper it is a curse from a witch. Others say it is karmic retribution for all the blood he has spilled. Every hack poet in the land is writing verses for her. Every prostitute is claiming a resemblance.
Fu Yuan has also vanished. At last, the ministers of the court can bear the paralysis no longer. They find him on the stargazing tower, alone with the heavens.
“Grand Advisor,” they plead, “the Khan is drowning in his dream. The empire is helpless. Only you can break this spell.”
Fu Yuan doesn’t turn.
“Good for him.”
“Good for him?!” The ministers are aghast.
He finally turns to face them, his expression as distant as a star.

“The Khan has seen through life and death. He has conquered the entire world. There is nothing left on this earth that can truly move him. This dancer in his dream, this beautiful impossibility, has given him back the ache of desire. Is that not a blessing?”
The ministers stare, dumbfounded. At last, one of them stammers, “But it is just a dream. An illusion.”
Fu Yuan’s laugh is cold and sharp.
“His empire was also once nothing more than an idea in his head. Is it not now the projection of his will? In a hundred years, will it not also be just another dream?”
His gaze sweeps over them, and each man feels his face burn.
“You think the dream is the illusion and the empire is the reality. You fail to see that the empire is the true illusion. It belongs to no one. The dream, however, is real. It belongs only to him.”
The night deepens. Lanterns begin to bloom across the palace grounds.
The Great Khan sits in his garden, his face strangely calm. An attendant scurries to his side and whispers what he has heard of Fu Yuan’s words.
When the attendant finishes, the Khan lets out a long, satisfied sigh.
“Fu Yuan, O Fu Yuan... how truly lonely this world would be without you.”
The attendant stands silently. The lamplight flickers in the spring breeze, and the Khan’s silhouette seems to melt into the vast, dark night.
A moment later, another sigh drifts from the shadows — soft, distant, and dreamy.
Is it for the beautiful phantom in his dream? Or for the world’s many perfect possibilities?
Chapter 15: A Riverside Colloquy
From the Third Volume of the Itinerarium of Friar William of Rubruck. The original is housed in the Vatican Library, though several pages have suffered water damage.
In the autumn of the year of our Lord one thousand two hundred and
[illegible]
, in the Empire of the Great Yuan.

May the Lord forgive the inadequacies of my reason, and may He guide these pages, that they may serve as a faithful record of what was seen and thought this day. This journey has shaken me in a way I could not have foreseen, in a way that may yet alter my understanding of the world and of Truth itself.

After the autumn rains, the great river had swollen its banks, appearing to merge as one with the firmament above. From a mountaintop, the tolling bell of a Zen monastery reached us, its tone deep and resonant, like the very mystery of the Orient which I find so difficult to grasp — a mystery that fills me with equal measures of reverence and confusion.

I was granted an audience with Fu Yuan, a man held in awe even by the Great Khan. His frame is tall and imposing, more so than any bishop I have encountered in the northern lands. His eyes are so deep they seem to pierce the soul, yet his speech is entirely without artifice. Compared to the studied gravity of the prelates of our Church, his calm is a more formidable thing.
I found myself wondering: from what earth could such a man spring?

In the morning, we accompanied him on a visit to a Zen monastery. There, the monks were engaged in an activity they call chan-can, or meditative inquiry. I have transcribed some of their dialogues here, though I confess they are beyond my comprehension.

One monk asked, “What is the nature of Buddha?” Another replied, “The cypress tree in the courtyard.” Later, a second inquiry was met with an even more bizarre response: “A dry shit stick.” Such words are not merely absurd; they are so foul as to be unworthy of contemplation.

And yet, the monks who received these answers were overcome with joy, as if they had touched upon some profound and hidden wisdom. Permit me to state plainly: my reason and my learning can offer no explanation for this. The replies are manifest departures from logic, and having studied for many years at the University of Paris, I hold logic to be the universal law by which mankind understands all things.
It was as I was expressing my bewilderment to Fu Yuan that he suddenly posed a question to me.
“If one wishes to cross the river,” he asked, “how is it to be done?”
I was taken aback by the simplicity, but replied, “By boat, naturally.”
He smiled faintly, his gaze sweeping over the vast expanse of water.

“The study of Buddha is like crossing a river,” he said slowly. “A Zen koan is the boat. But if a man spends his days studying the shape of the boat and the grain of its wood, yet never sets foot in the water, how will he ever reach the other shore?”

This metaphor struck my mind like a bolt of lightning. I must confess, I could not help but admire the wisdom of this Eastern philosopher. His words immediately brought to mind the theological disputations in our own abbeys — how often have I seen bishops argue endlessly over a point of Latin grammar in the Holy Scriptures, while forgetting the very Faith to which the words are meant to point?
Fu Yuan then elaborated.

“The Zen koan was designed as a tool to lead the mind into a state of profound confusion. It uses bizarre answers to shatter the edifice of logic, forcing a man into a corner of his own mind. Then, a sharp cry or a blow awakens him, allowing him to break the limits of his own consciousness. The monks of today, however, have mistaken the tool for the destination. They have mistaken the boat for the other shore. They remain on the riverbank, and have never once set foot in the water.”
I was astonished. His words were a perfect echo of certain mystical scholars at Paris, who wrote of the distinction between the instrument and its purpose!
Perhaps inspired by his mode of thinking, I attempted to pose a circular query of my own.

“If a question is itself without meaning,” I asked, “is the discussion of that question also without meaning? And if a question does have meaning, does the discussion of it therefore also have meaning?”
Fu Yuan listened, a flicker of amusement in his eyes. He defeated my entire inquiry with a single, devastating counter-question.
“Does ‘discussion’ have meaning?”

The words struck my soul like a thunderclap. In that instant, I finally understood why the Zen monks could smile at “the cypress tree in the courtyard.” Fu Yuan’s question was itself a Zen master’s staff. It did not require an answer; its purpose was to shatter the obsession with the question itself.

I confess, I tried to analyze his question with the tools of logic, only to find it an infinite regression. If discussion requires meaning to justify it, does the discussion of meaning itself require meaning? And by what authority is that meaning to be established? My mind felt as though it had fallen into a bottomless abyss, descending ever deeper into uncertainty.
[Several pages here are heavily damaged by water, with only fragments remaining.]

...Fu Yuan spoke of an ancestor-master named Bodhidharma, who was said to have crossed the great river on a single reed. I wished to refute this with the laws of nature, but remembering his earlier question, I held my tongue. Perhaps, as Saint Thomas says, some truths are simply beyond the reach of logic.
[An entire page here is illegible.]

When I came to my senses, I was standing on the opposite bank of the river. The water still churned, but there was no boat in sight. Fu Yuan stood silently beside me, his gaze fixed on the far horizon.

My mind descends into chaos. When did I board the boat? And how did I cross? That boat... where did it go? Is it possible that my mind, so completely shattered by his words, simply ceased to record the world for a time?
Or perhaps... we never took a boat at all?
Or perhaps... we never even crossed the river?
...I dare not think further.
His composure was absolute. Mine was in ruins.

May the Lord forgive my doubt, but perhaps logic is not the only path to Truth. Since that day, Fu Yuan’s question — “Does discussion have meaning?” — has haunted my thoughts like a phantom, forcing me to reconsider: is the very meaning we spend our lives pursuing itself a form of obsession?
Translator’s Note:

The final pages of this account are severely damaged. Some scholars question the authenticity of this manuscript, as the events described are not recorded in any other historical source. However, judging by the unique shift in Friar William’s later writings on the relationship between logic and faith, it seems this journey to the East did indeed have a profound and lasting impact on his thought.
Chapter 16: The Khan and the Mirror
The Khan wants a biography written of him, and the whole world knows it.
Night settles over the city at West Lake. Lamps blaze along the shore, the vibrant hum of life washing away the old stench of gunpowder and blood.
From a magnificent pleasure-barge on the water, a roar shatters the night.
“What is this trash! Throw this fool in the lake with the rest of them!”
The court historian, his forehead bleeding from his frantic bows, cries, “Forgive me, Your Majesty! Forgive this unworthy subject!”
And with a splash, he throws himself into the water.
The Khan fumes, gesturing at the scrolls scattered across the deck. “This emperor builds a world, and these fools can’t even capture a single one of his whiskers!”
He turns. “Tell you what — why don’t you write it, my dear Grand Advisor? In this whole world, who knows me better than you?”
Fu Yuan says nothing. He simply draws a mirror, as if from thin air, from the folds of his robe.
“This comes from the Far West. A great rarity. I was thinking of giving it to you, my lord.”
The Khan takes the mirror. He is stunned. He turns it over and over, studying his reflection from every angle. Then he points it at Fu Yuan, at the guards, at the night itself, unable to put it down.
A faint smile plays on Fu Yuan’s lips. “Though this mirror can reflect everything, there is one thing it cannot show.”
The Khan is hooked. “And what exactly would that be?”
Fu Yuan points a single finger at the glass.
“Itself.”
The Khan goes still, a thought flickering in his eyes.
Fu Yuan continues. “You, Great Khan, are that mirror. You reflect the entire world. All things become a reflection of your will. All things... but you.”
The Khan is lost in thought. “You’re saying... it’s impossible for me to see my real self?”
Fu Yuan’s eyes seem to hold the darkness of the lake.

“Precisely. Anything that can be observed becomes an object. The true self is forever the subject, the one who observes. Like the mirror — its nature is to reflect all things. For that very reason, it can never reflect itself.
Any attempt to capture the self is a paradox. The consciousness that desires to see cannot become the object that is seen.”
A wave of understanding crosses the Khan’s face. He turns and looks out at the dark water. “The man in the mirror is not me, nor the man in the book.”
He whips his head back to face Fu Yuan, his gaze sharp enough to cut.
“Then where the hell is the real me?!”
Fu Yuan offers only a slight smile. “Why would you ask
me
, Your Majesty?”
A deep, rolling laugh bursts from the Khan’s chest. He shoves the mirror inside his tunic.
“Let them judge my life’s work a thousand years from now! Why the hell do I need anyone to understand me!”
He waves a dismissive hand. “Forget it! When this emperor is dead, they can write whatever fables they like. It’s not like I’ll be reading them!”
The night deepens. One by one, the lights on the shore go out.
The Khan looks up at the moon, and then, unable to resist, he pulls the mirror out again.
In the deep darkness of the barge, the reflection is a murky shadow.
He lifts his sleeve and wipes the surface once, then twice. It makes no difference. He still cannot see himself clearly.
He lets out a long, quiet sigh.
Chapter 17: The Monk Who Knew Everything

These days, a new monk is all anyone in the city talks about. From the nobles in their halls to the peddlers in the alleys, his name is on every tongue. To speak of the “Divine Monk” is to be in fashion.
The Khan is not pleased. “In all the world,” he thinks, “who has more wisdom than my Grand Advisor?”
So he arranges a public forum, a contest of wits. He commands both men to appear on the same stage. The monk, he vows, will be humiliated before the eyes of all.

On the day of the forum, a sudden downpour gives way to a vast rainbow that splits the sky. White clouds gather in the shape of lotus blossoms — a sign of immense fortune. Before the assembly even begins, the streets of the capital are empty. The city holds its breath.
Scraps of paper fly to the stage like snowflakes.
“An earthquake struck my homeland. Is this the weight of our collective karma?”
“My son cried like an ox at birth. Can he be the Bull Demon King reborn?”
“I burp when I chant. Is my karma too heavy?”
“My dog wags its tail at the Buddha statue in my home. Is it, too, on the path to enlightenment?”

The Divine Monk sees all and answers all. His words leave some breathless with joy, others trembling in fear. A rain of copper coins falls into the collection box. It fills, then overflows. The young acolyte managing the box grins, scratching his head in delight.
Fu Yuan, for his part, answers every question with only three words:
“I don’t know.”
A murmur ripples through the crowd, then turns to scorn. They shake their heads in disappointment.
The Khan’s face is a mask of cold iron.
Back in the palace, his fury erupts. He points a finger in Fu Yuan’s face, cursing.
Fu Yuan remains calm. He asks a quiet question.
“Would you wish for every citizen to be like me, Your Majesty?”

The Khan scoffs. “If everyone were like you, who would listen to a damn word I say?” He thinks for a moment, then adds, “Besides, if the whole world was filled with men like you, how the hell would I ever have become emperor?!”
“Then would you wish for me to become like that monk?”
The Khan’s rage returns. “If you became a charlatan like him, how could I ever show my face in public again—“
He stops. The words hang in the air. He understands.
CREAK
Fu Yuan pushes open the window. The night is deep, the sky a spill of countless stars.
“When was the last time you counted the stars?” Fu Yuan asks suddenly.
The Khan, still fuming, snorts. “I don’t waste my time on such nonsense!”
Fu Yuan smiles. “And if someone told you they could count them all, would you believe them?”
The Khan grunts. “Of course not.”
Fu Yuan turns from the window, and for a moment, the stars seem to flicker in his eyes.
“You must understand,” he says. “Every word that monk spoke today was not an answer. It was an explanation designed to fit a question into his system.”
The Khan frowns. “Isn’t an explanation the answer?”
Fu Yuan’s voice is low and steady.

“An answer can be an explanation, but an explanation is not always the truth. His so-called omniscience is nothing more than the internal logic of his own framework. He uses karma, reincarnation, and consequence to resolve every query, but he never once touches reality itself.”
Fu Yuan’s gaze is deep, his tone dropping lower.

“Any system of thought that claims to explain everything is really just hiding one thing: its refusal to admit the unknown exists. True wisdom isn’t forcing the world into a box. It’s knowing the box has limits. It’s not about dodging the unknown. It’s about facing it.”
The Khan seems to grasp something. “But the common folk need answers. They can’t bear the weight of not knowing.”
A cold smile touches Fu Yuan’s lips.

“Selling the unknown as an answer only breeds dependency. It traps people in a system, leading them further from the truth. The true path isn’t to erase the unknown. It’s to show people it is nothing to fear.”
“It is our boundary. And it is our beginning.”
Together, they look out into the night. The great river of the galaxy is a faint, shimmering veil. In the quiet, the Khan speaks, his voice like a man dreaming aloud.
“Fu Yuan... do you think when we die... we go back there?”
The stars flicker in Fu Yuan’s eyes. He is silent for a long time.
Then he says:
“I don’t know.”
Chapter 18: The Enlightened
The night is cool as water. Autumn dew clings to the gardens, edging every leaf and petal in diamonds.
Through this silvered world moves a prince. He is dressed in the simplest cloth, unadorned, but his eyes hold the lucid fire of a flawless pearl and the ancient depth of a starless sea.

It is these eyes that disarmed the Khan. Days ago, they had extinguished the gall on his tongue, dissolving his resentment into nothing. He felt a sudden, absurd urge to kneel. If the man before him were not his own son, with the whole court watching, the Khan would have shamed himself then and there.

This is the Eldest Prince, who left without a word ten years ago. Today, he is known across the world as the Great Enlightened One, the enlightened master who has realized the supreme Way of heaven and earth.
His followers number in the millions. If you follow him, they whisper in the streets, the sick will be healed, the dying will find peace, the troubled heart will be calmed.
In a few days, he will take the stage to offer his teachings to the world. The Khan has already decreed that his words will be compiled, printed, and distributed — a new scripture for the ages.
The Prince moves along a path through the trees, a route known only to him and to Fu Yuan. He finds Fu Yuan waiting by the lake’s edge, gazing at the moon trapped in the water.
A night breeze ripples the surface, shattering the reflection.
“You see?” Fu Yuan says, his voice soft, his back still turned. “Even the water knows not to cling to the moon.”
The Prince smiles at this and sits beside him.

“Perhaps,” he says, his voice calm and clear, his gaze on the fractured light, “it is the moon that is too high and mighty to be held by the water.” He looks at Fu Yuan. “I hear the Grand Advisor still refuses to take disciples. Many follow you, yet you treat them with indifference, even scorn. Is that not needlessly harsh?”
Fu Yuan scoffs.
“Have you ever seen eagles hunt in a pack?”

“And that reveals the deepest rift between us,” the Prince replies. His eyes burn like torches, casting their light on the Grand Advisor. But Fu Yuan’s eyes are the abyss itself; they swallow the light and reflect nothing.

“Should wisdom be a selfish thing, Grand Advisor? It is meant to be shared, as is joy. People begin from different places. We must offer them skillful means to guide them onto the path. Even a single step, even a fleeting glimpse of the truth, is better than being lost to the darkness.”
Fu Yuan offers no argument. Instead, a strange curiosity enters his voice.
“I am told you found your enlightenment at the edge of the ocean. Tell me, then: have you learned where the universe comes from? And where life is meant to go?”
The Prince pauses, then gives a soft laugh.
“Grand Advisor, surely you have not fallen to such a level. My awakening was into the nature of being itself, not the answers to such empty questions.”
“I am asking for
them
,” Fu Yuan says, his voice flat. “Are you prepared to give them their answers?”

“Not now,” the Prince admits with a wry smile. “But by guiding them to take that first step, I believe they will one day understand. Think of a man in the desert chasing a mirage. The vision is an illusion, yes. But in walking toward it, he may yet find a true oasis.”
Fu Yuan looks back at the lake. He picks up a stone and tosses it in. The moon shatters once more. He points.
“I see what happens after you die.”
A visible tremor runs through the Prince.

“Your disciples will write down all you have said. Then they will add their own words and declare the whole of it to be unassailable truth. They will twist your inner awakening into a claim of outer omniscience. Anything you did not explicitly deny, they will say you endorsed.”
His voice is as cold as the lake water.

“They will build sects. They will raise temples. They will carve your likeness in gold for the fearful and the foolish to worship. Your Way, which you intend as a means to liberation, will become a cage for the mind.
You wish to teach them to find freedom within. They will only learn to seek dependence without. That very first step you speak of — it already leads in the wrong direction.”
The fire in the Prince’s gaze gutters, as if the night wind has blown through his very soul. He stares for a long time at the moon, slowly re-forming in the dark water.
“I understand,” he says, his voice a whisper. “I believed I was weaving a gentle path to freedom. Instead, I was weaving a new illusion. Such compassion... is it not just another form of attachment?”
The next morning, the Prince gathers all his followers. Facing the sea of expectant faces, he speaks, his voice a calm and resonant bell.
“I have called you here to tell you a truth. It may not be the truth you wish to hear.
You have come to me believing I hold a key that will save you. In this, you are in error. No one, however wise, can walk the path for another.
Every doctrine, every ritual, every icon you cherish is a shadow. It is a phantom built of your own fear, your own longing.
I have never claimed to be a god. I am not a savior. I am a man who has looked clearly into himself. The answer you are searching for is not mine to give. It is your own awakening.
There is no road that can be paved for you. There is no salvation that can be bestowed upon you. Freedom can only come from
within
, and it can only be found by
you

From this day, there will no longer be a sect, nor any so-called doctrine. I cannot lead you down that formless path, for each of you must cut it through your own heart. All that I can tell you is this: to find the truth, you must first let go of every fantasy you hold about what it is.”
The crowd erupts. Some weep in despair. Some shout in rage. Most are simply stunned into silence.

The Prince turns and walks away. At first, tens of thousands follow, certain this is but another test. But the road stretches on, and the crowd thins. In the end, only a dozen remain, walking behind him in silence, their questions finally exhausted.
Days later, the Prince stands amidst the clouds on a mountain peak. He looks down at the capital city, laid out like a game board. White clouds drift and coil below, obscuring half the view.
“Great Teacher,” one of his last companions asks, “what is it that you see?”
The Prince raises a hand. “Do not call me Great Teacher.”
He pauses, then says quietly:
“My Teacher is beneath those clouds.”
Chapter 19: The Bonfire
“Faith? What do you think Faith is? Light?”
The question hangs in the air.
A moth dives for the bonfire. It vanishes in a crackle, tainting the clean grassland air with the smell of char.
Fu Yuan points a single finger.
“No. It’s fire. It threatens with darkness, lures with light, rewards with warmth. And in the end, it takes your life.”

Confusion is written all over the face of the disciple beside him. “But... is not Faith worthy of our respect? This disciple always believed that the pilgrim Xuanzang’s journey to the West was celebrated by the world for precisely this reason — for his unwavering commitment, for his readiness to sacrifice everything...”
“Oh?” Fu Yuan cuts in. “And is that what your faith is for? The world’s admiration?”
The disciple shakes his head, emphatic. “No! Of course not! It is for the pursuit of Truth! Of Freedom!”
A cold smile touches Fu Yuan’s lips.
“Then why do you speak of commitment and sacrifice, but say nothing of the Truth he supposedly found?”
The disciple has no answer.
Fu Yuan stares into the flames, his voice dripping with scorn.

“Men will tell you they’ll die for their faith, but they never bother to ask what it is. The world praises the pilgrim’s long journey, but no one bothers to read the scrolls he brought back. What they chase was never the light. It was always the obsession in their own hearts.”
He leans in slightly.
“So what truly moves you? Truth and wisdom? Or the brutal theater of sacrifice?”
The disciple freezes, his brow locking in thought.
Fu Yuan’s voice sends a chill through the air, despite the fire.

“This is faith’s great trick: it makes the meek feel noble, the ignorant feel wise, and the coward feel brave. It turns all unexplained pain into the easy comfort of acceptance. You are nothing but sacrifices to your own petty desires.”
Another moth flutters toward the fire. Fu Yuan bats it from the air, his eyes blazing.

“True truth needs no sacrifice. It isn’t made more real by your passion. Any belief that demands you surrender your reason, your doubt — it is nothing but the bioluminescent lure of an anglerfish, drawing you into the jaws of the abyss.”
He gestures to the moths still dancing near their doom, a flicker of pity in his tone.

“They dive into the fire because they can’t tell light from flame. Is the human obsession with faith any different? You can’t distinguish the impulse to sacrifice yourself from a genuine thirst for truth.”
A deep, long sigh escapes him.

“The greatest lie ever told is that sacrifice is enlightenment. That to throw yourself into the fire is to touch the light. It has been this way for millennia, and it will be for millennia to come. Nothing will change. Someone will always be there to exploit this weakness in men, to weave a new faith, to unleash endless suffering upon the world.”
WOOSH.
With a flick of his sleeve, the bonfire is gone.
The disciple cries out. “Master—!”
A moment passes. His eyes slowly adjust to the profound darkness. He can just make out Fu Yuan’s silhouette, head tilted toward the sky.
Without the bonfire’s glare, a revelation — an immense, silent ocean. The great river of stars is a breathtaking smear of light, sharp and clear. A meteor dies in a silver streak.
Fu Yuan’s voice rises from the dark, as if from time itself.
“If you do not extinguish the Bonfire of Faith, how will you ever see a Sky of Truth like this?”
Chapter 20: The Great Khan’s Games

As the sun sets, Fu Ce finally finds him, in the heart of the grasslands. While the entire world is looking for the Grand Advisor, the man himself is here, feasting with herdsmen around a bonfire, tearing at roasted meat with his hands and drinking wine from coarse bowls.

The Great Khan’s birthday approaches. The idea was proposed: a grand athletic games, to which all nations would be invited. It would be an event without precedent, a gathering of nations to pay tribute. A showcase of national power, a boon for diplomacy, a surge of pride for the people, and a grand step toward the unity of all mankind.
But who could design and orchestrate such a thing? All eyes turned to the Grand Advisor. And then he vanished.
The Khan was furious. “That son of a bitch! I know he vanished on purpose!”

Fu Yuan is shirtless before a yurt, his skin a map of scars, like chisel marks on stone. The man is a living statue, rough-hewn and classical. He is grappling with a group of burly herdsmen. A roar goes up from the circle of onlookers; the air is electric.

By the time Fu Ce pushes through the crowd, Fu Yuan has already thrown the last man to the ground. After embracing his defeated opponents, he walks straight toward Fu Ce, his body slick with sweat and gleaming in the firelight. The herdsmen fall to their knees as if before a god.
Fu Ce drops to the ground as well. “Grand Advisor! The Khan commands your return!”
Fu Yuan laughs. “Then let him come find me himself! He wants his games, does he not?”
He points a thumb back toward the patch of trampled grass beside the fire. “If the Khan can best me on this wrestling ground, I’ll go back with him.”
Fu Ce does not know how to respond. He simply touches his forehead to the ground.
Fu Yuan pulls him up, and the two sit on the ground before the yurt.
“Ce’er,” Fu Yuan asks, “why are men so obsessed with competition?”
Fu Ce looks confused. “This nephew only knows that when I watch, my blood runs hot. The winner is like the proudest hawk, admired by all.”
He pauses, then adds, “Just now, when you were wrestling... you were like the Blue Wolf of the legends, stepping out of the dawn. You should have seen the fire in the women’s eyes!”

Fu Yuan nods. “Exactly. This fever for competition is a primal instinct. It drives evolution, survival of the fittest. But if a man is no different from an animal, what makes him a man? If humanity cannot transcend the beast within, how can it ever reach the next stage of its evolution?”
A young herdswoman brings a robe for Fu Yuan. Fu Ce watches her walk away, and swallows. He looks back to see a mocking smile on Fu Yuan’s face and feels a flush of heat in his own.
“This nephew has been with you for years, but I admit the beast is still in me. For all of mankind to reach your level... won’t that take ten thousand years?”
Fu Yuan roars with laughter. “Why would I need everyone to be like me? Tell me, your little bit of beast — what is that compared to humanity’s greatest folly?”
“The greatest folly?” Fu Ce asks.

“It is the madness of melting into the crowd,” Fu Yuan says. “Of erasing one’s own insignificance by becoming part of a collective ‘greatness.’ The kind of games the Khan wants is nothing but a carnival of collectivism. Every spectator is a vulture, waiting to feast on the kill.”
Recognition dawns on Fu Ce’s face. “I understand. You are the hawk of the grasslands. You would never fight with vultures over a carcass.”
Fu Yuan’s voice grows deeper.

“Humanity’s true evolution isn’t found in winning or losing. It is found in letting go of the obsession with the outcome. It is to stop measuring the self by external standards, and instead understand the meaning of life through an inner awakening.”
“Win or lose,” he says, gesturing to the plains, “it’s like the grass on this steppe. Some blades are longer, some are shorter. What does it matter?”
Seeing the unspoken question in his nephew’s eyes, Fu Yuan smiles.
“Go back and tell the Khan this. If he can agree to two of my conditions, I will return and help him.”
Fu Ce is alarmed. “Uncle, you don’t actually mean to wrestle the Khan?!”
Fu Yuan laughs.
“First: The trophy is for the loser. The winner already has his victory. What other prize does he need? Second: The host nation is forbidden to compete. How else can you prove your fairness?

When winner and loser can each find their proper place, when we no longer define heroes by victory or defeat — that is true education for the people. When a great nation does not act like one, when it does not use its strength to bully the weak — that is a sign of true power and grace.
That
is something the people can be proud of.”
“If the Khan can agree to these two conditions,” he declares, “then I will return and give him an event the likes of which the world has never seen!”
A red sun is rising at the edge of the world, leaving half the grassland still draped in shadow. Fu Ce looks back. Fu Yuan is a lone figure on a ridge, a mere silhouette against the dawn.
Fu Ce sighs.
When I see His Majesty,
he thinks,
I’ll just tell him I scoured the grasslands and heard only legends of the Grand Advisor, but never saw the man himself.
Chapter 21: Half-Day Magistrate

Fu Yuan arrives in a small town to visit an old friend, an official sent here in disgrace. He doesn’t expect, however, to be immediately pressed into service as the magistrate for the afternoon, his friend having conveniently vanished to enjoy himself.
In the public court, a husband and wife are bickering nonstop. The bailiff’s shouts for “Order!” are all but ignored.

The wife is a popular prostitute from the local brothel. She quit the trade for love. But once they were married, her husband stopped talking of poetry and passion, and instead went on about the cosmos and the Great Way day and night. She thought she was marrying a poet; she ended up with a monk.
The husband feels deeply wronged.

“Your Honor, this humble student has been a seeker of the Way of Heaven since he was a boy! This student chose to be a vegetarian at four! Back when we were... acquainted, we would spend entire nights in the brothel studying the ancient wisdoms of the
Tao Te Ching
. Why is this suddenly an issue?”
Fu Yuan’s patience is wearing thin.
“Fine. You, go be a monk. And you, go back to the brothel.”
The court stares, mouths agape. The couple cries out in horror, “Your Excellency, you mustn’t!”
“So this is no good, and that is no good?” Fu Yuan growls. “I’m the Grand Advisor to the Emperor, am I not? Is my time worth so little to you?”
They immediately begin knocking their heads on the floor. “Grand Advisor, forgive us! Grand Advisor, spare our lives!”
Fu Yuan muses for a moment.
“Alright, how about this? The two of you are going to swap roles. A little mutual education.”
The court members stare at each other, trying to parse the Grand Advisor’s profound and subtle meaning. The husband stammers, “This humble student... is too dull-witted...”
Fu Yuan scoffs.

“You prattle on about the Great Way every day, yet you’re this dense? Here is your sentence: For one month, I forbid you from discussing scripture. I forbid you from debating philosophy. You are to speak only of romance and the matters of the heart!”
The husband looks horrified. The wife, on the other hand, beams, and sneaks another glance at Fu Yuan.

“And you, woman,” Fu Yuan barks, pointing at her. “For one month, I forbid you from filling your head with sentimental nonsense. I forbid you from a single flirtatious remark. You will discuss only philosophy and the nature of the Great Way!”

The wife gives the Grand Advisor a sideways look. “What this old girl has on her mind, how are you supposed to know?” she thinks. “This so-called Grand Advisor is big and handsome, but not the sharpest tool. Back when my man was just a client, we ‘discussed’ in bed about ‘being like water’ and how ‘the unspoken way is the true way’ all the time.”

The husband, however, is deeply concerned. “But Excellency, this humble student’s pursuit is the Way of Heaven itself. To swap that for... for her... pursuit of worldly affection... the two are hardly equal in stature.”
Fu Yuan glares.
“She chases love, you chase the Way. Chasing is chasing. Six of one, half a dozen of the other. What’s the damn difference? I didn’t tell you to go work in a brothel, did I?”
He slams his hand on the table.
“Dismissed!”

Later, walking through a garden, a disciple asks, “Master, how can you say the pursuit of love and the pursuit of the Way are equal? The sages teach us to renounce the world to find enlightenment. The Buddha himself gave up everything...”
Fu Yuan cuts him off.
“What is Truth?”
The disciple pauses, then gives the standard reply. “Truth is ultimate reality. The unchanging essence of the cosmos.”
Fu Yuan’s voice is relentless.
“And if there is no ‘cosmos’? No ‘reality’? No ‘unchanging essence’? What then?”
“But Master, that is a matter of definition,” the disciple argues. “Truth doesn’t need defining. It simply is. It is the most real thing there is, is it not?”
A cold smile from Fu Yuan.
“Is it? You ‘pursue’ a thing called Truth because someone put the idea in your head that such a thing exists. Where did that idea come from? Wasn’t it
given
to you?”
The disciple stands, speechless.
Fu Yuan claps a hand on his shoulder.

“We can’t even answer if ‘Truth’ is real. So how is one man’s chase for it superior to a woman’s chase for love? It’s the oldest vanity. The man of morals feels more moral. The man of ideology feels more noble. The man of faith feels more blessed. And the man who chases Truth feels a cut above them all.”
He reaches out, plucks a flower, and holds it out.
“Love is this flower. It will wither, but you can touch it.”
He points to the moon.
“Truth is that moon. It seems eternal, but it could just be a reflection on water.”
“Which is more real?” he asks. “Which is more noble?”
The disciple feels the world shift under his feet, suddenly unsure of where to step. When he gathers himself, Fu Yuan has already rounded a hedge of flowers and vanished into the night.
The disciple calls into the darkness. “Master! Then what should I pursue?”
The night wind returns, carrying the scent of blossoms and Fu Yuan’s voice.
“The flower is already in your hand. What is there left to pursue?”
Chapter 22: Behind the Idol
The sandstorm passes. A temple from a forgotten aeon surfaces from the dunes.
Inside its main hall, the great statue of a goddess stands immense, one hand holding a sword, the other a set of scales. Her expression is solemn, severe.
“This is her,” a local official declares. “The legendary Goddess of Justice! She will watch over the dynasty’s laws and guard the world’s fairness. The Khan will send envoys to make offerings!”
