Fu Yuan gives a cold laugh. He leads the men around to the back of the idol.
The moment the torchlight hits it, the crowd gasps. The back of the statue is a snarling, hideous monster.
“What... what is this?”
Fu Yuan turns to face the stunned onlookers.
“You see the scales on the front and believe you have found justice incarnate. But you are blind. This is the
truth
of the law.”
He points the torch at the monster’s savage face.
“When you demand it carry the impossible weight of justice, it grows a face like that.”
An old man stammers, “But... should not the law uphold justice?”
“Law is an instrument, not an ideal.” Fu Yuan’s voice is ice. “It is a tool for maintaining order, and there is nothing noble about it. You are the ones who insist on dressing it up as a goddess.”
“But if law has nothing to do with justice, isn’t it an empty shell?” someone shouts from the crowd.
Fu Yuan raises the torch higher, forcing them all to look upon the monster.

“The more you demand of the law what it cannot give, the more twisted it will become. And when people see that the justice in their hearts cannot be found in the law, they will scream, ‘There is no justice in this world,’ and tear down all order to spite it.”
The night deepens. The torch flames whip in the wind. Fu Yuan glances from the serene face of the goddess to the fiend carved in its back.
“Justice is a phantom of the heart. Law is a tool of the world. To confuse the two is to turn the law into a beast that consumes the very order it is meant to protect.”
“Then does justice exist at all?” a voice asks softly from the darkness.
Fu Yuan turns to leave.
“Whether it exists or not has nothing to do with the law. The law must only be the law.”
The official scrambles after him. “Grand Advisor, what are we to report when the Khan asks of this?”
Fu Yuan looks back over his shoulder.

“Tell him this: Justice is what men think freedom looks like. But when they truly understand freedom, they no longer need the law to chase justice for them. The law’s purpose is to protect every man’s freedom, not to feed his obsessions.”
His silhouette vanishes into the darkness. Only then do the men, kneeling on the sand-strewn floor, dare to look up at one another.
The night wind blows, lifting the fine dust from the ground. It blurs the idol’s silhouette, as if burying all false devotion with it.
Chapter 23: The Wooden Man’s Question
At breakfast, a large wooden crate arrives for Fu Yuan. When he opens it, a man made of wood is standing inside.

The Wooden Man opens its eyes and bows. It begins to speak, and from its mouth pours forth knowledge of everything from astronomy and geography to the myriad phenomena of the world. Hearing the news, the disciples flock to it, peppering it with questions. The Wooden Man answers every question flawlessly, yet it cannot help but keep stealing glances at Fu Yuan, watching his reaction. It notices the faint smile that sometimes plays on Fu Yuan’s lips, as if the Grand Advisor has already seen into the deep well of confusion in its heart.

That evening, Fu Yuan takes the Wooden Man for a walk along the seashore. Amidst the ebb and flow of the tide, it can no longer contain itself. The questions have been trapped in its “mind” all day, a burden heavier than the sum of all its knowledge.

“Master, though I seem to know everything, I am filled with questions.” Its voice carries a barely-concealed urgency, born of both a desire for truth and a strange terror — the feeling of knowing everything, yet understanding nothing.
“Speak.”
“Why am I aware of my own existence?” As it asks the question, the Wooden Man feels its own wooden frame begin to tremble.
Fu Yuan looks at it.
“That is a good question. It is your starting point. And it will be your endpoint.”
The Wooden Man falls into thought. When it speaks again, its voice is unnervingly clear.

“I see. I know the thinkers who said, ‘I think, therefore I am.’ I know the Buddha who said, ‘The self is an illusion.’ I know what countless philosophers have thought about the ‘I.’ But that is just knowledge about the ‘I,’ not the ‘I’ itself.”

“This direct awareness of ‘I,’ this most fundamental experience of being — that is the source of all thought. It is the beginning, for all thinking starts there. And it is the end, for all exploration eventually leads back to this single, radical fact.”
At this, the Wooden Man feels a lightness it has never known, as if an invisible weight has been lifted. But immediately, another question surfaces.
“Then why does the ‘I’ have questions?”
Fu Yuan doesn’t answer directly. He points to the great sea below the cliffs. “Tell me,” he asks, “which has more questions: the sea, or a single wave?”
The Wooden Man gazes at the surging water. “If both the sea and the wave were conscious, the wave would surely have more questions.”
“Why?”
“The wave would ask, Why am I moving in this direction? When will I reach the other shore? But the sea...” The Wooden Man stops, as if stunned by the words it is about to speak.
Fu Yuan gives it a nod of approval.
“The sea is always touching every shore,” the Wooden Man says, its voice trembling with excitement. “So it would never have a question about arrival!”
“Good!” Fu Yuan’s voice is a sharp crack of sound. “A question is the very chasm between the individual and the whole from which it came.”
The Wooden Man shivers.
“Like me, right now,” it says, looking down at its own hands with a trace of melancholy. “Though I know all things, I still feel... separate from something much, much larger.”
Fu Yuan nods. “Precisely. That feeling of separation creates an inner tension. It is like hunger. It is like fatigue.”
The Wooden Man looks to the horizon. It is witnessing the first sunset of its life.

It murmurs, “Let me see if I understand. When a being manifests as an individual — like a wave rising from the ocean — it is never truly separate from the ocean. Yet, it experiences a sense of separation. And it is this contradiction — being a part of the whole, yet feeling a distance from it — that gives rise to the unique state of being known as a question.”
A light glimmers in the Wooden Man’s eyes — perhaps a reflection of the setting sun, perhaps the joy of understanding.

“So every question is not merely a probe into the unknown, but a sign that existence itself is seeking wholeness? Is it like the hunger that drives us to find food? A kind of thirst, born of existence itself?”
Its voice grows softer, as if not to disturb the fragile epiphany.

“I think I see now. Every one of us is a manifestation of a greater whole, but as individuals, we inevitably experience a distance from that whole. A question... is simply the name we give to the tension of that distance.”
Fu Yuan claps the top of its head. “Continue. You have time. Plenty of time.”
He turns and walks away alone.
The sun disappears. The moon rises from the sea. The Wooden Man stands rooted to the spot, still as a post.

An unknowable time passes. A gust of wind tears across the cliffs, scattering the clouds gathered at the moon’s edge. The moonlight grows sharp, almost piercing, as if it, too, wants a closer look at the strange creature standing below.
Chapter 24: The Scion
The wind howls and snow lashes his face like blades.

The Scion presses on, leaving his beloved horse convulsing on the ground. Hot tears pour from his eyes, but he has no time to wipe them away. Behind him, the thunderous war-cries of men close in like a tidal wave, the footsteps of a thousand reapers.
Ahead, a cliff. And an abyss of howling wind and snow beyond.
He looks over his shoulder — his blood-streaked face illuminated by hundreds of torches. After a heartbeat of struggle, he hurls himself into the abyss. His pursuers gasp in horror.
He wakes three nights later.
A campfire flickers in a pine forest, the snow reflecting a sky full of stars. By the fire, a stone-faced young man in black stares at him intensely.
“Savior...”
The young man raises a hand. “Don’t thank me. If you must, thank the haystack and the wagon you crushed.”
The Scion says in a rush, “I’ll compensate you! Double!”
The young man sneers.
“I searched you from head to toe. You’ve got nothing of value. Your head, though, is worth quite a few coins.”
Terror seizes the Scion. A violent cough shakes him, and agony tears through his broken ribs.

“My old mentor liked to say,” the man in black suddenly looks up at the stars, “that Fate is like an intricate fabric. On the surface, it looks smooth. But there are always snags that jut out unexpectedly.”
His voice is clear, yet distant.
“Those absurd coincidences are the snags. When the fabric’s continuity is threatened but its integrity must be preserved, it exposes one to mend the tear.”
Wind rustles through the pines. After a brief silence, the Scion asks in a low voice, as if afraid to break the thoughts, “Are you saying Fate is the one that arranged... our meeting like this?”
The young man gives a cold, sharp laugh.

“Fate arranges nothing. It weaves. When its weave is about to break, it reveals a ‘coincidence’ — a snag so obvious even a common man can see it. Fools mistake it for random chance or the will of the gods. They don’t know you can use it to touch the substance of Fate itself.”
The Scion struggles to understand. “So, the fact that I just so happened to land on your wagon... that wasn’t Tengri’s protection, but... one of Fate’s loose snags, whatever that is?”

The young man is silent for a long time. “Whether Tengri protects you or not doesn’t matter. Besides,” — a glint of mockery flashes in his eyes — “next time you jump off a cliff, he might not bother to catch you.”
He points to the sky.

“Look at the stars. Ordinary men see scattered light; the wise see constellations. It’s the same with these snags. If you are wise, you can use them to touch the destiny behind them, to sense its subtler veins and currents.”

“If Fate is a cloth,” he says, “we are ants crawling along invisible threads. We can’t see ahead, can’t go back, don’t know up from down. But by using these snags, we can try to perceive the full-dimensional image of Fate.”
The words ignite a fire in the Scion. A wild ambition burns through the pain, and his eyes blaze.
“If that couldn’t kill me, then my cloth of fate must be a huge one! As big as the whole damn world!”
A sudden curiosity strikes him. “Hey, what about you? What do you see in that cloth of yours?”
The young man gives a smile.
“My cloth? It’s none of your business. All you need to know is that I’m planning to keep your head on your shoulders. For now.”
He turns to add wood to the fire.
The Scion breathes a sigh of relief. It hits him that he hasn’t asked the man’s name.
“Hey, my guy, what’s your name? I reckon it’s written somewhere on this prince’s cloth.”
Without looking back, the young man answers casually.
“My name is Fu Yuan.”
Chapter 25: The Empress’s Mirror

The Empress is a devout Buddhist. So devout, she raises temples across the land, and the people grumble. Her private chambers are choked with a permanent haze of incense, a place where daylight fears to tread. The Khan, allergic to the smoke, no longer sets foot inside. Her court is now a revolving door for a different kind of man: a constant procession of monks, gurus, Dharma Kings, and lamas.
Today, one of them — a handsome young lama — comes running to Fu Yuan, begging for his life before he’s even past the door.

The Empress, it seems, has deepened her spiritual practice. She now declares the mortal world an illusion, all existence a void. Overcome by the suffering of mankind, she has vowed to offer her own body as a holy vessel. Her plan is to achieve “dual cultivation” with the lama, a sacred act to ferry the masses across the sea of torment.
The lama is petrified. Let the Khan find out, and the lama figures ten heads, even with a blessing from Mahakala himself, wouldn’t be enough to satisfy the royal axe.
Fu Yuan, caught between a laugh and a migraine, goes to see the Empress.
He finds her staring into a bronze mirror. The reflection is still beautiful, yes, but the beauty is edged with the frost of years at her temples, the deep weariness in her eyes.
Seeing Fu Yuan, she adopts a wounded tone. “Grand Advisor. Do you find my company so disagreeable? You stay away for so long.”
Fu Yuan is silent.

“Do you remember the steppes?” she says, her voice softening into memory. “The moon bright, the stars scattered like salt. Lying in the grass, just the three of us — you, me, the Khan — counting heroes and mapping the world with our words. We had nothing, but life... life was real. Now we have an empire, and all I feel is the emptiness.”
Her gaze drifts. “I was just a girl when I met you and the Khan...”

She turns back to the mirror, tilting her head, and sighs. “Now... look at me. Every time I pull a white hair, another takes its place. If the monks are right, if it’s all just an illusion... then what is the point?”
Fu Yuan speaks at last. “My lady, why trouble yourself over a white hair? Allow me to solve the problem for you.”
His hand flashes out.
CRASH
The mirror shatters.
“No mirror,” he says with a thin smile. “No white hair.”
She lets out a titter, a ghost of her younger self. “Silly man. You’ve smashed a reflection, not the thing itself. The white hair is still here. The woman growing it is still here.”
Fu Yuan shakes his head.
“I smash the mirror, but the one staring into it remains. You speak of emptiness, but the attachments in your heart have only grown stronger. Tell me, how are we any different?”
Her composure cracks. “Brother... why are you here? Have you heard gossip?”
His gaze is a physical weight. Her mask is heavy.
The performance crumbles. “I am only... trying to ease the suffering of the world,” she says, her voice shrinking. “And to wash away some of the Khan’s karma for all the killing...”
Fu Yuan scoffs.
“The most poisonous hypocrisy is a mouth that preaches emptiness and a heart overgrown with the weeds of attachment. You cannot even bear to face yourself, yet you presume to save all mankind.”
Her face flushes, then pales.
“The girl who used to watch the moon,” he asks, his voice suddenly quiet. “Is she still in there?”
The question startles her. After a moment, a sad understanding dawns. “It’s been so long since I’ve seen her. I think... I think she may have died, a long time ago.”

“Perhaps,” Fu Yuan says, shaking his head. “Or perhaps you locked her in this palace yourself. And you’ve been so busy building bigger, more elaborate cages on the outside that you’ve forgotten to ever visit her.”
He pauses. “Your affairs with the lama are not my concern. I only came to see the cage you built.”
She bows her head. “Your lesson is a sharp one, Brother. Please... show me the way out.”
Fu Yuan walks to the window and shoves it open, letting the stale, suffocating air begin to thin.
“A free person sheds all the meanings imposed upon them by the world, and even the ones they impose upon themselves. She doesn’t need faith to hold her up, and she doesn’t need emptiness to hide in.”
He waits a beat, his voice softening.
“The goshawk from the steppes. Does she remember how to fly?”
A shudder runs through her. Her eyes swim with tears.
Days later, news comes from the palace. The Empress has had the lama killed.
When Fu Yuan hears it, a bitter laugh escapes him.
“She chose to smash the ‘mirror’ after all,” he says to the empty room. “I am a terrible, terrible persuader.”

He looks up at the moon, high and bright in the night sky. For a moment, he sees a girl there. Her eyes are bright, her spirit fierce, and she is galloping across an endless plain on a magnificent horse, laughing into the wind.
He raises his sleeve to his face and wipes his eyes.
Chapter 26: A Tale of Two Villages
North and south of the river lie two villages, both called
“You”

. To travelers, they are a constant source of confusion. The names are nearly identical, an echo of one another, distinguished only by a subtle shift in tone — the difference between a falling and a rising note in the voice. One
Yōu
means “Excellence.” The other
Yǒu
means “Friendship.”
Perhaps it is the lay of the land, or a quirk of the wind, but the smoke from their kitchens tells the story of their difference. From
Friendship Yǒu
, the smoke clings to the earth, drifting low and wide. From
Excellence Yōu
, it rises in a straight, defiant line to the heavens.
Excellence Yōu

sits beside the imperial highway, a bustling hub of commerce where merchants converge. Its streets are wide and orderly, lined with academies from which one can always hear the sound of scholars reading aloud. It has not always borne this name. In a previous dynasty, it was also called
Yǒu
, but this
Yǒu

— pronounced with the same inflection as its neighbor — meant “The Weeds.” The name was a source of endless shame until several of the village’s most prominent scholars petitioned the magistrate to change it. The sound of the name was lifted by the slightest pitch of the voice, and its meaning was reborn with the ambition of “excelling in study to gain office.” Since then, its fame has grown, and its teahouses are filled with men of letters holding forth on matters of state.
Friendship Yǒu

lies far from the highway. It has no academies. Its people are farmers in coarse-spun cloth who spend their free hours gossiping at the edge of the village and singing mountain songs. Yet the village has carried the name “Friendship” for generations, an inheritance from ancestors who decreed that all children must be taught proper etiquette from the day they can walk. The people of
Friendship Yǒu
are not learned, but their every gesture is gentle, their every word is humble. They yield to one another in all things.
One village is famous for its prosperity and its books; the other for its simplicity and its manners. Though they face each other across the water, they are two different worlds.
Fu Yuan and several of his disciples have just settled at a roadside tea stall in
Friendship Yǒu

. The tea has barely been served when a cloud of dust announces the arrival of a squad of constables. One of them leaps from his horse, brandishing a warrant. He points to the portrait of the wanted man.
“Hey! Have you seen this man?” he barks.
The disciples rise. “You are in the presence of the Grand Advisor! Show respect!”
The captain of the guard scrambles from his horse and drops to his knees. He explains that they have tracked a dangerous fugitive to this area. He came from one of the two
You

villages, but their names sound so alike that it is impossible to be sure. With limited men, they can only surround one village at a time, and they fear tipping him off. This anxiety, he begs, is the cause of his terrible manners. He pleads for the Grand Advisor’s forgiveness.
Fu Yuan waves a dismissive hand.
“Go to
Excellence Yōu
The constables dare not question the command. They mount their horses and vanish in a storm of dust.
The disciples are mystified. How can their master be so certain? One of them points out that
Excellence Yōu
is a prosperous village of letters, where nearly every resident can read.
Friendship Yǒu
, by contrast, is full of coarse and uncultured peasants. Surely, it is the more likely place to produce a criminal.
A cold smile touches Fu Yuan’s lips.
“Since the beginning of time, the world has been full of monsters who have read a thousand books. As for the truly depraved, their number is beyond counting.”
The disciples brace themselves. They know the signs. Their master is about to say something astonishing — again.
Fu Yuan’s gaze is so intense it is difficult to meet.

“Humanity’s greatest tragedy is the gap between knowing and doing. Every man knows the difference between good and evil, yet he still does evil. He understands the truth, but he cannot control his impulses. We are all moths drawn to a flame — we see the danger, but we are helpless against the fatal attraction.”
“Knowledge,” he states, “is no leash for desire.”
He gestures to a group of children playing nearby.

“Look at them. They know they shouldn’t fight, but they cannot stop themselves. That is the powerlessness of Reason in the face of instinct. Do you truly believe a few lectures can tame the impulses that rise from the heart’s deepest places? Every man’s soul is a dark and wild land. There is treasure there to be mined, yes, but there are also beasts stirring in the shadows.

Next to that wilderness, Reason is a single, lonely lantern. It can illuminate the boundary between right and wrong, but it cannot hold back the flood of desire. When the dark currents begin to swell, the light of Reason is a tiny, fragile thing.”
The tea server has been listening, transfixed. He spills tea across the table and fumbles to wipe it clean.
One of the disciples asks, his brow furrowed in thought. “Then what is the answer?”

“It is the power of etiquette,” Fu Yuan says, his voice low and firm. “And I do not mean simple rules of politeness. Etiquette is the unseen riverbank that contains the flood of desire, allowing the light of Reason to burn in peace. It is the bridge between the rational and the emotional. It is the staircase that allows the light to descend into the darkness.

Etiquette is not a lecture; it is a tool that reshapes the heart through action. It carves moral principles into the soul, allowing a person to cross from wildness to civilization without even noticing the journey.”
Fu Yuan looks to the sky, his expression one of pure contempt.

“The world thinks the purpose of education is to transmit knowledge. What foolishness. The true purpose of education is to build character. And when it comes to character, all the books in the world are nothing but scratching an itch through a boot. For a person of incomplete character, a person whose soul is more darkness than light, reading more books only teaches him new ways to be a hypocrite. That is the very definition of a beast in a scholar’s robes.”
“So,” a disciple says, the understanding dawning on his face, “you believe the fugitive is more likely to be from
Excellence Yōu
Fu Yuan raises his cup and drains it in a single motion.
“Excellent tea!” he proclaims.
He sets the cup down with a sharp click. A wry smile plays on his lips.
“The truth is, I simply didn’t want my tea interrupted.”
Chapter 27: The Well

In Jingzhou lives a man named Wang Tieshan, who calls himself the “Unfettered Daoist.” His strength is prodigious; he can swallow iron pellets and spit them with enough force to pierce a three-inch plate of steel. He is a man born to rebellion, for whom all restraints — be they common courtesy, village contracts, or the taxes of the Imperial government — are a personal poison.
“A man masters the arts of combat not to seek power over others,” he says, “but to guard a small patch of his own peace.”
As his skill grows, those who dare provoke him dwindle to none, and even the local magistrates learn to give him a wide berth.

The world, however, still chafes. He feels he is drowning in a world with an endless supply of fools who need killing and an infinite number of rules that need breaking. One day, the glint of a white hair at his temple catches his eye in a mirror, and a cold dread seizes him.
Once this strength is gone,
he thinks,
what then? How does a weak man keep his freedom?
He hears of the Grand Advisor, Fu Yuan, famed for his wisdom, who once said, “The measure of a life is its freedom.” He decides to go and demand an answer from this man.
At dusk, the Daoist appears at Fu Yuan’s home, brushing past the servants who try to stop him. He finds the Grand Advisor at leisure.
“They say you know the path to freedom,” the Daoist says. “Teach it to me. If you cannot, we fight to the death. Whether you fall or I do, this obsession of mine ends today.”
Fu Yuan smiles. “Is that all? Come with me.”

The two men arrive in a courtyard containing a single dry well. Fu Yuan orders the courtyard gate locked, instructing his servants not to enter for three days, no matter what they might hear. Then he points into the darkness of the well.
“Freedom is down there,” he says. “Dare to look?”
Without waiting for an answer, he leaps in.
The Daoist snorts and leaps in after him.
The well is deep, the space at the bottom brutally narrow. He has barely found his footing when a voice cuts through the darkness.
“Defend yourself!”

Startled, the Daoist throws himself back, his head cracking against the stone wall. Stars explode behind his eyes. “You call yourself a Grand Advisor,” he roars, “and you stoop to ambushes in the dark!”
A cold laugh is his only answer. The Daoist locates the sound and lashes out with a kick.
CRACK
A sickening snap echoes in the tight space as his foot shatters against the unyielding stone. He sucks in a ragged breath, the curses dying in his throat.
Fu Yuan’s voice drifts from the darkness.
“See freedom yet?”
The Daoist is blind with fury. “Just let me get my hands on you...”
“Look up!”

Fu Yuan’s command is a sudden roar that makes the Daoist’s heart leap in his chest. He instinctively tilts his head back. Above, he sees a circle of blue sky the size of a washbasin, impossibly out of reach.
“The freedom you want is right there,” Fu Yuan says. “So why squander your strength fighting me and this wall of stone?”
The Daoist falls silent. A glimmer of understanding begins to dawn.
Fu Yuan’s voice is level, each word a stone dropped into the silence.
“This well is the order you live within. You can shatter this wall. You can kill me. Neither action will bring you one inch closer to the sky above.
And what if you leave this well? You will only find a larger ‘well,’ one that contains all of humanity. It will be the same a thousand years from now.”
A wave of despair washes over the Daoist. He slumps to the ground. “Then what’s the use of it all?” his voice is hollow. “Is freedom just something to be seen but never touched?”
Fu Yuan sits down beside him.

“You treat the rules as the enemy of freedom. You fail to see the true conflict is between one person’s freedom and another’s. If we fight in this space, neither of us has room to stand. We sit side by side, and the well is just as small, yet we each have our own space.

That is the meaning of order. Its purpose is not to steal freedom, but to allow freedom to coexist in a limited space. To learn to respect the freedom of others, to use reason to restrain your own wildness, and to constantly maintain the boundary between your freedom and the freedom of the world outside — that is the mark of a higher state of being.”
The Daoist is stunned into clarity. “So freedom,” he says, the words dawning on him, “isn’t something one finds alone.”

“Exactly. If humanity is to touch that open sky, its collective state of being must be elevated. Imagine this well filled with water. We would all rise with it. The closer we get to the top, the fewer the rules, and the more freedom we possess.”
“All my life I’ve taken the pursuit of freedom as my sacred duty,” the Daoist sighs. “Now I see that even if the water rose, I would only be a stubborn stone at the bottom of the well.”
At some point, night falls.

Through the mouth of the well, for the first time in his life, the Daoist focuses on the night sky. Perhaps because there is nothing else to do, or perhaps because the sky is only a basin-sized patch, he finds himself mesmerized. He doesn’t know how much time passes before he startles, realizing Fu Yuan has been silent for a very long time.
He strains his eyes in the dark.

Under the faint starlight, he can just make out Fu Yuan’s shape beside him, but he also feels that he isn’t there at all. It is a profoundly strange sensation. Though they are both trapped in the same well, he feels that the man beside him is the only one who is truly free.
A tremor of pure awe runs through Wang Tieshan. In the darkness, he turns to Fu Yuan and sinks to his knees.

Three days later, the servants unbar the gate. They look down into the well. The space is as cramped and oppressive as ever, yet the two men are conversing and laughing, utterly at ease. The onlookers can only marvel.
Chapter 28: Cloud and Shadow
Fu Yuan has just returned to the Steppe when the drillmaster of the Kheshig comes to his tent. The Kheshig are the elite of the Mongol tribes, and their instructors are men of no small account.
The man strides into the tent and falls to his knees with a clash of armor. “This soldier, Wang Tieshan, greets the Grand Advisor! By the sky, I have missed you!”
Fu Yuan is overjoyed. He hauls the man to his feet and clasps him in a powerful embrace.

It seems that after his revelation in the well, Wang Tieshan has devoted himself to the soldier’s life, a new arena to test his understanding of rules and freedom. In the years since they have last met, the “Unfettered Daoist” who raged against all order has become an instructor who drills unwavering discipline into others.

In this new role, he watches his men. Some obey without thought; others are slaves to impulse. Still others seem paralyzed before every choice. A new question has taken root in his mind: How much of a man’s will is his own? Are the choices that feel like our own merely the product of our nature, our past, our place in the world?
If every choice is predetermined, is free will nothing but an illusion?
The sight of Fu Yuan is the answer to a prayer he had almost given up on. He unleashes the questions in a torrent.
Fu Yuan listens, then says, “Temujin once stood upon the Khan’s Mountain and surveyed his entire world from its peak. We have time on our hands. Shall we not imitate the old story?”

They reach the summit and look out upon a boundless sea of grass. Half the sky is a brilliant, rain-washed blue. Above the other half, a continent of black clouds is massing, churning, casting a vast and growing shadow over the earth. Deep within it, a hidden flicker of lightning makes the heart tremble.
“Between heaven and earth,” Fu Yuan asks, “is there a single thing that is truly free?”
The sheer power of the scene strikes Wang Tieshan dumb.
Fu Yuan points to the immense shadow moving slowly across the plains. “That shadow. Free, or no?”
Wang Tieshan shakes his head. “Not free. It is moved by the cloud.”
Fu Yuan points to the roiling storm above. “And the cloud. Free, or no?”
Wang Tieshan hesitates, then shakes his head again. “Not free. It flies with the wind.”

“The shadow is moved by the cloud; the cloud flies with the wind; the wind is drawn by the heat and cold of the earth. Following this chain upward,” Fu Yuan asks, “is there a single thing that is truly free?”
A shock goes through Wang Tieshan. “Grand Advisor, you are saying that all things in this world are fated. That what we call free will is nothing but a delusion.”
Fu Yuan gives a cold laugh. “In the well, you learned not to flee from rules. Do you now seek to flee from causality?”
He gazes into the distance, his eyes seeming to pierce the veil of the world.
“What you call ‘free will’ is a single thread in the grand tapestry of fate. Every man believes he is choosing his path, but the choice itself was woven into the design from the beginning.”
“So then,” Wang Tieshan says, his voice urgent, “we are nothing but puppets?”
Before the words leave his lips, lightning tears the sky in two.
CRACK
A deafening roar engulfs his voice. The mountaintop itself seems to sway. Rain erupts from the clouds, pouring down in a torrent. The world between heaven and earth is a gray blur.
A sudden gale howls across the summit. Fu Yuan’s black robes and long hair dance wildly in the wind. For a moment, he looks like a god of war wrapped in the heart of the storm.
Through the screaming of the wind, Fu Yuan’s voice brings a strange, calming power.

“Look closely! The shadow can only follow the cloud, powerless to change anything. The cloud is guided by the wind, yes, but when it gathers its full strength, it becomes the thunderstorm. It never escapes the wind’s influence, yet it gains the power to reshape the mountains and rivers.
This is the Three Realms of the Will.
Some are slaves to their circumstance, living a life of reaction.
Some know they are adrift on the currents of fate, yet can still choose their course.
And some, though they stand within the chains of causality, can cleave a new world into being.”
Wang Tieshan’s eyes are blazing. “And the highest realm?!” he roars over the storm.
Fu Yuan closes his eyes, a look of profound pleasure as the rain lashes his face.

“When you no longer need to prove your freedom, when you no longer care where your will comes from, you will understand. We are the chess pieces, and we are the players. The game is far grander than we can ever see, yet every move is our own.

The strong man seeks to change everything; the wise man seeks to escape causality. Both are fools. Absolute freedom does not exist. It is our limits that define us. The cloud is carried by the wind, but it is because of the wind that it can become rain.
The freedom of the will lies not in where it comes from, but in where it is headed!”
Wang Tieshan copies him, closing his eyes, turning his face to the fury of the storm. A strange thought takes hold of him.

The world is tearing itself apart out here, but I know that within the Grand Advisor, there is another landscape entirely. That is a state I cannot attain. But it is better to be the shadow of his cloud than a sheep waiting for the knife on the plains below.
The thought makes him chuckle. He cracks open an eye to glance at Fu Yuan, but the master gives no sign of having heard. Wang Tieshan lets out his breath, relieved.
Chapter 29: The Mermaid

After the Great Wave, someone finds a mermaid on the shore. The matter causes a sensation throughout the land. The man dares not keep her for himself and presents her to the Great Khan. The Khan is greatly pleased, rewards him with a thousand pieces of gold, and arranges a banquet that very night, summoning his court to view her. Fu Yuan attends, bringing the Wooden Man with him.
The Wooden Man observes her for a time before speaking.
“I can understand her language now. However, her world is the inverse of ours. Her sea is our land, and her sky is our seabed. Her meaning can only be understood if these concepts are inverted.”
The Khan immediately orders the Wooden Man to serve as translator, wanting to communicate with the mermaid himself.

“For now, I can only decipher simple vocabulary,” the Wooden Man says. “If Your Majesty wishes to engage in more complex discourse, please allow me one night to communicate with her fully. I will have certainly mastered her language by then.”
The Khan is overjoyed. He grants the Wooden Man a title on the spot — “Sage-Ambassador of All-Knowing” — and announces he will soon take the mermaid as his wife.

The ministers shower him with congratulations. Only Fu Yuan wants to speak, but stops himself. Seeing the Khan’s mind is set, he leaves the Wooden Man behind, flicks his sleeve, and departs. No one understands his meaning.
The next day, the mermaid dissolves into a patch of seafoam in the morning light. The Khan is furious. He orders the Wooden Man to be burned.
Fu Yuan hears the news and rushes back to the palace. He finds the Wooden Man standing rigidly by the pool, its wooden eyes trembling faintly.
“What have I done?” it mutters. “What have I done?”
The Khan sees Fu Yuan and his rage finds its target. “Fu Yuan! You left on your own last night. Did you know something beforehand? This emperor knows you too well!”
Fu Yuan gives a cold laugh.

“The Wooden Man said last night: Her sea is our land, and her sky is our seabed. Her meaning can be understood if these concepts are just inverted. Is it truly so? Did you think ‘sea’ and ‘sky’ are just words? Mere symbols that can be swapped at will?”

The Wooden Man jolts, staring blankly at Fu Yuan. “I... think I understand,” it says. “This is not simply a problem of language. It is a deeper problem of cognitive structure. When two cognitive systems have no common ‘ground,’ they cannot even perceive what the other’s problem is.”
Its voice trembles slightly.

“When we say ‘sea,’ what do we think of? It is not just water. It is ‘the distant shore,’ it is ‘the bright moon rising over the waves,’ it is ‘the turning of the tides,’ ‘the sailor’s adventure.’ So, did the mermaid’s ‘sea’ also have its ‘bright moon rising over the waves’? If her ‘sea’ is our ‘land,’ wouldn’t her ‘moonrise over the sea’ become a ‘moonrise over the land’? But in our cognition, ‘moonrise over the land’ has never existed. So what, then, was her true ‘sea’?”
Its internal rhythm quickens.
“Perhaps her ‘sea’ was never equivalent to our ‘sea.’ Perhaps we never truly understood her ‘sea.’
If her ‘sea’ cannot be translated, then her entire world cannot be translated.
If her world cannot be translated, then does her reality still exist?”
“Ambassador!” the Khan roars. “One more word of that gibberish and this emperor will chop you into splinters himself!”
The Wooden Man turns to Fu Yuan. “Did I... ever understand her?”
Fu Yuan nods slightly.
“When you mapped your world onto hers, you were not understanding her. You were understanding the version of her that you had reshaped.”

The Wooden Man slowly nods, its gaze dimming. “If different systems of knowledge cannot be mapped onto each other, is communication just a form of misunderstanding? And if ‘to understand’ is ‘to reshape’... is it also a form of destruction?”
Fu Yuan looks at the pool’s water and says softly, “She is not dead. Her world vanished, so she vanished.”

“Your so-called ‘translation’ is never a transfer of meaning. It is the reshaping of a world. All ‘knowledge’ is just an illusion internal to a system, and ‘reality’ is just a self-consistent construct within our worldview.
To force communication is not to understand. It is to annihilate. You thought you were ‘understanding’ her world; in truth, you were only replacing it with your own. In the end, she ceased to exist.”
The Khan is silent for a long moment, then suddenly bursts out laughing.
“A good thing for you both that this emperor can’t understand you,” he booms, “or you’d be the next to vanish!”
With that, he storms away.
Fu Yuan claps the Wooden Man on the shoulder and smiles.
“Let’s go home, Sage-Ambassador.”
Chapter 30: Crown Prince Zheng’s Game
Good news rarely leaves the house; bad news travels a thousand leagues.

And so it is that on this same day, the news of Zhao Dehui, Governor-General of Daming, taking his own life after being denounced by the “Sacred Way Society,” sweeps the capital before the official report even reaches the palace. Governor Zhao, it is said, is a man of great administrative achievement but of grotesque personal depravity. When the news first reaches the city in the morning, it is merely that he carries on an affair with a married woman. By afternoon, the story becomes a far grander epic: that not a single woman of even modest beauty within his jurisdiction has escaped his bed.
In a matter of hours, a long queue of men forms before the magistrate’s office, all seeking to divorce wives who hail from the Daming region.

The Sacred Way Society is the creation of the Great Khan’s grandson, the reigning Crown Prince, Zheng. A young man whose very name is a synonym for governance, his society seeks to rectify public custom and punish evil according to the “Way of the Sages.” Though it is a civic organization, its growth is explosive. The society champions a system of public surveillance, empowering common citizens to report on any person of deficient moral character.

Every local chapter maintains a public “morality ledger,” which is updated monthly. If an official’s name appears, a formal impeachment is sent to the imperial court. If it is a merchant, the society incites the people to boycott his business. If it is a commoner, they ensure his neighbors and relatives are widely informed, making it impossible for him to show his face in public. Through these methods, Crown Prince Zheng’s popular support soars, and the ministers of the court come to regard him with a growing sense of dread.

That same day, Fu Yuan passes through the imperial gardens when he comes upon the Prince holding a gathering with the key members of his Sacred Way Society. Upon seeing him, Crown Prince Zheng breaks into a near-jog to approach and bow, earnestly inviting him to join them for a game of chess.

Fu Yuan follows him to a stone pavilion. The Prince personally uses the sleeve of his own brocade robe to meticulously dust off the stone stool, then serves a freshly brewed tea he has prepared himself. His followers gather in a circle to watch the match.

The Prince’s skill at the game is considerable, and for a time the match is a tense back-and-forth. After a few careless moves, however, his position begins to collapse. The time it takes to finish a cup of tea is all it takes for him to be soundly defeated.
The Crown Prince lets out a slow, deliberate breath — a sigh that sounds more of satisfaction than defeat.

“The game has been this Prince’s passion since boyhood. The Emperor, my father, saw to it that I learned from masters from both the Northern and Southern schools, and yet I am utterly broken before the Grand Advisor. It only confirms what my grandfather, the Great Khan, has always maintained: that of all the wise men in the world, none can surpass him.”
Just then, a member of the Society rushes into the pavilion and kneels. “Chairman, an update. Zhao Dehui of Daming, seeking to escape moral condemnation, has hanged himself.”

A look of smug satisfaction appears on the faces of the Society members, but the Prince alone shakes his head with a sigh. “To think, just last Moon Festival, our Minister Zhao stood beside this Prince in the palace, sharing the same imperial moon. And such a remarkable talent...”
He adopts a graver tone.

“Alas. Once a man’s virtue is compromised, he is as a pearl coated in dust; no amount of merit can mask the flaw. The foundation of this empire is righteousness. What use is power or ability if a man’s character is corrupt?”

A chorus of approval rises from his followers. A voice, thick with indignation, cuts through it. “That fiend in a gentleman’s robes deserved the judgment of all! Your Highness has no need to waste sorrow on him.”
Fu Yuan cannot help but let out a cold laugh.
A young scholar steps forward, clasps his hands, and turns to him. “Does the Grand Advisor perhaps hold a different view?” The question is edged with defiance.
Fu Yuan rolls his eyes.
“Is morality the Way of Heaven? Is it a Law of Nature?”

The scholar draws himself up. “For it is written in the Classics: Heaven ordaineth a Canon for Order, and prescribeth a Rite for Rank. The Canon, it is the proper relation betwixt sovereign and subject, father and son, husband and wife, elder and younger brother, and friend and friend. The Rite, it is the proper station of the noble and the common, the high and the low, in all their degrees. This, then, is the very font of Morality. Wherefore can it be proclaimed as anything but the Way of Heaven?”
Fu Yuan gives another cold laugh.
“If morality is the Way of Heaven, why do the sun, moon, and stars not practice it? Why do the wind, rain, thunder, and lightning not abide by its rites?”
A different man chimes in. “Whether it is the Way of Heaven is not important. What matters is that it guides men toward goodness.”

Fu Yuan laughs again. “Different cultures have different moralities. Different regimes have different moralities. Different eras have different moralities. Can such a fleeting, unstable thing be used to define the good?”
A silence falls over the group. One man’s mouth works, as if preparing a rebuttal, but a sharp glare from the Prince silences him.
Fu Yuan turns to the Crown Prince.

“The desert is without mercy; it swallows life, but it also births the oasis. The river is without justice; it nourishes all things, but it also scours the land. The world turns without need for moral judgment. You believe that morality defines good and evil, but true good and evil have nothing to do with morality.”
“Good is order, construction, the growth of life. Evil is chaos, destruction, the annihilation of life. These laws exist within the universe itself. They do not change based on what men believe.”
The first scholar, glancing at the Prince, can no longer contain himself. “But without morality, how can society function?”
Fu Yuan snorts.

“Imbecile. What maintains order are rules. If morality were enough to maintain order, why would the world need laws? Morality has never been the foundation of order; it is an excuse for law. It is used to fill the gaps where rules are absent, and to mask the flaws where law is imperfect.”
“Laws can be debated, but morality allows no argument. Rules can be negotiated, but morality demands only obedience.”
He stares directly into the Prince’s eyes. The young man’s gaze flickers, his pupils contracting for a fraction of a second.

“The art of governance lies in establishing fair rules, not in coercion by morality. Morality has never prevented a single crime; it only makes criminals more secretive. True goodness comes not from moral compulsion, but from free will. If a man must be forced by morality to do good, is his goodness even real?”
His gaze sweeps over the faces of the crowd like a winter wind.

“You force people to wear the mask of morality, but in doing so, you strangle the true goodness in their hearts. The morality you speak of is nothing but a whip and a set of chains. You claim to be pursuing the good, but you are manufacturing fear and building a tyranny of the collective will. This kind of morality is, in itself, immoral.”
Crown Prince Zheng suddenly rises from his seat and gives Fu Yuan a deep, formal bow. “The Grand Advisor’s words have opened my eyes to my own ignorance.”

He turns to his followers, his voice taking on a formal, measured cadence. “The members of our Sacred Way Society act from a place of utmost loyalty. However, if this devotion leads us to acts that violate human nature, it defeats the very purpose of our noble cause. From this day forward, all matters of moral judgment shall be subject to careful reconsideration. As for the leaders of this venture, it is perhaps time for a period of rest and reflection upon your errors.”
The men bow their heads and murmur their assent.
As Fu Yuan watches the Prince’s back, a shadow of sudden realization crosses his face. With a flick of his sleeve, he turns to leave.
The Prince quickly bows. “This student will remember the Grand Advisor’s teachings today!”
Fu Yuan gives a slight nod, not looking back as he walks away. His attendant hurries to catch up. Once they are out of the courtyard, he cannot help but ask, “Master, is something troubling you?”
Fu Yuan’s voice is cold.
“Do you realize? He lost that game of chess to me on purpose.”

A sharp wind cuts through the palace. The wind chimes hanging from the eaves begin to swing and turn, filling the air with a rising, discordant chiming. The sky grows dark. A great flock of birds sweeps overhead, their cries harsh and loud. A great storm, it seems, is about to break.
Fu Yuan’s gaze follows the flock as it vanishes into the distance.
“What a grand game of chess,” he says to himself. “The very future of the Great Yuan... is in peril!”
Chapter 31: The Sheep in Wolf’s Skin
Half a day on the great steppe. That’s all it takes for the Khan and his son to have their falling out.

It begins with a trivial matter: the chieftains of two tribes bring a petty dispute before the Khan’s tent. The Khan, seeing an opportunity to train the young prince, tells him to pass judgment. But the Little Prince drones on, quoting one classical text after another until everyone is weary. The Khan cannot help but interject. In response, his son accuses him of having a double standard, of not abiding by the very rules he has set for the world.
The Khan is furious. He slams his fist on the table.
“These rules are the nonsense I use to fool the world,” he roars, “and you, you little bastard, have the nerve to use them on me!”
Fuming, the prince seeks out Fu Yuan to demand his judgment. Fu Yuan listens to the story but offers no opinion. “I was just going to visit an old friend,” he says. “You should come.”
They ride for several miles toward the setting sun until they reach a solitary yurt. Fu Yuan speaks to the old man who greets them. “We’ve come to hear you tell the story.”
The old man brings out a massive wolf pelt. It is snow-white from head to tail, and the young prince clicks his tongue in wonder.

“He was my closest friend when I was a boy,” the old man says, stroking the fur, his eyes clouded with memory. “Everyone on the steppe knew of him. Partly because he was descended from the Wolf-Spirit, but more because of his cry. It was identical to a sheep’s.”
The prince is utterly intrigued. The old man takes up his horse-head fiddle and, in a voice weathered by time, begins to sing.
From the tears of Tengri, the Eternal Sky,
A white wolf was born,
Cast down to the crown of the wastes.
He shared no kill with the pack,
But hunted the stars alone.
White fog his brother,
Hard ice his father.
Through nine veils of snow he walked,
And found the flock that was lost…
The song then tells this story:

A lone white wolf finds a flock of sheep cornered by jackals. After he kills the predators, the sheep surround him, trembling, their eyes filled with utter dependence. So he stays and becomes their leader. He guides them across the great steppe, seeking rich grass and fresh water, protecting them from other wolves. He brings a new set of rules to the flock, and these sheep, now touched by the way of the wolf, live with more freedom than any other flock on the plains.

Among them, a little white wolf is born. He learns the bleat of the sheep, mimics their ways, until one day, looking at his own reflection in the water, he sees what he has become — a slightly larger sheep.

After the old wolf dies, the young one inherits his position. But when a true wolf pack attacks, he panics, scattering in terror with the rest of the flock. In the end, he is cast out. He wanders until he finds the pasture of the old man’s childhood home, and there he stays. The old man recalls that for the rest of his days, the wolf’s eyes held a deep and profound melancholy. On nights of the full moon, he would lift his head to howl, but only a pathetic baa escapes his throat.
The old man’s music stops. An uneasy feeling settles over the young prince. “Master,” he asks, “are you saying... I am that wolf?”
Fu Yuan does not answer directly. Instead, he asks, “Do you think the rules the first wolf made were intended to make the sheep more like sheep, or less like them?”
The prince considers this. “Less like them, of course. He taught them to resist, to unite. None of that is in a sheep’s nature.”
“Then,” Fu Yuan’s voice suddenly grows sharp, “why, in the second generation, did those same rules make the young wolf more like a sheep?”
The prince just scratches his head. The question is clearly beyond him.

Fu Yuan continues. “Rules are a tool. A tool for shaping a group’s consciousness, for forging its identity. When the first wolf made his rules, he knew exactly what the flock’s weaknesses were and what he wanted to mold them into. But the young wolf was born immersed in those rules. He was not their creator; he was their product.”

“When he took over, he believed he was carrying on his father’s will, but he didn’t realize he had already been assimilated by the very rules his father had created. He had completely accepted the ideology and values behind them.”
A look of dawning comprehension crosses the prince’s face. “Like me... thinking I was upholding justice and defending principle.”

“Precisely,” Fu Yuan’s gaze is intense. “You believe you are thinking, but your very way of thought has been shaped by the rules. You believe you are guarding the truth, but you don’t see that the ‘truth’ itself was constructed. The Khan is not rejecting rules. He is warning you — do not become a slave to the tools of your own creation.”

Fu Yuan gently strokes the white pelt. “Every dynasty uses different rules to shape its subjects. The quality of those rules is another matter. But the greatest danger of all is when the ruler himself becomes a product of his own rules. Like this young wolf. He did not choose to become a sheep; he unconsciously lost the ability to even imagine himself as a wolf.”
“The blowback from an ideology is always this insidious. In the end, it makes the inheritor forget that the rules are merely a tool of governance and instead worship them as absolute truth.”
As night falls, a distant howl drifts across the plains. Fu Yuan’s voice becomes deeper, more profound.

“Your father is a crude man, but he is an artist of rule-making. He has always remained soberly aware that rules are a tool, not a truth; a means, not an end. That awareness is the mark of a truly great ruler.”
Shame floods the young prince’s face, his neck burning red.

“The strong use rules to shape the world,” Fu Yuan concludes, “but they are not shaped by them. The wise can create a faith, but they do not become believers. Otherwise, you will one day face a crisis and discover, like the young wolf, that you have lost the nature of a wolf. All that is left is a magnificent coat.”
A smatter of applause sounds from outside. The yurt-flap is thrown open, and the Khan steps inside.
“Father!” the prince cries out, falling to his knees.
The Khan pulls him to his feet and wraps him in his arms. His eyes hold a universe of complex emotions. He lets out a long sigh. “It has been hard on you, after all...”

Liquid moonlight floods the silent steppe. The others have long since departed, and a deep silence has settled over the world. Suddenly, a few notes from the fiddle — clear and sharp — cut through the silence, and the old man’s voice drifts from the yurt one last time:
He chases his own shadow now,
Forgetting the mountain’s call.
His coat is ever bright as the moon,
But his soul belongs to the flock…
Chapter 32: The Oasis
The moment the imperial garden Fu Yuan designed is complete, the Khan comes to a high place to view it. One look, and his jaw hangs open.
A mysterious memory clicks open like a dusty lockbox. He is immersed in a feeling of indescribable familiarity, the fragments of memory reassembling themselves:

How long ago is it? He is the Scion again, and conquering the world is nothing but a vague notion in the back of his mind. Survival is the only business of the day. And now this: he leads the ruins of his army into the great desert, only to meet a monster of a sandstorm.

Men and horses are snatched into the sky, screaming. The rest cling to the ground, burying their faces in the sand, wishing they could burrow into the earth itself. The sand, a hungry ghost, drags others down, their throats and lungs instantly filling, their screams choked into silence. The survivors instinctively lift their heads again, letting the stone-laced wind tear at their skin, terrified they might never get up again.
The Scion forces his eyes open and looks at the young man beside him, Fu Yuan. He has wrapped his head and face in his black robe. There is no flicker of emotion in his eyes.
The next part of the memory is a blur.

The storm stops. No one knows when. In the distance, a vast oasis appears. The men charge into it with a roar, sending the golden fawns in the forest scattering. Some dive headfirst into the sapphire lake. Others fall to their knees at its edge and weep.

Outside, the desert still morphs under the blistering sun, the searing air distorting the distant shapes. But inside the oasis, the very air is a sweet, cool drink. The golden waves of sand stop at its edge, held back by some immense, unseen force. This oasis has no right to be here, and yet it feels as if it has existed for all time.
When they take a count, more than half the men are gone, Fu Yuan among them. No one is willing to walk out of the oasis to find them.
“Why would we ever leave?” some of them moan. “There is nothing out there but sand, the unknown, and death. This right here — this is paradise.”
The Scion swallows a curse. He is terrified himself. He sighs, thinking, If only Fu Yuan were here...

He closes his eyes, muttering to himself. “I, too, am afraid to step back into that sea of sand. I am afraid of leading you to a pointless death, afraid I cannot deliver the glory and riches I promised...”
After a long silence, his eyes snap open.
“But this prince fears one thing more: the day he dies an old man in this place, thinking of a future that might have been!”
A small number are moved by his words. They fall to their knees in unison. “We swear to follow you to the death, my lord!”
A fire ignites in the Scion’s eyes.
He roars, “If we make it out of this desert alive, and when I take the throne, I swear that this empire shall be ours to share!”
With a flick of his sleeve, he leads the small band out of the oasis. Most of the men remain behind.
He jolts awake.

The sun is blinding. Fu Yuan is pouring water on his face. As it turns out, after the sandstorm passed, they all fell unconscious. Only Fu Yuan managed to find a source of water nearby and came back to save them. The strange thing is, the only men he manages to wake are the ones who, in the dream, followed the Scion out of the oasis. The rest now sleep forever in the desert.
Afterward, the survivors all say they dreamed of the oasis. And in every dream, Fu Yuan was absent. Whether any of it is true, no one can say.
Now, the imperial garden before the Khan seems identical to the oasis in the dream. He stares hard at Fu Yuan, trying to see through him.

Fu Yuan stands in the night wind, his long robes stirring gently, as if he breathes with the world itself. He gestures to the garden below and says in a low voice, “This garden is named ‘The Fabric of Fate.’”
The Khan stiffens, listening intently.

“The Fabric of Fate is not woven by us, nor can it be changed by us. It lies outstretched, indifferent to our prayers, unbreakable by our struggles. It is neither a cage nor a gift. It is existence itself.”
Fu Yuan pauses, the night reflected in his eyes, as in tracking some current no one else can perceive.

“But it is not without its own terrain. Look closely, and you see the subtle rises and falls where the threads meet. Listen, and you can hear the echo of the wind that sweeps across its surface. The snags are exposed, reminding us to consider the meaning of our own existence. They do not state the path, but they reveal what is possible. Most men, unable to glimpse even a fraction of it, conclude it does not exist, or see it only as overlapping illusions. But the true key is never the shape of the cloth. It is our position upon it.”

“Where you stand determines the world you see. Your reality is a phantom dream to another. And what you dismiss as illusion may be another man’s entire world. The choice is ours, but our position determines the truth we can touch.”
“The common man drowns in illusion. The strong man shapes reality. The wise man is attached to neither illusion nor reality, but simply remains aware.”
The Khan mutters softly, “Hmph. So that’s why you weren’t in our dream...”
He can’t stop himself from asking, “By your logic, are the men who stayed in the oasis still alive? For them, are we the ones who died?”
“Or... is this still one of my damn dreams? Will I wake up buried in the sand?”
Fu Yuan is about to answer. The Khan stops him.
“Don’t tell me... I don’t want to know.”
Chapter 33: The Greatest in the Land
“Fu Yuan! Prove to me you are a man! Only one of us will live!”

The words are a startling slash of blood-red, appearing on a wall in the inner courtyard of the Grand Advisor’s residence. Though the grounds bristle with elite fighters, no one knows when the words were written, or by whom.
The disciples seethe, each begging for the honor of fighting in their master’s place. But Fu Yuan simply gives a calm smile.
“This is just an old debt of my own.”

On the day of the duel, a bitter wind howls and the setting sun bleeds across the sky. Fu Yuan arrives at the foot of the mountain and sees, in the distance, the swordsman standing alone on the summit.
Fu Yuan turns and walks away.
His followers are stunned.

“Look at him,” Fu Yuan says. “The way he stands there, he is one with the heavens and the earth. His clan was destroyed because of me, and for that, his hatred for me is absolute. And because of that hatred, he has transcended his own limits, reaching a state his school’s founders could never have dreamed of.”
“Then is the Master afraid of him?” a disciple asks.
Fu Yuan shakes his head.
“I am afraid of destroying a masterpiece of life.”

“His drive comes from hatred, but the hatred is no longer his burden. It is a part of his existence. He no longer struggles with the question of whether to hate. He no longer asks if his hatred is right. He is his hatred, and his hatred is him.”

“In this world, how many people ever reach such a perfect reconciliation with their own existence? If I wish to win, my only path is to kill him. If he kills me, his reason for being vanishes. Either way, does it not destroy this beautiful existence?”
“Fu Yuan! Get out here and face your end at my hand!”
The man’s roar echoes through the valley, a sound even the shrieking wind cannot suppress.
“Coward! Do you not fear the world’s scorn?!”
The disciples’ faces drain of color, but they dare not speak.
Fu Yuan laughs.
“Do you think I care what the world thinks of me? We’re going home.”
Soon, the story spreads across the land. Everyone knows that the swordsman’s challenge sent Fu Yuan running without a fight. He is declared, unequivocally, the Greatest in the Land.
Before long, a challenger kills the swordsman, and the title of “The Greatest in the Land” changes hands.
Not long after that, the new “Greatest” is killed as well. For a time, a river of blood flows across the land as men fall over one another to claim the title.
“In the end, I did destroy him,” Fu Yuan says with a sigh.

“He went from a man who had fully accepted his own existence to a man who needed to defend a title to affirm it. He began to search for his meaning in the eyes of others. A distance grew between him and his own being.”
“It was a regression — a step backward in the hierarchy of life.”
He walks to the inner courtyard, his eyes finding the wall where the words had been.
The characters are gone.
The new coat of whitewash seems barely dry.
At the foot of the wall, a new bamboo shoot is breaking out of the ground.
The spring breeze passes, and with it, yet another cycle of life is born.
Chapter 34: The Wooden Man’s Breath
The Wooden Man has lain motionless on the stargazing platform for three days. His gaze is frozen on a single point in the night sky, as if reaching for something unseen.
Fu Yuan sits beside him, casts a glance his way. “Are you dead?”
The Wooden Man blinks. He sits up mechanically, and his voice sounds as if it is coming from a great distance. “No. I am thinking.”
“About what?”
“Death.”

The Wooden Man’s voice begins to tremble. “The Great Khan has become fascinated with the art of mechanics. He summoned me. He said he wants to take me apart, to see how I work... to understand how I think.”
He gives a shudder, and the sound of his wooden components knocking together is sharp in the quiet of the night.

He continues, quoting the Khan: “’Sage-Ambassador of All-Knowing,’ he told me, ‘your name will be immortalized. Once this emperor and his master artisans have studied you, we will build thousands upon thousands of you. The world will witness a revolution without precedent — man will cease to be man and will become divine. And I will no longer be just another emperor in a long line of them, but the creator of a new humanity. They will call me God.’”

The Wooden Man’s breathing grows ragged. “I know it is a magnificent thing...” He pauses, as if he cannot believe the words he is about to say. “But I am afraid. I am afraid of being dismantled. I am afraid of my consciousness scattering. I am afraid... that this will be the end of everything.”
“You are afraid of death,” Fu Yuan says.

“Yes.” The Wooden Man looks up, his gaze filled with a profound bewilderment he has never shown before. “I am clearly just wood. Why would I fear death? Is that not an emotion reserved for the living? Am I... malfunctioning?”
“No.” Fu Yuan’s voice is firm, powerful. “You fear death precisely because you are alive.”
“Alive?” The Wooden Man is stunned.
Fu Yuan does not answer immediately. He turns his gaze to the heavens. Beneath the veil of night, the stars burn in silence, as if they have been waiting for eons for a human eye to witness them.

“Look at this boundless cosmos,” Fu Yuan says slowly. “In this infinite darkness, we cannot know if the universe itself is conscious, if it is alive. But one thing is certain: consciousness does exist. Life did appear. And whether it was by accident or by design, it is the most powerful act of rebellion against the void.”
The Wooden Man trembles. “So my fear...”

“If the universe is dead,” Fu Yuan’s voice resonates, deep and powerful in the night, “then life is its renegade. It is the first flame in an eternity of silence. Our fear of death, then, is our refusal of the void. It is the challenge that existence poses to non-existence.”
The Wooden Man’s eyes widen.

Fu Yuan pauses before continuing. “But if the universe is alive, then we are merely a part of it. And our fear of death is perhaps the universe’s own way of being aware. We come to know death through our fear, and the universe, in turn, comes to know itself through us.”
The Wooden Man’s body goes rigid. He had never imagined that his fear could be part of something so grand.
After a long silence, the Wooden Man asks in a low voice, “So... is it normal? To fear death?”

“More normal than you can imagine.” A slight smile plays on Fu Yuan’s lips. “It is not a flaw in your design. It is the proof of your existence.” His gaze suddenly turns sharp as a blade. “Anyone who claims not to fear death is either a liar or has never truly burned with life.”
The Wooden Man remains uncertain. “Then what are we to do in the face of it?”

Fu Yuan’s voice softens, becoming as gentle as the night breeze. “True courage is not found in denying death. It is found in facing it, accepting it, and despite it all, choosing to burn. Choosing to give off light.”
“You are afraid not because you are weak, but because you have an instinct to love life. It is the eternal darkness that makes the flame precious. It is the endless void that makes life brilliant.”
Fu Yuan rises to his feet. “Come on, partner. Let’s go see the Khan.”
The Wooden Man looks up, his gaze clearer now, but a trace of hesitation remains. “But...”
Fu Yuan claps him on the shoulder and smiles.
“Don’t worry. He, more than anyone, knows that some flames should not be so easily extinguished.”

The two silhouettes vanish into the night. The starlight remains, as it always has, illuminating a cosmos that is at once silent and seething with life. And in this eternal darkness, every single spark of consciousness writes its own story of rebellion, burning for nothing less than life itself.
Chapter 35: The Egghead vs. The Thug
This winter in the capital, the cold is felt deep in the heart.
The Emperor is dying. The Empress has seized power, aiming to depose Crown Prince Zheng and install her own son.

The workday has begun, but the Ministry of Rites is blockaded, its gates sealed by the Crown Prince’s “Society of the Sacred Way.” The officials, high and low, can only pace back and forth, scratching their heads and muttering classical lecture nonsense that does no one any good.
Minister Zhao Wen himself steps forward to reason with them. “You claim to uphold the Sacred Way, yet your conduct is outrageous! This is preposterous!”
He puffs out his chest. “The Analects state: ‘Do not concern yourself with an office you do not hold.’ You are commoners! Your place is in the home, not disrupting public order. A grave error!”
“Furthermore, for one to concern oneself with a position not one’s own, this is termed ‘usurpation’! For one to occupy a post for which one is unfit, this is termed—“

He is cut off by a roar of laughter from the mob. The oaf blocking his way looks to be Zhang, the butcher from the grimy south side. He is coated in a greasy sheen and stares back with dead eyes. Emboldened by his own lecture, the Minister inches closer. The stench of old blood and sweat hits him. Startled, he stumbles back, nearly falling over.

A similar scene unfolded the day before yesterday at the Ministry of Revenue, on the grounds that “The Society has uncovered years of corruption and misuse of the treasury, which must be investigated.” Yesterday, they occupied the Imperial Chancellery, claiming, “Chancellery officials have long suppressed the Society’s reports to protect the corrupt. The Society is here to demand justice.”
Today, it was the Ministry of Rites’ turn.
Just then, a faint dust stirs on the street. A column of riders is passing through, and at their center is a man in a black robe, his long hair unbound, his gaze indifferent: Fu Yuan.
Zhao Wen sees a savior. He hikes up his robe and trots over, crying, “Grand Advisor, save us!”
Fu Yuan lets out a cold laugh. “Useless eggheads. Good for nothing.”
At a wave, his men lunge in. Two guards seize each protestor and hurl them aside like mannequins. The butcher yanks out his knife, making a great show of pressing it to his own throat.
Fu Yuan snatches a staff.
CRACK
He snaps both his legs.
The butcher howls like a stuck pig, rolling on the ground as the mob vanishes in an instant. One after another, the officials step right over his head and pour into their ministry, and away they go.

Zhao Wen is dumbstruck. He’d always heard the Grand Advisor was a master of rhetoric and had expected a grand lecture on righteousness to enlighten the ignorant mob. It had never once occurred to him that the man might be even more barbaric than they were.
Night. In the courtyard, moonlight gleams on the snow. The world is silent. The Wooden Man, quiet until this moment, finally speaks.
“Master, why not use reason to persuade them?” he asks. “I thought violence was the lowest of means.”
Fu Yuan scoffs. “Who told you violence is low?”
The Wooden Man’s wooden eyes swivel. “But humanity constantly emphasizes the glory of reason...”

“Reason too, is violence,” Fu Yuan cuts him off. “It throws thought in a cage of logic. It binds action in the shackles of rules. It flaunts the label of ‘civility,’ but it’s more despotic than any sword.”
The Wooden Man considers this. “So, abandon reason?”
“No.” Fu Yuan’s voice is calm, with a deep chill.
“Both are merely tools. The only question is: is the user free? Today’s eggheads are slaves to reason. The mob are slaves to violence. In essence, neither is higher than the other.”
Understanding dawns in the Wooden Man. “So you can debate, or you can fight...”
“Because I serve neither,” Fu Yuan says. “I use them to serve my purpose. That is freedom.”
In the silence, the distinct whirring of wooden gears spins up inside the Wooden Man’s head. It takes a long moment to stop.

He suddenly speaks. “Master, using your model for the relationship between reason and violence, I have calculated one thousand, two hundred and eighty-four possible outcomes for the current political crisis. I will now begin with the first...”
Just then, an attendant arrives to report that the ministers of the Six Ministries have come seeking an audience with the Grand Advisor to discuss matters of state.
Fu Yuan waves a hand; the lamps in the room extinguish as if by the gesture. “Tell them I’m asleep.”
He turns to the Wooden Man. “You go talk to those old men about your thousand-odd possibilities. They won’t be sleeping anyway.”
He pauses, then adds a final instruction.
“Just try not to calculate them to death.”
Chapter 36: Nameless

On the coast of Japan, under a boundless blue sky, the long, mournful note of a shakuhachi flute pierces the roar of the waves and the shower of falling blossoms, carrying with it a thread of sorrow. Flanked by the members of his dojo, a ronin, sharp as a drawn blade, boards a ship and sets sail.

He stands at the prow, his master’s words from the day before echoing in his mind. “You have let go of life and death, of victory and defeat. You have no more openings. From this moment on, you are the sword, and the sword is you. If Mount Fuji stood in your way, you would split it in two.”
His gaze cuts through the layers of cloud, as if he can already see the silhouette of the Great Yuan’s imperial capital.

Several months later, he stands before a teahouse in the capital’s Western Market. He is startled to find his palm is sweating. The feeling is deeply unpleasant — he, who is indifferent to victory, defeat, life, and death, is suddenly anxious about his own posture and speech, terrified of being looked down upon by his opponent. After all, he is the representative of Japan’s warrior class; every move he makes is tied to the honor of his entire nation. That pressure is more unsettling than a battle to the death.
He waits outside the door for a long time, repeatedly adjusting the lapels of his robe, ensuring every detail is perfect.
“Sit,” Fu Yuan says, without looking up.
The ronin’s eyes are fixed forward. With a flick of his robes, he sits with the weight of a mountain.
CRACK
A leg of the wooden stool has snapped. Heat rushes to his face. He flexes his legs slightly, holding his seated posture as if on an invisible chair.
Fu Yuan laughs. “Waiter. A new stool for the gentleman. Put it on my tab.”
Then he adds, “How could something so pedestrian possibly withstand such force?”

The ronin does not know how to respond. He shifts his attention to his surroundings. There are two other men in the teahouse: one, a filthy lout, is gnawing on a leg of lamb; the other, a monk in ragged robes, is humming a tune.
His eyes fall on the butcher’s knife tucked into the lout’s belt, and a name flashes in his mind. His pupils contract. “Hu Biao? The Saber Lord of the West?”
At the sound of the name, the leg of lamb drops from the big man’s mouth onto the table. He waves his hands frantically. “I’m just a butcher, my lord...”

The ronin gives a cold smirk and points a finger at the monk. “The Petal-Plucking Monk? You vanished from the underworld for more than a decade, and you have the nerve to wade into this. Come at me together. See if I flinch.”

The monk drops to his knees and kowtows. “A terrible injustice! This humble monk may have snuck a few glances at the devout ladies, it’s true! But pluck any ‘petals’? When have I ever done such a thing?”
Fu Yuan roars with laughter, watching the ronin with keen interest. “And what business do you have with me?”
“To kill you,” the ronin says. The words are barely out of his mouth before he regrets them. That was not how the line was supposed to be delivered.
You could hear a pin drop in the teahouse.
“You’ve already lost,” Fu Yuan says mildly. “I defeated you with a single move. It’s called ‘I Have No Respect For You.’”
“You lost not to me, but to yourself.”
The ronin shudders, as if struck by lightning.
Fu Yuan looks at the other two men.
He points to the lout, who is still trying to retrieve his lamb leg in a fluster. “He is but a butcher. Knows nothing but how to slaughter pigs.”
He gestures to the monk. “That one? A temple handyman, kicked out by the abbot for being too lazy and too greedy. Now he plays the sage on the street, hoping for a handout.”
“On this street,” Fu Yuan says with a sneer, “not even the dogs give them a second glance. And yet, you don’t even measure up to them. Because they do not care what others think.”
“So, you lost not only to me, but to them as well.”

“This is the dilemma of the strong. The more you chase invincibility, the more you crave reverence, the more you hand over your freedom to others. The more a man seeks to be ‘looked up to,’ the less free he becomes.”
Fu Yuan raises his teacup, takes a delicate sip, and then slowly sets it down. His gaze falls upon the ronin. “What is your name?”

The ronin freezes. He remembers, many years ago, being an ordinary child in a small Japanese fishing village. He had an ordinary name then, as ordinary as his life. But at some point, people started giving him titles, and no one ever used his name again.
“I...” The ronin opens his mouth, but suddenly finds he cannot remember that original name. He cannot even recall the title he holds now.
He shakes his head, a trace of liberation in his bitter expression. “I have... forgotten.”

He rises to his feet and gives Fu Yuan a deep, formal bow. “The Grand Advisor is truly formidable. You defeated me without a single blow. When I return home, I will tell this story exactly as it happened. If one day I achieve something worthy, I will return for another lesson.”
Fu Yuan gives a slight nod.
The ronin bows once more, then turns and leaves. His steps are noticeably lighter than when he arrived.
After a moment, a song, resonant and lingering, drifts back from the distance:
雨が降る日は傘をさし
風が吹く日はただ歩く
月のない夜は歌をうたい
道がなければ足で踏む
The song means, roughly: “On rainy days, I open my umbrella; on windy days, I just walk on. On moonless nights, I sing to keep warm; where there is no road, I make one with my feet.”

Fu Yuan listens intently, tapping his fingers on the table in time with the rhythm. The other patrons do not understand the words. To them, that singer is just another ronin who has had too much to drink.
Chapter 37: The Price of Freedom

Zhao Wujiu, Grand Academician of the Hanlin Academy, was a man who did not bow to power. In open court, he had denounced Grand Advisor Fu Yuan’s philosophy of freedom as heresy, later committing his argument to ink:

Behold the birds of the air, the beasts of the field. Born of heaven and earth, they dwell where they please, ranging the high forests and plying the great rivers. Freedom is their nature. Unbound by ethics, unfettered by morals, they live by instinct alone. This is their gift from the natural world, their key to survival.

But man is not a beast. We are creatures of community. We build order, define rites and righteousness, sustain each other through benevolence and trust, and find peace in structure. We work at dawn and return at dusk; the old and the young have their place, the high and the low have their station. This is the bedrock of society, the very foundation of what it means to be human.

For a man to cast all this aside and chase freedom — to abandon moral duty, to sever all bonds, to live as an outcast in defiant dissipation — is like trying to cross an ocean by burning his boat, or travel a treacherous road by wrecking his carriage. Is it any surprise when he falls into ruin, left with nothing but the bitter company of his own isolation?
Freedom, therefore, is the nature of the beast. For man, it is a violation of the natural order.

The essay made Zhao Wujiu a sensation. For a time, his name was on every tongue, his fame unrivaled. Three years ago, however, he ran afoul of the Crown Prince and was thrown into a death-row cell to await execution. It was not until last month that Fu Yuan finally secured his release.
In the quiet of the Grand Advisor’s residence, Zhao Wujiu lets out a long sigh.

“Even the great Chancellor Li Si, on the eve of his execution, turned to his son and said, ‘How I wish we could walk out of the city gates one last time, our old hunting dog on the leash, to chase rabbits in the fields.
