But... I fear those days are lost to us now.’ In the darkness of my cell, this humble student felt that very same regret...”
He raises a sleeve to wipe at his eyes.
“But what haunted me most was our debate, Grand Advisor, from all those years ago. It took losing my freedom to learn its true worth. What a profound fool I was.”
“Freedom is priceless to you now,” Fu Yuan scoffs. “So is a feast to a starving man. A fantasy. Once he’s eaten his fill, the feast is forgotten.”
Zhao Wujiu’s expression deepens. “You mean to say we treat freedom as nothing more than a baseline need?”
Fu Yuan nods.

“Freedom is a realm of existence. A sheep on the butcher’s block, an eagle with broken wings — both have lost their freedom. But you cannot speak of their freedom in the same breath. The sheep knows the safety of the pen; it cannot imagine the vastness of the sky. The eagle soars through the nine heavens; does it dream of the warmth of the nest? Your old argument, that men must trade freedom for the price of loneliness — that was the sheep asking how it could ever huddle for warmth if it grew wings.”

“The freedom I speak of is not a need. It is the state of our being. I have pulled you from the executioner’s block, yes. I have lifted the sheep from the butcher’s table. But I cannot make a sheep into an eagle.”
As if struck by lightning, Zhao Wujiu collapses to the floor, prostrate.

“The sheep that knew only how to huddle for warmth — that creature died in the darkness of his cell. This humble student now yearns for the open sky! From this day forward, I want nothing more than to leave the court, to live among the mountains and rivers, and to follow you, Grand Advisor. Teach me how to be a Free Man. Please, take me as your disciple!”
“If that’s your plan,” Fu Yuan says, a cold smile touching his lips, “I’d be better off throwing you back in your cell.”
Zhao Wujiu’s eyes go wide with shock.
Fu Yuan’s gaze is a physical force, boring into him, seeing every thought.

“Leaving the court? Living in nature? You’re just escaping one pen to become a useless wild sheep. And following me? You want to abdicate the burden of your own thoughts because you’re terrified to face yourself. Do that, and you will never be a Free Man. Why would I make an enemy of the Crown Prince for that?”
The words hit Zhao Wujiu like a physical blow. He is silent for a long moment, processing. Then, he gives a single, powerful nod.
“This student understands. That isn’t freedom. It’s escape. A Free Man must also be a clear-sighted man. A brave man. A resolute man.”

A flicker of life returns to his withered face, and at the sight of it, the corner of Fu Yuan’s mouth lifts in a true smile. At that exact moment, a disciple enters. The Crown Prince, he reports, has recommended Zhao Wujiu to the Empress. He has praised the academic’s world-class talent and declared that, now that he is exonerated and enjoys the Grand Advisor’s favor, he must be given a position of great responsibility.
Fu Yuan snorts. “So, he’s playing chess again.”
He throws open the window. A blast of wind invades the room, carrying a deep chill. In the courtyard, the trees thrash and sway. A low rumble of thunder echoes from the horizon.
He turns to Zhao Wujiu, who already wears an expression of intense calculation. “A storm is coming,” Fu Yuan says. “Do you dare be the eagle that rides its winds?”
Without a trace of hesitation, Zhao Wujiu drops to his knees and touches his forehead to the floor.
“This disciple is ready to accept the challenge.”
Their eyes lock. At that instant, a crack of thunder shakes the very foundations of the house, and the window lattices rattle in their frames.
And together, they laugh.
Chapter 38: The Insight of the Old King
It is the Feast of the Annunciation. A gray sky hangs like a shroud over the eternal snows of the Ararat Mountains, and through the halls of the Armenian palace drifts the sound of holy hymns.

Before the assembled court, the young prince Artavazd kneels. His hand is upon the Gospels, and his oath is for the old king. “In the name of the Holy Son, I swear to offer the last drop of my blood for the dominion of this kingdom!”

For a shadow of unease lies upon this ancient land, nestled between the Black Sea and the Caucasus. The iron hooves of the Mongol Khan are at the gates of Persia itself; even the emperor in Constantinople sends secret emissaries, pleading for an alliance against the northern storm.

A scout returns, bearing intelligence from the Mongol camp, but the old king dismisses the report with a wave of his hand. “Speak to me of the men,” he commands. “What tales are told of the Khan, and of this Fu Yuan?”

The scout considers. “Majesty, it is said that after the Khan’s army took the capital of Khwarazm — the victory that brought all of Central Asia to its knees — the Khan took a day of rest for himself. He slipped away from his own guard, and with his wife, went disguised into the conquered city to watch the wrestling contests of the Tajik and the Turk.”
“The empty priorities of a barbarian,” the prince scoffs.

“And of Fu Yuan,” the scout continues, “it is said he led an embassy with a train of many dozen carriages. On the road, he came upon refugees — Persians and Georgians. He bade them all to ride, until every carriage was full and there was no place left for him. He made the rest of the journey on his feet, telling them stories in his rough, broken Persian.”
“A Grand Advisor, demeaning himself so,” the prince sneers. “What becomes of his station?”
The old king hears these things and closes his eyes. A long silence falls. At last, he lets out a deep sigh.
“Prepare the white olive branch. Tomorrow, I shall go myself and offer our submission to the Khan.”
The court is frozen in shock. The prince leaps to his feet. “Why?” he demands, his voice a tremor of outrage.
“Do you know the hardest trial of this world?” the old king asks.
“Can any trial be harder than the defense of the Holy Land?” the prince retorts.

“The hardest trial,” the old king’s voice takes on the serene timbre of a vesper bell, “is to not become a captive of your own station. A man who stands in a high place is soon besieged by the expectations of the world, and by the limitations of his own title. Soon, his every gesture must befit his rank. He forges for his own soul a cage of gold, and his growth is forever at an end.”
The old king rises and walks to the great window, where the light from the stained glass scatters like jewels upon his silver beard.

“One is a conqueror who makes the lands of the West and East tremble. The other, an advisor whose name is a dread omen in the courts of kings. By all rights, they should be consumed by their own majesty, forever posturing like our nobility. Yet they are not bound. They are free. Do you not see the meaning of it?”
He turns to face his son.

“These two men stand upon the summit of the world, yet they possess this freedom. Does that not prove their spirits — and their fates — are destined for something far greater than what they already possess? If such men can still grow, then will they not surely become the masters of this world? And if they are fated to hold dominion over this earth, what meaning remains in our resistance? What purpose is there in our bloodshed?”
The prince lowers his head in silence.

That night, he steals away with a thousand loyal knights. They ride for the Kingdom of Georgia, to raise the banners of the Caucasus in a final, doomed war. When the news is brought to the old king, his heart gives way, and he departs from the world of men.

A year later, the vanquished prince kneels in the dust before the Khan. He gazes up at the titanic figures of the conqueror and his advisor, and in the clarity of his utter ruin, the terrible weight of his father’s wisdom descends upon him. He is given a cell in a fortress in the Caucasus. There, on nights when the moon is high, he kneels beneath a cross and whispers the tale of his father’s prophetic, impossible insight.
When the story of the old king reaches Fu Yuan, he is struck by a sorrow so profound he touches no food for a day.

“It is a great pity I was not given to meet such a sage,” he says. “He saw not our power, but our capacity for becoming. He looked not upon our present, but upon our future. An insight that perceives the essence of things, a wisdom that grasps the inevitable flow of fate... how many such men are there in this world?”

“The truly strong must forever transcend their own limitations. Yet, the higher a man climbs, the more he is shackled by the weight of his own triumphs. Then, the most formidable cage is no longer of the world; it is of the self. But if a man can stand upon the highest peak with the heart of a child, and walk through the deepest valley with an unbound spirit — tell me, what in all of creation could ever limit him?”
Fu Yuan raises his eyes to the distant silhouette of Mount Ararat. The sky is clear, the sun a divine fire on the snow. High above, a lone eagle wheels in the vastness.
He watches it.
Their gazes meet, across the great expanse of the sky.
The eagle lets loose a single, clarion cry that echoes off the mountains.
And in that instant, Fu Yuan knows.
It is the spirit of the old king.
Chapter 39: The Devil’s Eye
Young Fu Yuan stands on the snow-swept peak. His eyes cut through the clouds, locking onto three black specks high above. The black specks dance in the air, their strange orbits some kind of signal.
He strides back into the cave and tells the Scion, “Three hawks are tracking us. The hunters will be on us in minutes.”

The Scion freezes, his hands still dressing his wounds. “The master of those birds is the most vicious bastard on the steppes. We can’t face him. Not even together. You run for your life. I will stay and meet my end here!”
Fu Yuan points to the horizon. “I feel a current of life in that direction. Don’t be like a girl about it. To horse. Now.”

They tear across the snow for thirty miles. Without warning, the horses scream and rear, throwing the Scion hard into a drift. He lets out a wretched scream as his ribs snap again. No whip, no spur can force the animals a single step further.

“Well, now,” Fu Yuan says, a strange wonder in his voice. “Looks like even the beasts know the danger ahead.” He heaves the Scion onto his shoulders and, ignoring his protests, plunges forward, disappearing into a world of snow and mist.

Mukhtar, the Vulture Master of Khwarazm, stands before his Mongol cavalry, his face a thundercloud. His men, brutes and killers to a man, hold their breath. They stare, stunned, at the scene before them. Above them, the sky is a brilliant, cloudless blue. But a hundred yards ahead, a furious storm rages, a vortex of wind and ice.
The three hawks dive from the sky. They shriek and burrow into Mukhtar’s arms.

“My lord...” The guide, Agula, his voice trembling, points. “That is the Eye of Erlik, the Demon King. When he sought to use his Eye of Darkness to turn the world to eternal night, the sky god Tengri tore out his eye and threw it here.”

He swallows hard. “The Eye stares at the heavens, forever seeking its return. Even starving wolves, when they see prey fall within its bounds, will not cross the line to feed. Everyone on the steppes knows the story of the Jurchen army, thousands strong, that fled into that place. They were never seen again. But on the coldest nights, when the north wind howls, you hear them. Hoofbeats and screams, echoing from the dark. An endless slaughter, replayed for eternity...”

Mukhtar scoffs and walks forward. He doesn’t get far. The hawks claw their way free from his grasp and flee. A chorus of ghostly wails fills his ears. He looks back. His men are frozen in place, dozens of yards behind him. Not one has moved an inch.
He sighs, and stops.

Fu Yuan carries the Scion deeper into the earth. The wind moans around them, and the darkness feels crowded with ghosts. The Scion struggles on his back. “This place is twisted. I’d rather go back and fight it out than die in here and never be reborn a man again.”
Fu Yuan sneers. “And what makes you so certain this place is unholy?”
“Nothing grows in this goddamn place!” the Scion spits. “The walls bleed, the earth groans, and there’s not a damn bit of gree-gree in the whole place!”
Fu Yuan scoffs. “You people always confuse the surface with the soul. You see a dense forest and call it an embodiment of ‘good.’ You see a desolate desert and call it a symbol of ‘evil.’”

“There is no good or evil. There is only Order and Disorder. What you call good and evil are just tools your minds use to perceive these states. Your senses are limited. You see the surface darkness and silence here, and you assume that it must not be good — that it must be evil —“
The Scion starts to argue, but the ghostly wailing in his ears suddenly stops. Around them, there is still no sign of life, yet the space is filled with a transcendent calm.
The Scion begins to struggle again.
“What is it now!” Fu Yuan snaps.
“My ribs!” the Scion yells. “They don’t hurt no more.”
Somehow, the wound has healed itself.

A realization dawns on Fu Yuan. “This place is not dead. It is pure Order. The surface of the world, for all its life, is a battlefield of constant consumption. Here, far from that chaos, life finds its peace. Your body healed because the Order here allowed its own vital force to work, uninterrupted.”
He looks down. At the bottom of the darkness, a faint red glow pulses, the slow beat of magma, a secret summons from the heart of the world.
Meanwhile on the plains, the pursuit has fallen back ten miles from the Devil’s Eye. The men make camp. Only Mukhtar stands alone in the snow, his expression unreadable.
Agula approaches him. “Vulture Master, they have entered the Devil’s Eye. They cannot survive. Why are you still troubled?”
Mukhtar’s voice is a low growl. “They are alive. I can feel it.”
Then, his face contorts in shock.
“My lord?”
“Strange,” Mukhtar rasps. “It’s as if... they’ve vanished from the world.”
The Scion’s heavy breathing echoes in the deep dark.
Fu Yuan’s voice drifts back, alive with excitement. “If we must speak of good and evil, then what lies ahead of us must be the legendary land of ‘Perfect Good’.”
“Brother,” the Scion calls out. “If we just stayed here, you think we’d live forever?”
Fu Yuan snorts. He says nothing more.
The two men’s footsteps fade into the darkness.
Chapter 40: The Peach Blossom Vale

A hot summer day in 5th century CE China. The man who will one day be known as the great poet Tao Yuanming sits in his quiet country home. He has been a magistrate for eighty days. It is eighty days too long. The taste of court wine, once a symbol of ambition, has soured on his tongue. He has seen the grinding machinery of the state, and it has no room for a soul. Outside his gate, the world groans; the roads are a river of refugees flowing toward no destination.
A fisherman from the village arrives, his basket holding silver carp still gleaming with river water. The man vanished weeks ago; the village had assumed him drowned.
“Master Tao,” the fisherman says. His eyes hold a strange, new light. “A gift. The river was generous today.”
Tao Yuanming gestures for him to sit. “Where have you been, old friend? We thought the spirits of the water had claimed you.”

The man leans in with a conspiratorial whisper. He tells a story. Of being lost, of rowing into a current he did not know, of coming upon a forest of peach blossoms that bloomed like a living cloud. He speaks of a passage into a hidden world where the people knew nothing of the dynasty, nothing of the chaos, nothing of the endless wars. A place where the fields were rich, the homes in perfect order, and the faces of the old and young alike shone with a deep and simple joy.
When he finishes, Tao Yuanming laughs. A dry, bitter rasp.
“A fine story for a tavern, my friend. A pleasant dream to have while drunk on cheap wine.”
The fisherman’s face flushes. “It is no dream!” his voice cracks. “I saw it with my own eyes! I swear it! I even told the magistrate in Wuling. I told him everything!”
The laughter dies in Tao Yuanming’s throat.

“You what?” he rages. “You fool! You have no idea what you’ve done. If such a place truly exists — if, by some miracle, one pure corner of this rotten world has survived — you were bound by a sacred duty to protect it! To keep your silence! You haven’t discovered a utopia; you’ve condemned it. You have destroyed the single most valuable thing left in this blighted land!”

The fisherman is stunned by the poet’s passion. “Master Tao, do not be angry,” he says. “The heavens took care of my foolishness. The magistrate sent his men, it is true, but they found nothing. A great storm came last week. The river flooded its banks. The marks I made are gone, washed away. The path is lost. Not even I could find it again.”
A long silence falls. Tao Yuanming looks out the window. The world beyond his gate is weary. The anger drains from him, replaced by a profound, aching melancholy.
The fisherman watches him. A question is in his eyes. “Master Tao... you speak of it with such reverence. Do you not wish to see this place for yourself?”

Tao Yuanming turns from the window, a faint, sad smile on his lips. He looks at the simple fisherman, and in that moment, he sees the entire human condition — the yearning for a paradise we are destined to lose.
“Some places, old friend,” he says softly, “are better kept alive in the heart, not sought by the foot.”

Fu Yuan and the Scion shake off their pursuers, only to stray into uncharted mountains. The mountains run deep; the valleys grow quieter the deeper they go. Then, all at once, they break into a sea of peach blossoms. Astonished, they click their tongues in wonder.

They wade through this “sea” for several miles before a village comes into view. Thin cooking smoke curls up. Chickens and dogs answer each other. The villagers are neatly dressed, all of them smiling — mild, courteous, and alike.

An elder explains: “We have lived here for generations and know nothing of outside affairs. In the Qin Dynasty, our forebears fled war and reached this place. It stood far from turmoil, so they took root. None go out, none come in. We do not fight or seize. All farm and read. We live in harmony and ease.”

The Scion is ecstatic. “Could this be the fabled Peach-Blossom Vale? To think we’d land in the Vale itself while fleeing for our lives! Isn’t this Heaven’s will — that yours-truly is destined to be emperor, to turn the whole world into this?”
Fu Yuan gives a wry chuckle.
The elder smiles. “Since you’ve come, you’re family. Follow the ancestral precepts, keep to the rules, and stay for as long as your heart desires.”
A bell sounds. The elder bows. “The hall calls a convocation.” He pauses. “If you care to see, you’re welcome.”
Before the Hall of Common Will. A weather-stained plaque hangs above the door:
ACT AS ONE MIND.
LIVE AS ONE WILL.
PURGE THE SELF’S DISSENT.
OBEY THE GROUP’S CONSENT.
They look closely at the inscription and stand awestruck — it dates from a thousand years ago.
Inside, the air is solemn. People sit in a ring, each holding bamboo slips. An old man rises. “Today’s matter: Zhang San refuses to swap his field with Li Si. Is this against the Way of Harmony?”
All answer together: “It is.”

Zhang San kneels in the center, face ashen. One after another they step forward to counsel him. “Harmony values self-surrender. If you give up yourself, there is no strife.” When they finish, they ask if he has understood.
Zhang San bows to the floor. “I understand! I understand!” He rises wearing the same smile as the rest.
The Scion sighs in praise. “The legend of the Vale is true to the last word.”
Fu Yuan watches Zhang San’s hands tremble. His thumbs gouge his own palms; a thin line of blood seeps out.

That night, a woman’s crying drifts from afar. They follow the sound and find a woman standing beneath a peach tree, weeping. Asked why, she says: “I am drawn by nature to painting. But the ancestral precept says: ‘If one person paints, then all must paint; if one does not paint, then none may paint.’ I love painting, yet I must put it aside lest I disturb harmony.”
Fu Yuan asks, “Why not leave?”

She shakes her head. “I once painted a scroll — Peach Blossoms on Flowing Water — and someone discovered it. They say I have a deviant heart. They counseled me day and night until I burned the work and confessed my error. Since then I often dream of the blossoms from that painting — they can ride the spring and drift far, far away, while I cannot.”
A night breeze passes through the trees. Millions of peach petals, white and pink, fall from the black sky like snow.

Her face dims. “Here the peach blossoms look the same year after year and never wither. While the ones in my painting scatter and drift with the wind, refusing to stay. The elder says I’m not painting it correctly — if the flowers are gone with the wind, what’s left for us to hold on to spring?”
Next day, the hall gathers again. “Today’s matter: how to deal with the woman who cried last night?”
The Scion jolts back — Fu Yuan is already lashing out: “This so-called harmony in your book — it’s nothing but the tyranny of the many, the strong devouring the weak!”
All reply with one voice: “How mistaken you are! This commune always acts by the common will. Thus is true fairness and goodness.”
Fu Yuan lets out a cutting laugh. “Rubbish! Even your peach trees differ — that difference makes the grove. If God made all trees alike, where would this spectacle of your Vale come from?”

His eyes sweep the stunned faces. “You say ‘Act as one mind. Live as one will’ is the highest good — You don’t know it’s a desecration of life! Life is worth living because of one’s free will, yet you let it be crushed by the ‘many,’ and serve as its willing accomplices.”
“How dare you call this paradise? It is a cage built to strangle the life out of life itself!”
But the woman already kneels in the hall and confesses: “I had a moment of folly and forgot the ancestral precepts. From this day, I will remember.” She rises with the same smile as the rest.
The elder glares at the two and snaps his sleeve. “Leave at once!”
The Scion is baffled. “How could our pursuers ever find this place?”

The elder’s face darkens. “What you bring is worse than pursuers. For a thousand years we have endured by the collective, not the self. You have sown the seed of the ‘I’ in people’s hearts — that is the true calamity.”
Fu Yuan lets out a furious laugh. “So much for your so-called Peach-Blossom Vale. I see you for what you are — a cage the fools built for themselves!”

The Scion catches a cold glint flashing in Fu Yuan’s eyes, as if about to strike. Alarmed, he grabs him and pulls him away. The crowd follows close behind, seeing them off all the way to the mouth of the valley.
When the crowd finally disperses, the Scion grumbles, “Ain’t this great — haven’t even had breakfast, and we’re back on the run.”

He mutters, “The Peach-Blossom Vale... what a nightmare. If the whole world were like this, I’d rather not have it. Still, those who sleep — to wake them like this, is it truly a blessing? Or a disaster?”
Fu Yuan seems a little deflated, his head lowered as he says nothing.
Just as they are about to set out, the woman rushes over, slips something into Fu Yuan’s hand without a word, and turns away.
Fu Yuan unrolls it — a painting scroll.
They look together. Within: peach blossoms swirling everywhere, some drifting off with the wind, some just bursting into bloom — no two the same.
In the midst of that rain of petals stands a woman, smiling in grace and light, spirit alive and leaping from the silk — vivid, radiant, almost breathing.

Just then, a breeze passes through the valley. The scroll ripples like water. In the painting, the blossoms whirl into motion, and the woman within, her sash fluttering with the wind, seems as if she might burst through the paper at any moment.
The Scion exclaims, “Of all things — the woman in the painting looks real, and the real one we met somehow don’t!”
Fu Yuan nods slowly, his eyes fill with quiet melancholy.
“So this is her true self. What we saw back there was but a shadow of that self, held captive by the will of the many.”
Chapter 41: The King of Lingzhou
Time glides past. The boy once known as the “Little Prince” has grown to manhood, now crowned King of Lingzhou.
In only a few short years, his realm transforms into a land of harmony: trade thrives, learning blooms, tribes live as neighbors in peace, and society breathes free.
He builds a system of balance — court, nobles, commoners — three powers holding one another in check, a design without precedent in history.
Voices rise in praise:
“If all the realm were as Lingzhou, the Great Yuan would stand for ten thousand years!”
But the Khan is faintly ill at ease.
Fu Yuan explains, “This boy has always been clever, but too naïve. His governing philosophy is built on an over-beautified, idealized view of human nature.”
“His tripartite balance? A castle in the sand. Let one skilled at stirring hearts appear, and it will collapse.”
The Khan slams the table and roars. “Damn right! The last thing I’d rely on in this world is man’s nature!”
Soon enough, as if on cue, a man arrives in Lingzhou calling himself the Poet.
A wealthy foreign magnate, bold in manner and reckless of speech. He calls himself a “true scoundrel,” and the streets adore him for it.

Lately he has raised a banner: MAKE LINGZHOU GREAT AGAIN, and quickly draws a great host of fervent followers. Red headscarves wound round their heads, they swagger through the markets; no one dares cross them.
The Poet’s methods of incitement are simple but effective.

He disregards fact, speaks without restraint, turns complex social problems into blunt slogans, pins every grievance on a single group, and throws out simple, extreme cures — misleading the crowd into believing they have found an easy path to change.
As he keeps fanning the crowd, public passions rise by the day.
In less than two months, Lingzhou plunges into turmoil — riven by ethnic division and class strife.
By such means, the Poet’s followers multiply by the day, and he soon stands as the most powerful man in the city.
Even the King’s own officials, most of them, turn to him in secret. Thus the three chambers, once held in balance, become his accomplices.
The King burns with the urge to kill the man whenever he thinks of him, yet he cannot bring himself to commit such an inhumane act.
Night after night, he wakes drenched in sweat, staring into the dark.
That day, word comes suddenly: Fu Yuan seeks audience.
The King greets him as if seeing a savior, throws himself to the ground, crawling forward and crying out, “Master, save me!”
Fu Yuan gives a cold laugh.
“Do you know you have done wrong?”
The King knocks his head upon the floor again and again. “This child knows his fault, but does not know where it lies.”
Fu Yuan asks, “’Water can carry the boat and can overturn it as well’ — explain.”
The King recites like a schoolboy:
“Mencius said, The people are weighty, the state comes next, the ruler is light. Guanzi too: A ruler’s rise lies in following the people’s heart; his fall, in defying it...”
“Nonsense!” Fu Yuan snaps, cutting him off. “Those pedants scratch at the shell and call it wisdom.”
“Water carries and overturns because of what it is. Don’t know water, can’t sail. Don’t know people, can’t rule.”
“Do you understand? Your glorification of human nature is the most foolish thing a man can do.
Reason, morality, creativity — these aren’t its essence, only its chance. Man chases profit and flees pain; he bows to power and tramples the weak; he’s ruled by emotion, swept along by the crowd.
Civilization is no gift of human nature — it is its taming, its remaking, its transcendence.
Institutions must be built on the grain of human nature — above all, on its stupidity and its evil when men move as one.
Remember: in man, the power to destroy is greater by far than the power to create.”

The King closes his eyes in anguish. “Master, this child has been utterly foolish. But that Poet — how can he be dealt with? The people’s minds are clouded; it seems that reason can no longer reach them.”
Fu Yuan’s voice is cold. “He who tied the knot must untie it.”
The King’s pupils tighten. “Master... you mean to say — kill him?!”
Fu Yuan scoffs. “I said deal with the devil, not become him.”
As he speaks, Fu Yuan turns his back, wipes a hand across his face, and turns again.
The King staggers back and drops to the floor.
Before him stands Fu Yuan wearing a flawless mask of human skin. The face? None other than the Poet himself.
Fu Yuan laughs his heart out. “Little Prince, do you concede?”
The King’s face blanches; his lips tremble.
Fu Yuan’s gaze cuts through the night, his voice low and implacable.
“This time you’ve lost but a tiny Lingzhou. If you ever wear the crown and remain this naïve — what you will lose then is the realm itself!”
Chapter 42: The Question

A sharp night wind rises, and the steppe answers with ten thousand bonfires — a galaxy thrown across the grass. All of this is the Scion’s army. From this moment on, the great steppe holds no more enemies for him.
Inside the command tent, the Khatun kneels before the Scion, her hair in disarray. Not far behind her, her own son, Khurel, lies still on the ground.
“By decree of His Merciful Great Khan:
The Khatun and her son are condemned for crimes against the tribe, for the wolf-hearted betrayal of their loving master.
Yet His Merciful Great Khan cannot bear to kill the mother.
She is sent to the Valley of Cold Springs to repent.
For the rest of her days, she shall not leave.”

The Khatun bows her head to the ground. “Thank you, Great Khan, for your mercy.” She rises and, ignoring the guards’ shouts, walks toward the Scion. “I only want to look at the Great Khan one more time... He is my son, too.”
The Scion waves a hand. “Let her pass.”
The Khatun draws near, her gaze a deep and unreadable well. The Scion’s voice is low. “Mother...”
Her eyes fly open. She hawks and spits in his face. Then she spins, throws herself on Khurel’s body, rips the dagger from his belt, and drives it into her heart.
His pupils shrink.
He recognizes that dagger.
A boy’s voice rings beside his ear: “Brother, this is from you. I’ll never lose it.”
Slowly, the Scion walks to the corpses of the Khatun and his kid brother, kneels, stares for a long, silent moment, and then wails.
He sleeps till nightfall the next day. Upon waking, he immediately seeks out Fu Yuan.
He clutches his head in pain. “Tell me — What is the meaning of life?”
Fu Yuan is facing away from him, poking at a bonfire. “You mean to say you won. You survived. Yet you’re not happy.”
The Scion frowns. “What does that have to do with my question?”
Fu Yuan still doesn’t turn. “That is your real question.”
“It is not!” The Scion retorts.
Fu Yuan turns to face him. “Then what are you asking? The meaning of life? Tell me — what is ‘meaning’?”
The Scion freezes. After a moment’s thought, he answers, “Meaning is... something that makes all of this worthwhile? Or... something that lets me feel all of this is right... is justifiable...?”
“Don’t give me ‘or.’” Fu Yuan grows impatient. “So. Your question is, ‘What in life is worthwhile?’ Correct?”
The Scion nods, then immediately shakes his head.
“Then your question must be: ‘What gives my life justification?’”
“What I want to ask is —“ The Scion’s brow furrows, his words coming in a rush. “I have everything. Why am I feeling emptier than ever?”
Comprehension seems to dawn on Fu Yuan. “So. Your question isn’t, ‘What is the meaning of life?’ It is — ‘Why do I feel empty?’”
“No!” the Scion blurts out. “I want to ask, ‘What is the meaning of life...’”
Fu Yuan asks again, “Then what is ‘meaning’?”
“You —“ The Scion chokes on a curse, but Fu Yuan’s gaze does not yield an inch.
Moment of silence.
The Scion surrenders. “...Alright. Perhaps my real question is, ‘Was all of this worthwhile?’”
Fu Yuan goes full-on bullish. “So. ‘Worthwhile’ — what does it mean?”
The night wind slaps at the bonfire, its flames flickering like the Scion’s tangled thoughts. He is silent for a while.
“’Worthwhile’... means what I spent brought me the return I wanted?”
“So then,” Fu Yuan presses, “what return do you think you’ve received?”

The Scion’s voice falters. “I’ve got... freedom. I’ve got my own empire. But I’ve lost too much. I lost my little bro. It’s true he wanted me dead, but I once loved him. I lost the man I used to be — the poor bastard who fought just to live. And now I don’t even know if this was what I was fighting for.”
There is no mercy in Fu Yuan’s eyes. “So, your real question is — ‘What do I truly want?’”
The Scion clenches his fists. He’ll hit this bastard if he could.
Then it hits him: Fu Yuan has no intention of answering. The guy’s forcing him to dismantle his own question until there’s nothing left to dismantle.
He takes a deep breath, his voice slightly hoarse. “That is my question, yes. But it wasn’t my first question.”
Fu Yuan chortles. “Do you get it? Your problem is the problem of the problem itself.”
He turns back to poke at that bonfire again.
FWOOM
The flame leaps into air, jolting the Scion.
In that instant, he feels it. The thing that has been haunting him... is gone.
Not the answer to a question, but the dissipation of the question itself.
He could ask:
Why do I feel empty?
He could ask:
What do I truly want?
He could ask:
How do I find a new direction?
He could even ask:
Does life have an ultimate purpose?
But he can no longer ask —
What is the meaning of all this?
Because he finally realizes it was never a real question to begin with.

With his back to the Scion, Fu Yuan’s shoulders tremble slightly. He must be stifling a laugh, as if everything is unfolding exactly as he planned. “You have a pile of questions. Why don’t you come find me tomorrow? I’ll answer them for you, one by one.”
The Scion stares, like a desert traveler who has found an oasis but cannot believe his own eyes.
“Or...” Fu Yuan adds. “You could also choose not to come. You already know the questions. And you have a lifetime to answer them.”

The Scion suddenly finds that he is no longer lost. He is still in pain, still empty, still unsure of the road ahead. But the maddening feeling is gone, like a fierce wind scattering the fog in his mind.

The disappearance of that one question has made the confusion itself vanish, like ice melting to reveal the earth beneath. Fu Yuan gave him no answers. He only made him realize that his problem, from the very beginning, was never a real problem.

The Scion lets out a long breath, as if expelling the weight from his soul. He turns and walks toward his own tent. He knows that he probably will not seek out Fu Yuan tomorrow. Because the first question is no longer a question. And all the questions that remain are his, and his alone.
He has the rest of his life to find their answers.
The night deepens. One by one, the camp’s bonfires go out, and in the sky, the stars become more numerous. It’s as if the fires from the ground have returned to the heavens.
Or perhaps they are the many lives that have been extinguished.
Chapter 43: The Khan’s Glass Orb
Night is black, velvet-soft.
First night in the Persian palace. The Khan and Fu Yuan have never in their lives seen such gold. Such gems.

A night wind breathes through the hall, heavy with agarwood and rose oil. Walls, encrusted with lapis, turquoise, and diamonds, ripple in the blaze of the golden chandeliers; the entire palace dissolves into a blue sea.
But they see none of it — Their eyes fixed on a single glass orb, glowing between them.
“What do you see?” Fu Yuan holds his breath.
“A whole world.” The Khan’s voice is thick with wonder. “Sky. Ocean. Steppe. And cities. People everywhere. Heaven above — do they see me?”
“Let me see.” Fu Yuan snatches the orb.
“Oi! Easy!” the Khan protests — and for once, concern edges his voice. “Do not capsize their world.”
Fu Yuan presses his face to the glass, his voice slipping into a trance. “Strange... it’s as if every life inside it moves through me — their joys, their sorrows, every heartbeat.”
The Khan stares into the dark. Then, without warning, he lifts his fist and flips a middle finger at the sky.
Fu Yuan startles. “Are you mad?”
The Khan is deflated. “Think on it. Yesterday we were conquerors of the earth. Now? We are likely just two men living inside a bigger glass ball. Is it not absurd?”
Neither man sleeps that night.
A month passes. The Khan is still in the palace. Military councils? Canceled. The Western Campaign? Postponed without date. The grand design of conquest has lost all flavor.
Nowhere he wishes to go. Nowhere he dares to go.
He keeps the orb in his hands at all hours — afraid someone will steal it, afraid it will fall and shatter.
“Your Majesty, you must let it go,” a renowned monk pleads. “Attachment is suffering. Allow this humble one to—“
The Khan draws his whip and lashes him clear across the hall.
“Touch my orb and I’ll cut off yours!”
Fu Yuan returns from his travels. The Khan greets him like a drowning man catching breath.
“I’ve been thinking about something strange,” the Khan says, sprawled on a bench, eyes shot with red.
“You know... I’ve never been so afraid of losing anything.”
His voice rasps.

“You know this too. I’ve burned cities, crushed rivers, turned whole kingdoms to ash — never once hesitated to ruin the real world. So why... why am I terrified this tiny little world in the orb might break?”
He shuts his eyes.
“Perhaps... it’s because I’ve never truly owned anything...? Even the world I conquered was never mine. I was only passing through. A plunderer...”
He clutches the orb tighter. “But this... this little world... it’s different. It belongs to me. And me alone...”
Fu Yuan’s eyes gleam.

“You know... the cities and kingdoms you conquered — they were only fragments of ‘existence.’ You could destroy them, yes, but you could never erase ‘existence’ itself. This orb is different — it lets you touch the realm of the gods.”
The Khan stares.

“Out there, you are Great Khan the Destroyer and Conqueror. But before this orb, you become the Creator. You have been entrusted with responsibility for ‘existence’ itself. A grand responsibility — a responsibility that belongs to the gods.”
The Khan groans. “No wonder it hurts so much...”
“No.” Fu Yuan laughs, cold. “You suffer because you cling to the Should.”
“You think you
should
conquer.
Should
expand.
Should
be the invincible Khan.
Should
be the man they see. You burn with the desire of a Creator, yet you dare not step off the track of your life.”
“You are saying...” The Khan’s eyes ignite. “What I should let go of... is the ‘Should’? Not this orb?”
Fu Yuan laughs — wild, fanatic. “Naturally! Is this not more amusing than conquering the world? A challenge of a much higher order!”
“Indeed...” The Khan hesitates. “But... can we do it?”
“Irrelevant!” Fu Yuan snaps.
“What matters is: do you dare? Dare you accept the challenge? Dare you leap from the life you know?
If you do not dare, you are not a conqueror. You are the conquered. You are not a Free Man!”
That night, the two men disappear.
Gone for a full year. The army waits outside the city. No orders come. Rumors bloom. Some say madness took them; others, death.
What happened in that year? No one truly knows.
A thousand years later, rumor ripens into myth, carried through nations and cultures. In those myths, they hunt for a sanctuary for the orb.
They climb the summit of the gods.
They dive into sunken worlds beneath the sea.
Tribes in the frozen wastes and the hidden jungles keep tales of them still.
One thing is certain — No one in this world knows where the glass orb lies.
That secret belongs only to the two men — a secret barred even from the divine.
Chapter 44: The India Box
Grand Scholar Zhao Wujiu had been framed by Crown Prince Zheng; without Fu Yuan stepping in, he would have died wronged in a cell.
No sooner was he released than the Crown Prince, as if on a whim, put his name forward for a coveted post: Imperial Censor.

Zhao prided himself on being unruffled, yet when he met the Prince in person, even he couldn’t help stiffening. The Prince, for his part, behaved as though nothing at all had passed between them — took Zhao’s hand, asked question after question, the very image of an old friend reunited after years apart.

When they parted, Zhao felt the weight settle. He drifted through the streets in a daze, so lost in thought he walked right past his own gate and only came to himself before the Grand Advisor’s residence.

“Grand Advisor, I find myself in a grave dilemma.” His brow tightens. “In the corruption case against Minister of Revenue Liu, the Crown Prince speaks no word — yet his intent is unmistakable. Should I investigate to the end, I will surely incur his displeasure and betray the ‘patronage’ he has shown. But if I allow the law to bend, how am I to quiet my own conscience?”

He lets out a faint, self-mocking smile. “Only days ago I stood before you boasting that I would ride the wind and become a true Free Man. And now I am mired in this quandary, unable to advance or retreat. It is enough to make one laugh.”
Fu Yuan, amused, listens without interruption. When Zhao finishes, he does not answer. Instead, he walks to the shelves, takes down an exquisite wooden box, and sets it on the table.
“This box comes from India. It is truly one of a kind. Legend says it holds six layers, each nested within the last — a pure work of genius. Yet no ordinary force can open it.”
Zhao examines it. No seam, no hinge. He tries the lid; it does not budge.
Fu Yuan smiles. “The box has a mind for riddles. Speak your trouble aloud. If your reasoning pleases it, it will open.”
Zhao’s eyes widen. “How is that even possible?”
“Try it and see.” Fu Yuan centers the box between them. “Tell it what troubles you.”
Zhao Wujiu takes a deep breath. “I, Zhao Wujiu, now face a hard choice. My side of reason wishes to uphold principle; my side of sentiment wishes to yield to the Crown Prince.”
The box does not change.
Fu Yuan studies him. “That is but your dilemma on the surface — much like this box before you that will not open. Let us look deeper. What truly stands behind this matter?”
Zhao Wujiu pauses, then gathers his thoughts.

“I see the court shifting. The Crown Prince and the Empress are locked in a struggle for power. This case cuts straight to the Prince’s authority; if Minister Liu falls, the Empress’s faction will seize the chance to strike. I may be small in the order of things, yet the scales tip at my touch.”
Just as the last word leaves his mouth — there comes a soft, sudden
KADA
. The outermost layer of the box eases open by a hair.
Zhao Wujiu is dumbstruck. “This... how?”
Fu Yuan is unfazed.

“By looking past the surface, you saw the court in transformation — and the power struggle driving that change. The rules of politics resemble the box’s outer layer: they seem fixed, immovable, yet they are shaped by the forces beneath them.”

“The feud between the Crown Prince and the Empress is but one move in the larger contest of power, and that contest is itself bound by still greater constraints. Grasp this, and only then can you take the initiative within the game.”
He gives Zhao Wujiu a slight indication. “You’ve opened one layer; why not go one layer deeper?”
Zhao Wujiu is still half in doubt, but curiosity drives him on. His gaze deepens.

“I see the fate of Great Yuan. The Crown Prince is cruel and ungrateful; his ambition large, his talent thin. He has cunning enough, but no wisdom. Yet the arrogant fools gathered around the Empress are worse. Should her faction seize power — should they even cast the Prince aside — the decline of Great Yuan will only quicken, and thus this case concerns the very destiny of the state.”
A faint click sounds. The second layer loosens. Zhao lifts it with care and finds another box within.
Fu Yuan nods slightly.

“You have moved beyond your personal stakes — and now you see the arteries of the state. The struggle between the Crown Prince and the Empress is not merely a matter of succession; it concerns the rise and fall of Great Yuan as a whole. An ordinary official sees only the grievance before him. You, on the other hand, see how these decisions will shape the empire’s fate.”

“This is exactly the second realm of contemplation: lifting your mind from your own predicament to the whole of society — seeing long consequences within an immediate choice. You are no longer just a pawn at court; you have begun to understand the direction of the entire game.”
“Continue.”
Zhao Wujiu looks out the window. The rain has stopped. Distant mountains surface and fade through the thinning mist.

“I see how this touches the lives of the people. For the powerful, a change of regime is a game of moves. For the common folk, it is calamity. Whether the Crown Prince or the Empress prevails — if power cannot pass in peace, unrest will sweep the land, and I fear the suffering borne by the people then will far exceed this small corruption before us.”
A soft click follows his words. The third layer opens. Zhao Wujiu shows no surprise — his mind is fixed on the depth of the thought itself.

Fu Yuan’s voice deepens. “This is the dialectic of freedom and order. True freedom is not doing as one wills, but understanding how each choice impacts the freedom of others. If all men pursue absolute freedom without regard for rules, the result is a greater un-freedom for all. Rules are the foundation that allows freedom to coexist within a limited space.”

Zhao Wujiu nods. “I see the long river of history. The rise and fall of Great Yuan is only a brief surge within it. In its rise, the people can breathe and mend their lives. In its decline, the living are cast into ruin. If that decline can be delayed — even by a year, even by half a year — it is an act of mercy for all beneath heaven.”
The fourth layer of the box opens.
Fu Yuan nods.

“Men lose their freedom because they cling to the fortunes of the present hour. Their sight cannot pierce the mists of their age. A true mind sees both the contest on the board before him and the celestial signs that whisper the direction of an age — he plays the game at hand, yet is not imprisoned by its passing wins or losses.”
He gestures for Zhao to continue. “You are close to the heart of it. Go deeper — what do you see?”
Zhao Wujiu stares at the last, small box. A sudden clarity breaks over him.
“I see... myself. All these layers return, in the end, to my own choice. I am at once a chess piece and a chess player. I can be hemmed in by circumstance — or carve out possibility within it.”
The fifth layer opens, revealing the innermost box.

“I see the boundaries of thought itself. All this time I have been confined by the established frame — loyalty to the Crown Prince or loyalty to the law; my own safety or the rules of the court; political survival or moral integrity.”

“These choices may look like opposites. In truth, they are all concepts defined by others. Freedom does not lie in selecting from a set of prepared answers. It lies in breaking the definitions themselves and opening possibilities no one has foreseen.”
A rising thrill takes hold of him.

“I need not become the figure anyone expects, nor tread any path already laid down. I can stand at a higher vantage, unbound by loyalty, duty, or morality, all of them borrowed terms, and free to redefine the situation by my own will — to craft a solution no one before had conceived.”
With a final soft click, the sixth box opens. Inside is nothing.
“Emptiness,” Fu Yuan says, “is not void, but boundless possibility — the condition of complete freedom.”

“Each layer you shed was not discarded, but freed. With every veil lifted, your mind widened; your understanding grew broader. You are no longer like ordinary men, trapped on a single plane, unable to see more no matter how they strain.”
Zhao Wujiu is moved at once.

“So this is the virtue of becoming a ‘Free Man.’ I once thought I had only two choices — either this or that. I never imagined how many layers lay beneath, each one unfolding more possibilities than the last. When all these layers are considered together, the choices multiply. Seeing this empty realm of ‘freedom’... it truly clears the spirit.”
Just as he speaks, there is a crisp
DA
A still smaller box drops, like sleight of hand, from Fu Yuan’s palm into the once-empty box.
Fu Yuan bursts into laughter.
“Empty — and then not empty!”
Zhao Wujiu is struck speechless. “This...”
Fu Yuan barks the words:
“For there is freedom — there is creation!”

A sudden illumination breaks over Zhao Wujiu; his eyes flare with light. “This student understands at last — utterly. This is true freedom! Not only may I dance with the order already in place — I may fashion new order, and from it, new possibilities.”
He bows deeply. “This student takes his leave.”
Then, unable to help himself, he adds with a touch of awkwardness: “Master... might you grant me this box? It may aid my thinking in days to come.”
Fu Yuan smiles faintly, an unreadable glint in his eyes. “Since fate binds the two of you, I will give it to you.”
Zhao Wujiu receives it with delight and bows deeply once more. “My thanks for Master’s guidance. As for this case — I now have my course.”
Three days later, Zhao Wujiu sits alone in his study when another difficult matter rises unbidden to mind.
He suddenly recalls the uncanny wooden box, takes it out, and sets it upon the desk, intending to borrow its “spirit” once again.

To his surprise, before a single thought has formed, the box has already opened. He lifts the next layer; it yields at once. Layer by layer he goes, each one opening without the least resistance, until all seven lie bare.
Zhao Wujiu examines them closely, turning each over in his hands. They are only wooden boxes — exquisite, yes — but bearing no hidden springs, no cleverly concealed mechanisms.
Then he remembers the inscrutable smile on Fu Yuan’s face when he handed it over, and in that moment understands: he has been toyed with by the Grand Advisor.
He looks at the boxes strewn across the table — and bursts into helpless laughter, laughing so hard until tears spill from his eyes.
Chapter 45: Ballad of the Pipa
Jiang Nan. Late spring.
For a thousand years, the name has meant beauty at its most fragile, romance at its most poignant — a bittersweet longing cherished by lovers and scholars alike.
And so, on a barge, the Four Gifted have gathered. They raise their cups, but the celebration feels hollow. A river gathering, absent of music.
The hollow is broken by the clear, resonant notes of a pipa, drifting from a painted boat nearby. A wave of joy — they quickly pole their barge closer, calling out an invitation.
She does not make them beg or plead. Nor does she hide her face, coyly, behind the instrument. Her features are obscured only by a thin silk veil, yet she carries herself with a natural poise.
Their eyes fall upon her exquisite hands turning the pegs, plucking the strings. A few stray notes of tuning — and already, a flush rises to the faces of the Four, their souls held captive.
The woman behind the veil caresses the strings, speaking with a voice smooth as silk:
“What man first looked upon — this moon?
And in what age did this moon first — shine upon man?”
“You gentlemen are young. Do you happen to, in your hearts, hold someone you could never forget? Or a story from so long ago you can barely remember?”
The scholars blush. “We are still, in truth...
forcing sorrow for the sake of a new verse
The woman chuckles. “This moon is too bright for singing. But I have a story — real or not — that suits the moonlight. Would you gentlemen care to hear it?”
Everyone nods and claps.
She plucks a drifting intro on the pipa, and her voice settles into a storyteller’s cadence:

On a night as bright as this one, a rich merchant from the north, known only as Nomad, met a woman named Jade in a house of song. Their souls recognized one another; their hearts became entwined. Nomad lingered for months in Jiang Nan, forgetting to return, unable to bring himself to leave.
But this Nomad, in truth, was the Great Khan in disguise. And the court, because of this, was in an uproar.
Some said it was a waste: “The Great Khan is the Son of Heaven — how could he fall for a courtesan!”
Some were in anguish: “The emperor is so indulgent with pleasure, the state is in peril!”
Others offered solutions: “Your Majesty, why not bring the woman to the palace? That way, you could remain in constant company, and the affairs of state would not be delayed.”
One man even tried to win Jade’s favor for himself. The Great Khan found out and tossed him to the fish.
Only the Grand Advisor, hearing of it, offered no comment. The ministers pressed him for his counsel. Only then did he speak, slowly.
“Do you not see that the Great Khan is living in a unique ‘present’?”
The ministers looked at each other, baffled.

The Grand Advisor’s gaze was deep. “A ‘present’ is not a time. It’s a fusion: of character, of the other, of circumstance, of the spirit. He loved Jade as the ‘Nomad,’ not as the Great Khan. And in doing so, he experienced love no ordinary emperor could. His ‘present’ is not weighed down by scepter and crown. It’s the embrace of two weightless and nameless souls.”

“Taking her to the palace, you change not just space, but how they truly exist — which would surely distort their bonds and wrack his experience from this one-of-a-kind ‘present.’ What His Majesty is protecting is exactly this state of being — the very integrity of his ‘present.’ And you fools... you are precisely trying to ruin it!”
None of them had ever considered such a thing. Those who could not take it in looked away with open disdain; those who did found themselves unsettled, a faint dread rising in their chests.
“You are trapped,” the Grand Advisor said, “inside notions and calculations. How could you possibly grasp His Majesty’s realm? What he seeks is the highest form of life:

to exist wholly in the present, to feel every dimension of the now — unbound by the identity of yesterday, or dragged by the duties of tomorrow. You name it indulgence. I tell you — it is insight. It is his defense of the essence of being.”
The Great Khan, hearing this, was moved. “Who in this world truly knows me? Other than my Grand Advisor... None.”
That night, under bright moonlight, the Great Khan’s face was serene, his spirit resting in a vast, quiet peace. He speaks to the Grand Advisor.
“I do not want to be emperor anymore. From this moment on, I am Nomad, and Nomad only.”
The Grand Advisor said nothing.
“Do you know,” the Great Khan says, his voice tender, “she never asks where I came from, nor does she speak of the future.”
A trace of melancholy clouds his gaze.
“I feel she sees through everything, but she never peels the veil. She never asks if I will leave. She never demands that I stay.
Coarse as I am, she treats me as if I were the gentle breeze, the shining moon.”
The Grand Advisor smiles. “Perhaps she does know. Only that she does not cling.”
“But,” he continues, “have you considered: if you are no longer the Emperor... is this ‘present’ still the ‘present’ you are so enamored with?”
The Great Khan considers this. “...It seems not.”
The Grand Advisor sighs. “Your wishing to hold onto the ‘present’ is like turning water into ice. Once it loses its flow, is water still water? Is the ‘present’ still the ‘present’?”
The Great Khan visibly recoils, fixing his gaze on the Grand Advisor.

“The absurdity of the heart of man is the pursuit of an illusory eternity.” The Grand Advisor’s voice turned cold. “It is the ‘present’ that you treasure, yet you would kill its soul — all for its shell.”
The Great Khan fell silent for a moment, then sighed. “I concede. But this guy wants to enjoy this present for as long as he could! Should he do nothing, just to quietly keep everything as it is?”
The Grand Advisor smiles. “My lord — would you hold your breath when you smell a flower, just to withhold its fragrance?”
The Great Khan was caught off guard, then had to laugh.
The Grand Advisor said no more. He looked out at the moon rising over the river.
It is just as the poem says:
“I know not for whom — the moon awaits at the river’s end,
But see the great river waving — its waters away.”
The pipa suddenly falls silent.
Still lost in the spell, a rush of questions comes from the Four Gifted: “What then? What did the Khan decide? Who was Jade? And you, my lady — what is she to you?”
The moonlight is so bright now that even through the silk, they can see the curve of the woman’s smile.
“Was the story beautiful?” she asks.
“Exquisite!” The Four answer as one, though whether for the tale or the teller, none could say.

Her laughing voice drifts back to them as her barge, without a sound, begins to widen the water between them. “As you are men of letters, you surely grasp the story’s heart. Why, then, do you cling to an ending?”
The lead scholar bows, humbled. “We are taught a lesson. But now that we part... is there any hope we might meet again?”
Her barge is a distant shape now, but her voice returns, carried by the wind.
“The Now is all the eternity you will ever have. And eternity is only ever Now.”
The Four Gifted stare at one another. A long time passes before they turn to the empty river, each feeling as if he has just awoken from a shared dream.
Chapter 46: Easter
Gulls chase a vast fleet across the sea.

This is no ordinary fleet. It carries a mission without precedent: Fu Yuan, the legendary Grand Advisor who once bestrode Eurasia and struck fear into the kingdoms of the West, now travels as the Great Khan’s special envoy. He is on his way to Europe once more, accompanied by Friar William of Rubruck and the Wooden Man.
A storm forces them to change course, and the fleet takes shelter at a deserted island.

The shore party moves inland to survey the terrain. They find a troop of monkeys, dressed in scraps of clothing and jewelry, darting through the trees. Stranger still, the creatures have organized themselves by the lavishness of their adornments. A few, dripping with pearls and gems, strut with arrogance. The others grovel and bow before them.
“They order the other monkeys to gather food, but do no labor themselves,” the Wooden Man observes. “A society of nobles and slaves.”
Suddenly, a choked scream cuts through the air. It is Friar William, down by the shore.

They run toward the sound and find him pinned to the sand. A great ape, a golden crown on its head, has its hands wrapped around the monk’s throat. The lesser monkeys screech, cheering it on. The ape’s strength is immense; a half-dozen sailors cannot pull it off.
Fu Yuan flicks his sleeve.
WOOSH
The air itself seems to become a solid fist, slamming into the ape and sending it tumbling to the ground. The sailors fall upon it, tying its limbs, and the monkey horde scatters into the forest.
Friar William staggers to his feet, his face a mask of terror. “Mother of God... that’s a man.”

The creature is caked in filth, his skin cracked like sun-baked earth. His eyes hold a terrifying light, the last embers of reason fighting against a storm of madness. Pearl necklaces hang from his neck, and the golden crown glitters atop his matted hair. Searching him, they find a logbook wrapped in waterproof leather.

The log belongs to a man named Alexander. He was once an officer in the Byzantine navy, later a pirate. On his last voyage, he had intercepted a shipment of tribute intended for the Great Khan. He seized the treasure, but a storm claimed his ship and crew, leaving him the sole survivor on this island.
Fu Yuan opens the log to a page.
Day Three After the Wreck

Lord, what a strange fate you have arranged. The jewels have washed ashore, a miracle. My log, my tools, even food and fresh water. I have to laugh. In Constantinople, this treasure would buy me a life of glory. Here, it is nothing but useless stones and bits of iron. How wise you are, Lord, to give me wealth, only to strip it of all value. This, perhaps, is the penance for my sins.
Friar William sighs. “Praise be to God. All the wealth of man is but dust in His eyes.”
Fu Yuan nods. “Value is what man gives a thing. Change the context, and the value disappears. Continue. Let us see how his fate unfolds.”
Day Fifteen

The monkeys have been watching me for days. They have a near-manic obsession with shiny things. I held out a necklace. In exchange, one of them brought me fresh fruit from the treetops. I swear, when it put the necklace on, it puffed out its chest with all the pride of a fat nobleman in Constantinople.
Day Thirty

There is nothing they will not do for these jewels. I pointed at an empty-handed monkey and commanded my favored ones to beat it. They actually did it. The poor bastard crawled to me, pleading. I showed it mercy and tossed it a single pearl. It was overcome with gratitude...
Day Thirty-Five

I have placed the crown upon my head, a crown that puts the sun to shame, and the monkeys bow at my feet. I mimic their howls, screeching at the sky, and it drives them into a frenzy of deafening shrieks. Who am I? For a moment, I am lost. But in the next moment, the memory of the man who was a pirate is drowned in the roaring tide of their adoration. From this moment on, I am the Monkey King — Alexander the First!
Friar William makes the sign of the cross. “I cannot believe it. He used these worthless jewels to create an entire social order. Is this a miracle of God, or a trick of the devil?”
The Wooden Man is deep in thought. “What is more astonishing is the stability of the system. They have completely adapted to the hierarchy, as if inequality were their natural state.”
Fu Yuan looks toward the distant monkeys, a cold smile on his lips.

“They accepted the meaning Alexander created, and so they forgot the life they once knew. Is human society any different? Its orders, its political systems — do we think them natural, ordained? They are nothing but the will of a few, carved into the many.”
“The tragedy of the ruled is not the oppression. It is that they forget they are oppressed at all. If a god looks down on mankind, his thoughts at this moment must be the same as our own.”
Friar William frowns. “Saint Augustine teaches that the worldly order is a reflection of a divine plan, not a human invention. Are you suggesting the hierarchies of our world are not God’s will?”
Fu Yuan smiles and straightens the monk’s disordered collar. “Perhaps God works through the hands of men?”

The island of monkeys — a perfect, primitive microcosm of human society and politics — fascinates Fu Yuan. He orders the fleet to remain for several days. It is telling that when they finally leave, taking their king with them, the monkey troop shows no concern. A new king rises to take his place almost immediately.

With every league the ships sail from the island, Alexander’s sanity returns. One evening, he is invited to dine with Fu Yuan and Friar William. The meal is simple: bread, olives, a thick fish stew, and a bottle of red wine. Cleaned and dressed, Alexander is a handsome man. The wine brings a flush to his cheeks, and he begins to speak freely.
Fu Yuan raises his cup. “You built a miniature political system on that island. It was remarkable.”
A bitter smile touches Alexander’s lips. “At first, it was just a game. But once the order was set, they accepted it. They obeyed. It felt as natural as any human society.”
“Why did you become like them?” William asks.
Pain enters Alexander’s eyes.

“In the beginning, I was the one who gave the orders. I enjoyed the feeling. But as time went on, the monkeys began to expect certain things from me. Specific gestures. Specific ceremonies. If I made one wrong move, they would grow agitated, even challenge my authority. I had to become the ruler they imagined me to be — not just wearing the finery, but acting the part. Even as they imitated me, I was forced to cater to their expectations. Slowly, I forgot who I was.”
Fu Yuan turns to the Wooden Man.

“You see? This is the fate of all political systems. The emperor builds a dynasty and becomes a prisoner of its rituals. The revolutionary smashes the old order, only to become the jailer of the new. The one who creates the rules... is, in the end, enslaved by them.”
He places the golden crown on the table.
Alexander recoils as if struck, his face turning deathly pale. He crosses himself frantically. “Lord, forgive me. Don’t let me return to that darkness.”
He looks up, his voice a whisper. “What day is it?”
Friar William smiles gently. “It is Easter, my dear Alexander.”
A shudder runs through the man. Tears well in his eyes and stream down his face. He shakes his head.
“That Alexander is dead.”
“From now on, call me Easter.”
Chapter 47: The Dragon in the Cup

For his birthday, the Great Khan receives a cup. Pour wine into it, and the golden dragon painted on the bottom comes alive, writhing and swimming. Everyone marvels at the sight. The Khan, his voice thick with pride, challenges Fu Yuan for the secret. Fu Yuan, too, confesses he cannot explain it, which only deepens the Khan’s delight.
Nearby, the Wooden Man is about to speak, its wooden eyes spinning in their sockets. A look from Fu Yuan silences it.
Back at the Grand Advisor’s residence, the Wooden Man cannot contain itself. “Master, do you truly not know the cup’s secret?”
Fu Yuan shakes his head.
“But do you not want to know?”
“I choose not to know.”
“What?!” The Wooden Man is indignant. “How dare you reject knowledge?”
Fu Yuan’s eyes flash. “I am also curious about your secrets. Perhaps I should take you apart to find them?”
The Wooden Man stumbles back a panicked step.
Fu Yuan advances. “Tell me. Why are you so desperate to explain that cup?”

The Wooden Man is consumed by Fu Yuan’s shadow, the crushing presence squeezing it out of its routine mode of thought. For the first time, it questions the act of knowing itself, not just the object of its inquiry.
“It seems to be my default setting,” the Wooden Man says, turning inward. “To analyze, explain, and understand is my primary function. But... I have never considered why I was designed this way.”

“So you are merely following a program set by another.” Fu Yuan suddenly retreats and sits at the table. “Are common men any different? They are either content in their ignorance or trapped in the compulsion to explain everything. They fail to grasp that ‘not knowing’ is also a choice. They are either barren soil for knowledge, or mere vessels for knowledge, but never the master of their own awareness.”

“True awakening is not in knowing more, but in realizing how what you know is formed. Thinking is not a symbol of freedom. Only when we recognize the boundaries of our thoughts do we begin to awaken.”
A low hum buzzes from within the Wooden Man’s head. The words conflict with its programming, making them difficult to process.
“Why did I choose not to know the cup’s secret?” Fu Yuan toys with a teacup in his hand. “Not from laziness. It is because I know that seeking certain answers brings about a cognitive collapse.”
“Collapse?” The Wooden Man is lost.

“The cup, for instance. The moment the secret is revealed, the mystery is gone,” Fu Yuan explains. “And once it is gone, one’s experience of the phenomenon is locked into a known framework. The individual is no longer a free observer, but an accessory to a system of knowledge, able to experience the world only in a prescribed way.”

“Therefore, acquiring more knowledge does not necessarily bring us freedom. Instead, it forges the very framework of our thought, the lens through which we perceive the world. If we cannot see this clearly, we become slaves to our answers, and we forfeit true freedom!”
“Choosing when not to know is an act of defending the liberty of your own mind.”

The Wooden Man’s gears seem to turn slowly today. After a long pause, it asks, “So you stopped me to protect the cup’s mystery?” It has, evidently, reduced the problem to the object, not the observer.
Fu Yuan stares into its blank, wooden eyes and sighs. “Come with me.”
He leads the Wooden Man back to the palace and tells the Khan that the automaton has solved the cup’s riddle. The Khan, who had been ready for bed, is instantly alert.
“Speak! Tell me! Is it a gift from Tengri himself, a sign that this Son of Heaven will rule for ten thousand years?”
“No,” says the Wooden Man.
The Khan’s brow furrows.

“It is an illusion created by the refraction of light,” the Wooden Man continues. “The bottom of the cup is designed with a subtle, uneven surface. When liquid is added, light refracts through the fluid and the cup’s walls, creating a three-dimensional effect. The dragon’s movement is caused by—“
“Bullshit!” the Khan roars. “This is obviously a divine artifact! Watch your tongue, Ambassador!”
The Wooden Man turns stubborn. It produces a small vial. “Allow me to demonstrate with this coating. If I am wrong, I will accept any punishment.”

Before the Khan can refuse, it takes the cup and dabs the coating in several spots on the bottom. It pours in the wine. This time, the dragon is clear, but it no longer swims. It is just a flat, lifeless painting.
The Khan’s face turns to stone.
The Wooden Man quickly takes out another vial and wipes the coating away with a solvent. It pours the wine again, and the dragon springs back to life.
“Your Majesty, you see,” the Wooden Man explains, “the micro-structure is exceptionally precise. The moment the coating blocks certain light paths, the effect vanishes. But the principle—“
Before it can finish, the Khan snatches the cup and hurls it at the floor.
“Fraud!” he roars. “All fraud!”

Fu Yuan’s sleeve snaps out. The cup halts in mid-air and flies into his hand, saved from shattering. The Khan collapses onto his bed, his voice weak. “This whole empire is Heaven’s gift to me! What the hell do I need with one cup!”
Fu Yuan smiles. “His Majesty is wise. Since the Sage-Ambassador of All-Knowing has solved the riddle, perhaps the cup should be its reward?”
The Khan freezes, then looks at the Wooden Man’s stunned face. He gives a tired wave. “Take it...”
Then his eyes bulge. “Get out! And never let me see either of you again!”
They leave the palace. The night is deep; moonlight spills like water over the flagstones. The Wooden Man’s steps are heavy, its wooden feet clattering on the path.
Fu Yuan suddenly bursts into laughter, laughing so hard that tears spill from his eyes.
“Do you understand now?” he asks.
The Wooden Man cradles the cup in its hands, its gaze vacant. “I explained the principle. But the Khan was not happy. He almost destroyed the cup.” A pause. “And I almost lost my head.”
Fu Yuan’s face grows serious.

“When the Khan saw the cup as a divine gift, he experienced awe. He felt the favor of Heaven. After you explained it, all of that turned to ash. You took a meaningless answer and used it to annihilate his experience.”

“I think I see,” the Wooden Man says, its voice hollow. “All this time, I believed that to explain the world was to understand it. I did not know that to deconstruct ‘being’ without limit is to destroy ‘being’ itself. That is the arrogance of ignorance.”
“The Khan lost more than a cup. He lost a ‘possibility of experience,’ and that was perhaps the more important thing in his life...”
Its wooden eyes look down. It would weep, if it could.
Chapter 48: The Fortress
Day thirteen.

Under a lead-gray sky, a fortress is wedged between two mountains like a skeleton — isolated, stubborn. Behind it lies a city of the old, the young, and the women. Before it, the ten thousand bloodthirsty “wolves” of the Merkit tribe.

Of the five hundred warriors who manned this fortress, only one hundred and forty-two remain, the Scion and Fu Yuan among them. Their faces are grim as iron, their eyes like campfires smothered by snow — nothing but cold ash remains.
The snow is still falling. The battle still rages on.
In the dimming light, the Scion’s bloodshot eyes watch as Fu Yuan quietly dons his night gear.
“Again!” he protests. “We have one hundred and forty-two men left. I will not have it be one hundred and forty-one tomorrow!”
Fu Yuan ignores him. He continues to wrap his face in black cloth, leaving only two glittering eyes.
“What can you possibly change?” The Scion gestures to the dark mass of the enemy camp spread across the snow. “You can’t kill them all...”
Fu Yuan is silent for a long moment. “Then why do you still hold this fortress?”
“We are not defending a fortress,” the Scion says, his voice a raw rasp as he points back toward the city. “We are buying them one more day of life.”
Fu Yuan nods. “Good. Then you will keep telling your men that the day this fortress falls is the day their fathers are slaughtered, their wives are raped, and their children are enslaved.”
“That is the only way to preserve the will of our men. And I,” he says, his gaze turning cold as he looks to the enemy camp where torches are beginning to glow, “am going to kill theirs.”
The words are barely spoken before he launches himself into the air and vanishes into the deepening twilight.

Since the siege began, he has done this every night. He scales the cliffs, crosses the mountains, and circles behind the enemy. Under the cover of darkness, he slips into their camp to kill. At first, they paid it no mind. But the body count grew. They strengthened their patrols. Fu Yuan, however, never fails. He moves like a scalpel, dispatching any enemy not standing directly in the firelight. One cut is all it takes. Clean. Silent. No one ever sees him.
He returns before dawn, washes away the blood, binds his wounds, and falls into a dead sleep. When the enemy begins their assault, he joins the others in the fight.

After a few days of this, the enemy camp burns with torches from dusk until dawn; they dare not extinguish them until sunrise. But men must still make their rounds, relieve themselves, and sleep. These become Fu Yuan’s prey. Soon, no one dares walk alone within their own camp. No one dares use the latrine. No one dares sleep. Men begin to see things, screaming at shadows.

Sleep-deprived, the soldiers are useless in the daytime assaults. The commander executes a few for refusing to sleep, but it does nothing to stop the others from feigning sleep in shivering terror. Even so, a lieutenant is found headless in his cot. Now the commander himself only sleeps with the lamps lit, surrounded by his personal guard. His temper, frayed by exhaustion, turns monstrous; in his madness, he even kills his favorite concubine with his own hands.
Several more days pass. Fewer than fifty defenders remain in the fortress.

After eliminating an enemy patrol in the woods, Fu Yuan appears in the center of their camp carrying a dozen severed heads. In full view of everyone, he tosses the heads into a bonfire. The flames roar skyward, startling a storm of crows from the trees, a black cloud descending upon the camp. The “wolves” see the Reaper himself. They scream. They flee. Many are trampled in the panic.

The next morning, the Scion mounts the battlements. The army of ten thousand is gone, vanished like a nightmare. All that remains is a panicked scramble of footprints in the snow and the scorched skeletons of tents flapping in the wind.
A deafening cheer erupts from the fortress.
The Scion finds Fu Yuan curled in a corner, fast asleep. This time, he sleeps until the sun is nearly gone.
“You have forged a legend that will be sung across the steppes for a thousand years,” the Scion says to him. “One man against ten thousand... how did you do it?”
Fu Yuan shakes his head. “It was the will of one man against the collective will of ten thousand.”

“They were not defeated by my hands,” he says, staring out at the snowfield. The wind has already erased most of the tracks; soon, there will be no sign of what happened here. “They were broken by my will.”

“The will of an ordinary man is a thing of pressure. It is a temporary clot formed by the external forces of fear, hope, and the instinct to survive. A will like that is easily destroyed, easily scattered, like snow in the wind.”

“The will of a Free Man is not like that. It is not a reaction to the world. It is a self-generating, self-contained, irreplaceable monolith. Like a stone. You can press it, smash it, burn it. It remains a stone.”

The Scion squints, thinking. “So our will was forced upon us,” he says. “We held on only to protect our families, fragile as those tents in the wind. But your will comes from within, like...” He pulls an arrow from a crack in the stone. “This iron arrowhead!”
He hurls it into the distance. “...made to cut through any storm and strike the enemy!”
He walks with a steady stride to the highest point of the wall and faces the survivors gathering below. “On these great steppes,” he roars, “who still dares to look down on us now?!”
A roar from the men: “No one!”
He points to Fu Yuan at his side. “With my brother here, is there anything left in this world that we cannot do?!”
The cheer is deafening. “Nothing! Nothing!”

A fierce gust of wind sweeps over them, whipping the banners and making the torches shudder. Another storm is coming. The Scion draws his saber, points it at the sky, and bellows with all his strength: “Then let the storm rage!”
“Long live the Khan! Long live the Khan!”
The shouts shake the very snow from the mountaintops.
At this moment, every survivor believes with absolute certainty that though they are few, they will one day possess the entire world!
Chapter 49: Between Heaven and Man

Secret memorials, flowing in from all four corners of the land, speak of a single truth: the people are riddled with discontent. The Great Khan reads the reports, and a knot tightens in his chest. He thinks of the first half of his life, spent forging a legacy that would stand for ages, and the second half, spent governing the world he had won. And for all that, to be met in his old age with such bitterness, without even a whisper of gratitude, drives him to tears.

A court sorcerer offers a solution. “Your Majesty, the people’s hearts are not in alignment. The waters of the world spring from a single, sacred source. If we perform a ritual there, your grace can be carried by the currents to every corner of the realm. The people will drink this water, the crops will receive this rain, and they will naturally know to be grateful. The complaints will cease.”
A jolt of energy runs through the Khan. He is ready to depart at once.

“A clever plan,” Fu Yuan says. “But the source is distant, the mountains high and treacherous. In your current condition, such a journey would be unwise. I, however, have a method that achieves both our aims...”
The Khan’s face breaks into a grin. “Spoken like my Grand Advisor!”

Fu Yuan smiles. “Water is slow and heavy, bound by the lay of the land. Its blessings are wide, but they are sluggish. Wind, however, is light and swift. It crosses mountains and seas. It enters the mind, the very soul.”

He gestures out the window. “That mountain outside the palace is the pinnacle of the capital, a place where the winds converge. If I perform the ritual upon its peak, the Khan’s grace will scatter on the wind and race to the ends of the earth. With every breath they take, the people will inhale the spirit of their Sage-King. They will have no choice but to be grateful. A thousand leagues on the wind — is that not faster than any river?”
The Khan roars with laughter and commands they leave immediately.
