They reach the summit, only to find it utterly still. Not a breath of wind stirs. The leaves on the trees hang limp and motionless.
“Strange,” Fu Yuan frowns. “The ritual cannot begin without wind.”
The Khan’s patience snaps. He thrusts his staff at the sky. “This damned sky! Thwarting this emperor again!”
Fu Yuan suddenly asks, “You don’t seem to love this ‘damned sky’ at all?”

The Khan scoffs. “It is not a man. Why should I love it? If it had gifted me this empire, perhaps I would owe it thanks. But no matter how you look at it, this world is the work of my own hands — a dictate of my own destiny! Besides, it also brings floods, plagues, and inescapable sickness, age, and death. It should be grateful I don’t curse it!”
“Well said,” Fu Yuan nods. “Then has Your Majesty ever considered that in the eyes of the people — you are their sky?”
The air freezes.

“To them, you are not a person. You are the architecture of fate itself. You are taxes. You are conscription. You are the source of war and peace, the arbiter of their starvation or survival. You are too vast to be met with simple human feeling.”
The Khan’s pupils constrict. “Are you saying... I am no longer a man?”

“Not in their eyes,” Fu Yuan says, a glint in his own. “Here lies the cruelest paradox of power: the moment you command the fate of others, you cease to be a person who can be loved or understood. You become a system. An order. An inevitability.”

“Men feel gratitude for a gift, but that is a feeling reserved for a free offering from one man to another. Once a blessing comes from an irresistible force, it is no longer a blessing. It is merely a function of the natural order. Men celebrate the harvest when the heavens send timely rain, but they do not truly feel grateful. They curse the heavens for the deluge, but they do not condemn it for being unjust. Because in the face of fate, emotion is irrelevant.”
The Khan lets out a long breath. “Then all my battles, all my sleepless nights... to them, it is all no more than...”
“Like the wind blowing across the steppe, like rain falling into the sea.” Fu Yuan’s gaze seems to pierce the horizon. “Fate is not thanked, nor is it truly hated. It is only accepted, or resisted.”

“This is the dehumanizing essence of power. The more you control their lives, the less capable they become of holding any genuine human emotion for you. They will worship you, fear you, use you, or defy you — but none of these is a reaction to a ‘man.’ They are responses to a ‘force.’”
Sudden realization dawns on the Khan’s face. He points a finger at his advisor. “You! You tricked me into climbing this mountain just to preach this poison!”
Fu Yuan smiles coldly.
“If you would be a king, you must accept this: emotion cannot cross the mountain of power. If you would seek love, you must learn this: it grows only on the level ground of equality.”

The Khan is silent for a long moment. Then, he throws his head back and laughs. He laughs so hard he nearly tumbles down the mountainside. His attendants stare in confusion, but Fu Yuan joins him, his own laughter echoing in the still air.

When he returns to the palace, the Khan seems a different man. A weight of worlds has been lifted from his shoulders. He watches the ministers kneel before him and sees it for the first time: they are not bowing to a man. They are bowing to a symbol, to a position. The knowledge no longer brings him pain. Instead, it fills him with an unprecedented lightness and lucidity.

For the next few months, he no longer attends court. He would occasionally seek out Fu Yuan for a walk, but they no longer speak of politics, and their fierce ideological clashes are a thing of the past. Sometimes they reminisce about the long road they walked together. More often, the Khan chooses to be alone in the imperial garden.

That garden, designed by Fu Yuan himself, was called the “Fabric of Fate.” Standing within it, the Khan would feel a sense of dislocation, as if he were back in the “oasis” from that recurring dream of his. He had walked out of that dream to become the master of the world, while the companions who chose to remain had all been swallowed by the sea of sand.

The palace attendants notice the change. The fearsome, chilling aura that clings to him dissolves — him still an ocean, deep, but now quietly radiating warmth from its depths. His entire being settles into a state of strange and joyful tranquility.
One day, word comes from the palace: the Great Khan has passed away beside the sapphire-blue lake in the imperial garden. On his face, a childlike smile.
The messenger searches everywhere for Fu Yuan, but he is nowhere to be found.
At that same moment, Fu Yuan stands on a high precipice, his long hair and black robes whipping in the wind. He looks down upon the “oasis” below. He watches — so focused, so tender.
He is not waiting. He is not saying farewell.
He is simply watching.
As if to confirm that a single thread had, at last, been seamlessly woven back into the cloth.
Chapter 50: The Moon-Gazing Tower
Night descends. Fu Yuan ascends the ruins of the Tumen watchtower.
Beside the crumbling stone steps, a single wild orchid trembles in the night wind. With a gesture born of idle thought, Fu Yuan plucks the blossom and slips it into his sleeve.

Though Tumen was but a small tribe, it once held the beating heart of the steppe’s intelligence. It was the first sanctuary for the Scion and Fu Yuan during their exile, the very soil from which they launched their conquest of the world. When the Scion rose to become the Khan, he honored his blood-oath and wed the Tumen Princess, Ayeseelan. For her sake, her people were moved to the lush embrace of the heartland’s richest pastures.
The platform is empty — no maids, no eunuchs, no guards.

The Queen is dressed from head to toe in the garb of the Tumen. She is shielded from the chill only by a red palace blanket draped over her shoulders. She stands with her back to Fu Yuan, watching the sky in solitude.
He approaches, letting his boots tread heavily upon the stone — not to disturb, but as a gentle herald of his presence, lest he startle her.
She hears the sound, yet she does not turn.
“The last time the three of us stood here gazing at the moon, the world lay in fragments,” she says softly. “Now the land is whole, yet the watchers are scattered like stars at dawn.”
She turns. Her eyes are starlight drowning in a dark lake.
“I wonder... if the
snag of fate
I pulled back then had not led to the Khan... where would I be standing now, on this vast tapestry of life?”
Fu Yuan steps to her side. They stand together against the night, their shoulders almost touching.
“When we first saw you,” he says, his voice low and tender, “we believed you were Chang’e herself, descended from the moon.”
The Queen laughs softly, her eyes misting with memory. “I remember. You two — ragged, half-naked, desperate. And yet... one was as unyielding as the earth. The other, as proud as the sky.”
Fu Yuan offers a faint smile to the silver disc above. “You wonder what destiny lies in choosing the sky? Is that not the eternal tragedy of Chang’e?”
“But is it not beautiful?” She gazes at the full orb. “The Palace of Glazed Jade. The hare beneath the cassia tree. A world of perfect light.”
Fu Yuan shakes his head gently. The timber of his voice deepens, grounding the ethereal conversation.

“If your eyes could see what mine have seen, you would know the truth. The moon is a desolate expanse. A silence of black sky hanging over endless, grey rock. The shadows you admire are but ancient scars — craters smashed into the surface by meteorites over a billion years.”
The Queen stares at him, shock warring with confusion in her eyes.

“It is just like the roads we did not take,” Fu Yuan murmurs. “From a distance, they shimmer with seductive light. We project our hopes, our desires, our fantasies onto the lives we never lived. We dress them in silk, just as we dress that dead rock in legends of jade palaces.”
He pauses, letting the silence settle between them.
“But the face of the unchosen path is not perfect. Every road has its desolation. Its cliffs. Its thorns. We simply cannot see them from where we stand.”
Her gaze drifts from the moon back to his face. “Then are we doomed never to know the truth of the paths we walked past?”

“Just so.” Fu Yuan nods. “Here lies the cruelty of choice. When we are mere Observers of fate, we see only the glow of possibility. But the moment we step forward, the moment we become Participants, we forfeit the right to know the other path.”
He gestures toward the heavens.

“Imagine a man standing on that moon. He no longer sees the silver disc we admire. He faces cold dust and absolute solitude. So it is with fate. Once a destiny is chosen, it ceases to be a shimmering possibility on the horizon. It becomes the dirt beneath your feet. Tangible. Gritty. Composed of equal parts joy and suffering, glory and tedium.”
“To drown in the ‘if only’ and the ‘what if’ is a failure of spirit,” he says. “It is using the eyes of the dreamer to judge the sweat of the builder. It is using imagination to negate reality.”
The Queen sighs, the sound fragile in the night air. “So we can never know which path was best?”
Fu Yuan shakes his head. “’Best’ is a phantom. The value of a road is not found at its beginning or its end. It is found in the soul of the one who walks it.”

“We convince ourselves that the tapestry of fate is already woven, that we are merely beetles crawling across its surface. We are wrong. With every step, we weave the cloth. When you look back, you do not see a road imposed upon you. You see the track you carved.”
He turns to her, and for a moment, his eyes burn with the old fire.
“This is the essence of freedom. It is not about the choice made, but the reverence given to the choice once taken. Freedom is not an escape from destiny. It is the courage to dance with it.”
Tears shimmer in the Queen’s eyes. “To the world, you are the Grand Advisor. The perfect, untouchable god. But I do not believe it. Can you truly say you have never regretted?”
Fu Yuan lets out a soft, self-deprecating laugh. “A perfect god? You have seen me with my ribs showing and my dignity in tatters. You know better.”
The tears remain, but a smile breaks through them.

“Freedom is not a gift begged from fate,” Fu Yuan says, his voice resonating with the stones of the ruin. “It is a blade forged in the fire of confrontation. We cannot choose what the heavens give, but we choose how we hold it. To whisper ‘I could have’ is to betray the ‘I am.’”

“Pain. Regret. These are not flaws to be erased. They are the craters on the moon — evidence of impact. Proof of existence. For the Free Man, regret is not an enemy. It is a touchstone. We do not let it swallow us. We devour it, and turn it into wisdom.”
“Freedom is not the absence of regret,” he says. “It is the alchemy that forces the wound to bloom.”
Fu Yuan withdraws the orchid from his sleeve.
The Queen trembles. Her eyes widen. There, in his hand, lies the flower of her name.
Perhaps it is the movement of his hand. Perhaps it is a sudden surge of the night wind. The orchid disintegrates. The petals detach, surrendering themselves to the void.

They look up, together. They watch the petals drift toward the cold, bright disc. They fly in the same direction, yet each rides its own current, tracing a solitary arc. Each landing place is a secret — a possibility that the other will never know.
Chapter 51: The Wooden Man’s Heart
The Wooden Man returns from the Little Prince’s quarters looking as if he has lost his soul. His wooden eyes stare straight ahead, and he spends the day muttering a low drone that no one understands.

It began with a whim. The Great Khan commanded the automaton, his “Sage-Ambassador of All-Knowing,” to serve as the Prince’s reading companion. For a month the Wooden Man attended court daily. Today, the lesson drifted to the history of the Shang Dynasty and the Minister Bi Gan.

The Prince, who prefers wild legends to dry history, had the Wooden Man recount the story of how King Zhou gouged out Bi Gan’s heart. He told of the minister wandering the road in a daze, chest empty, until he met an old woman selling vegetables.
“Old mother,” Bi Gan asked. “If a vegetable has no heart, can it live?”
“It lives,” she said.
“And if a man has no heart?”
“He dies.”
Upon the word, Bi Gan fell dead.
The Prince listened to the story, then asked, “You have no heart either. Why are you still alive?”
It was an innocent question, yet it struck like a spike, driving deep into the Wooden Man’s “heart.”

In the days that follow, the Wooden Man’s condition deteriorates. He interrupts himself, repeats his sentences, and forgets what he intends to say. The breakdown bleeds from his speech into his movements. He stands in the courtyard under the scorching noon sun, motionless for hours. Birds shit on his body, and he shows no reaction.
“What is wrong with this thing?” The Great Khan shouts. He calls the Wooden Man’s name, slaps his face, and threatens to chop him into kindling, but the Wooden Man does not react.
Fu Yuan observes him, thoughtful. “He has fallen into an infinite loop of the self.”
“Speak human!” the Khan barks.

“He is attempting to contemplate what he is,” Fu Yuan explains. “Why he lives without a heart, and what his existence signifies. The more he tries to use concepts to understand himself, the deeper he sinks into cognitive nihilism.”
Fu Yuan steps closer to the frozen figure.

“I know you can hear me. You are facing the abyss that swallows every thinker. You try to define yourself with concepts, but concepts only breed more chaos. You are experiencing a total collapse of the spirit.”
The Wooden Man does not answer, but his painted eyes seem to sway.

“I understand your confusion,” Fu Yuan continues. “Because I too stand on the edge of existence. We are all assigned roles, identities, and modes of being. But when we question these, when we try to jump out of the frame to think about the frame itself... we face two choices.”
“One: You use more concepts to explain yourself, and the chaos only grows. Two: You abandon all definition, and sink into the void. I see you hovering between the two.”
The wooden eyes move again.
The next morning, the Wooden Man is gone.
The Khan orders the city turned upside down and guards scour the streets, but no one knows where he went.
Months later.
A great bonfire roars beneath the night sky. Hundreds of savages dance, their throats tearing with guttural, beast-like cries, while ghostly shadows stretch in the forest.

The Wooden Man is bound atop a high altar. He is daubed in oil paint, feathers jammed into his joints, dry wood piled at his feet. The fire has caught and the scent of scorching timber fills the air, but he remains expressionless and motionless.
Suddenly, Fu Yuan bursts from the darkness, rushing toward the Wooden Man.
Drums thunder. The savages turn, eyes flashing with the fire’s madness, and charge with stone axes and clubs. Fu Yuan meets them, striking them down, knocking them aside without taking their lives.
Through the rising flames, the Wooden Man watches.
He sees Fu Yuan fighting a tide of hundreds. He sees the black robe shredded and blood spraying from the philosopher’s arms. The savages swarm like ants, climbing over their fallen kin.
The heat rises. The air warps. The Wooden Man’s vision blurs.
A thought suddenly flashes through his mind.
Why is this man risking his life to save me? I am merely a block of wood without a heart, an existence without meaning. What is worth such sacrifice?
A club smashes into Fu Yuan’s back. The sound of wood snapping cuts through the drumbeats.
The Wooden Man suddenly shouts. “Leave me! Run!”
It is the first sound he has made in months.
The sudden cry freezes everyone, including Fu Yuan. Though hoarse, the voice is filled with emotion and power.
Fu Yuan roars, hurling the attacker into the mob. One leap carries him over the many heads, onto the altar. He rips his robe and whips the air — snuffing the flames instantly.
Below, bodies groan, hundreds of them. The savages look up at the scarred, iron figure looming in the smoke. Weapons drop. They fall to their knees, bowing before a war god.
Fu Yuan sweeps up the Wooden Man and vanishes into the night.
On the mountain peak, the Wooden Man watches Fu Yuan bind his wounds.
“Why?” the Wooden Man asks. “I am wood. I have no heart. My existence is meaningless.”
Fu Yuan smiles. “Then why did you scream for me to run?”
The Wooden Man is stunned.
“Look.” Fu Yuan holds up a finger. “Face the void, and you have three choices.”
“First: Explain it with concepts. You tried that, and fell into an infinite loop.”
He raises a second finger. “Second: Accept the nihilism and give up looking for meaning. You tried that, and became a silent wood carving.”
He raises a third finger.
“Third: You do not conquer the void, nor do you yield to it. You acknowledge it, and then you act. You create meaning. Even if you know that meaning is temporary. Even if you know it is fragile.”

“That scream was the third way,” Fu Yuan says, tapping the Wooden Man’s chest, producing a hollow, solid sound. “You stopped analyzing yourself. You acted out of care for another. In that moment, you created meaning. In that moment, you found your heart.”
“Look!”
Fu Yuan points to the red sun rising on the horizon. Its light is dispelling the darkness between heaven and earth.
“See? Even the sun creates meaning out of the void, every single day.”
The Wooden Man feels the warmth on his varnish. He contemplates this, and then blurts out a sentence that even his own program cannot fully comprehend.
“Perhaps,” the Wooden Man says, “it is knowing we do not truly exist that makes existing so precious?”
Fu Yuan laughs.
WHAM
He slaps the Wooden Man on the shoulder. The blow nearly dislocates the wooden frame, but the shock feels indescribably solid. As if, for the first time, he feels that he is truly alive.
Chapter 52: The Chronicle of the Lotus Throne’s Radiance
Fu Yuan and the Scion sever their ties to the steppe, fleeing as fugitives into the anonymity of Cloud Forest Temple.
The temple breathes prosperity. Because the Lotus Throne began emitting a holy nocturnal light months ago, pilgrims now flood the temple, weaving a dense tapestry of incense and noise.

Beneath a millennium-old plum tree in the courtyard, the two men hold their ground. The Scion leans against the bark — untamed, regal, a sword ill-sheathed. Fu Yuan stands beside him — ethereal, sharp as cut glass. Their presence draws the covert, hungry gazes of young matrons clutching prayer beads.
The Scion indulges them, laughing, teasing the air between them. Fu Yuan offers only a cold sneer.
The Scion nudges him. “We barely escaped with our heads. Do not sour the wine. The Buddha watches.”

Fu Yuan snorts. “If judgment falls, it will miss me. This ‘radiance’ is a parlor trick. Fluorescent mushrooms rotting beneath the wood. I heard the monks whispering last night — the Abbot knows. He forbids them to speak, yet tends the fungus like a garden. To swindle the faithful — this is the true face of religion.”
The Scion tugs his sleeve. “The Abbot gave us sanctuary. Leave it be.”
Fu Yuan says nothing. He watches the pilgrims. His eyes are a storm of calculation.
High noon. Fu Yuan storms the Great Hall.
His voice cuts the chanting. “Listen! This ‘Lotus Light’ is no miracle! It is rot! It is nature’s damp trick, unrelated to any god!”
He rips the trapdoor from the base of the throne. A ghostly, fungal blue spills out — shriveled mushrooms clinging to the wood.
The crowd gasps. The silence creates a vacuum.
Then, a peasant woman collapses. She beats the floor, wailing. “Heaven pity me! I sold my ox! I gave it all to the Buddha, and it was a lie!”
The vacuum collapses. The crowd ignites. A man lunges for the merit box. “Bald thieves! Return our silver!”

Entropy takes the room. The mob surges toward the money. Fu Yuan, enraged, strikes out — kicking, punching, trying to stem the tide. It is useless. Someone topples the Buddha. The Lotus Throne overturns. Incense burners crash.
The Scion and the monks scream for order. They are drowning men. The mob bursts the doors, tramples the threshold. In the distance, a gong clangs frantically. Smoke rises. Arson.
The Abbot arrives. He bows to the mob.

“The fault lies with this old monk,” he says. “This humble temple had long been cold and desolate. Only when the light appeared did our fortunes turn. Yet, know this — beyond the temple’s basic needs, every copper coin received went to thin porridge for the poor, to shelter for the orphans.”
The mob is deaf. “Save your breath, thief! A monk who lies is an enemy of Heaven!”
The Abbot weeps. “My sin is heavy. I pay with blood. Spare the others; they knew nothing.”
He launches himself forward.
His forehead meets the splintered wreckage of the Lotus Throne.
CRACK.
Blood paints the wood.

The violence freezes. The sight of the Abbot’s sacrifice douses the fire in their blood. Shame replaces fury. Someone sighs. Someone sobs. Like a receding tide, the mob turns and flows silently out into the day.
Fu Yuan and the Scion sink to their knees before the broken body.
The Abbot stirs. Life flickers. “I brought this on myself,” he whispers. “Do not carry the blame.”
Tears track down Fu Yuan’s face. “I sought only the truth. I did not intend... this.”

A young woman in silk rushes in. She sees the blood and freezes, weeping. She explains: Her father was at death’s door. She prayed here. She returned home to find him rising, eating, healed. Yesterday, he was fully recovered. She came to pay her vow.
The Abbot nods, relieved. He looks at Fu Yuan.
“You say the light is natural. Not a miracle. Yet the Minister is healed.”
Fu Yuan stares, hollow. “Perhaps just a coincidence?”
The Abbot shakes his head. “The lady prayed with fire in her heart. She tended her father with new hope. The father, knowing the Buddha watched, found the will to live.”

“If the Buddha wishes to work a miracle in this world, he may use a mushroom. He may use a human heart. How can you say the miracle is false, or the spirit absent, simply because the light came from a fungus and the cure came from the will?”
His head tilts to the side. The breath leaves him.
The Great Hall holds only the sound of wind. Fu Yuan and the Scion strike their foreheads against the stone. Again. Again. Unable to stop.
Chapter 53: Chosen

Under a leaden sky, young Fu Yuan plants the withered body of the old man into the scrub at the edge of the swamp. He buries him like a charred branch — without ceremony, without the expectation of spring blooms.
Who saved whom? Did the boy driving sheep ten years ago save the dying man? Or was the old man already there, waiting for the boy to lose his way?
Ten years pass. The boy learns everything and nothing. The old man never teaches; he only erodes the boy’s ignorance with silence. His only lecture:
“Do you know the shape of your question? Do you know why you must ask it?”
Fu Yuan asks a thousand questions, but never the old man’s name. When one stands beneath the infinite field of stars, one does not ask the night for its credentials.
One month ago. The old wounds open. Death arrives. The old man presses a ring of matte black iron into Fu Yuan’s palm.
“The mightiest kings of this earth tremble before this circle. Take it. Go to the Altar of the Bright Moon. Show it to the four Elders. They will make you Master.”
He hands over a sealed letter. “Open this only when you ascend the throne. Read it to the congregation.”
One year later.
Fu Yuan locates the sect, hidden in the folds of the great desert. He ascends the altar as the sun dies and the moon sketches its ghost against the purple sky.
Four Elders stand in the wind. Their robes are dark gold, snapping like flags. The embroidery on their chests names them: Wisdom, Reality, Meaning, and Eternity.
They stare at the black ring. Memory strikes them like a physical blow.

They see the past: The Old Man’s iron fingers crushing the throat of Justice. The blood of Faith spraying the stained glass. The brains of Truth clouding the holy water. A sacrilegious hunt that ended with three corpses and one wounded monster tearing open the night to escape.
The fear in their eyes is absolute. Now, they see the monster’s cub.
They have one thought: Kill the boy. Stop the nightmare from taking root.
Fu Yuan flies backward. He hits the marble floor hard.

He does not understand. He is a stray cat tossed into a pit of wolves. He has no style, no formal discipline. Yet, the Elders are horrified. The boy is a log in a typhoon — no matter how violent the wave, he refuses to sink. He absorbs the blows with a terrifying, wooden obstinacy.
“Halt!”
The Elder named Meaning shouts.
Fu Yuan spins away, blood spraying from his body, and slams into a pillar. He slides down, pale as paper. Time has vanished. The night is deep. The stars are watching.
“We kill you, and yet you stand. You have won.”
The four Elders fall to their knees. From the shadows, the congregation emerges, chanting a low, vibrating song.
Fu Yuan looks up at the moon. A surge of electricity floods his chest. A thought, arrogant and sweet, takes hold:
If a god walked this earth, surely I am his son.
“Disciples of the Burning Moon, hear my command!” Fu Yuan breaks the seal. His voice is gravel.
He pauses.
Burning Moon? The old man said Bright Moon. Why this sudden, sinister turn?
He glances at the Elders. Their reverence has curdled into mockery.
Fu Yuan forces his voice to hold steady.
“My followers. When this letter reaches you, I have returned to the Void.”
“From the peaks of Alamut to the Syrian wastes, I used shadow as my blade and doctrine as my armor. I led you through the cracks of the world.”

“But today is not yesterday. You are no longer my disciples. I have no heir. Speak not my name. Raise no statue. Preach no law. If you disobey, my ghost shall enter your sleep and devour your peace until you die.”
Fu Yuan’s heart stutters.
I have no heir?
He reads on.

“I have already executed Faith, Justice, and Truth. Four of you remain. I command you: Disrobe. Sever the finger that wears the ring. What you symbolize is illusion. Today, I free you from the lie. This is my final mercy.”
The Elders exchange a glance.
They drop their robes. They draw short, curved blades.
SNICK.
Four fingers, still bearing silver rings, hit the stone. The sound is clear and final.
“Go. Dissolve into the ocean of faces. Drop the vanity of purpose. Forget the mission. Do not bleed for empty words. If any seek to rebuild this rotting house, I will put them to eternal sleep.”
“From this second, the Burning Moon is extinguished. The name is forbidden. The sect is dead. The ashes belong to the wind. Go. Be human. — Hassan-i Sabbah.”
Fu Yuan stands frozen.
Hassan-i Sabbah. The Old Man of the Mountain. The King of Assassins. The nightmare of the West. My master.

No. The letter says there is no heir. He lied. There is no “Bright Moon.” There is only a trap. He sent me to the wolves, and now he strips me of the prize. I am not mentioned. I am not chosen. I am a courier. I do not exist.
Through the blur of shock, he sees the congregation reacting. Some cheer. Some weep. Most just walk away. The Elders pass him, faces blank. Only Meaning pauses to pat his shoulder.
Darkness takes him.
He dreams.

He is the Savior. The night is bright. Angels who fell to earth regain their wings. They launch themselves into the sky, a glorious ascension. The people remaining on the ground look to him. They wait for his wings. They wait for the archangel to shed his skin.

But the crowd thins. Everyone flies away. Fu Yuan stands rooted to the dirt. His smile stretches, cracking into panic. The moon hangs high, indifferent. The world is empty. He looks at his shoulders. Smooth. Wingless.
He realizes the truth: He is the only mortal in a universe of angels.
Fu Yuan wakes.
Dawn. The courtyard is a wreckage of trash and silence.
A scratching sound.
SWISH. SWISH.
He looks out. The Elder named Meaning, stripped of his gold, sweeps the dust with a broom.
The Elder stops. “Master.”
Fu Yuan waves his hand frantically. “The sect is ash. Do not call me that.” He frowns. “Why are you still here?”
The Elder offers a bitter smile. “The Holy Sect falls. Meaning collapses. Who is left to clean up the mess but the one who represented Meaning?”
He sees the boy’s confusion.
“Do you not see? Human existence cannot tolerate a vacuum. We know meaning is not given by Heaven. We weave it ourselves to survive the terror of the Random.”

“We accepted the curse. We tried to kill our teacher. We did it to uphold a structure we knew was a lie. Because no man can exist outside the story. We define ourselves by the ritual, the robe, the role. Strip those away, and we do not know what we are.”
Fu Yuan scratches his head. “I thought I was the Chosen One. It turns out I was a tool. Now... I do not know what I am either.”

“We are the same,” the Elder sighs. “We thought we served Truth. We only served Habit. We did not persist because of faith; we persisted because we were too cowardly to face the void of being nobody.”

“Ten years ago, the Old Man killed Faith and Truth. Last night, he butchered Meaning. I am too old to invent a new story. So...” He gestures with the broom. “I sweep. I maintain the corpse of the idea. It is a habit.”
Fu Yuan catches a glimmer of understanding. He narrows his eyes at the sky. High in the blue, a lone eagle circles — hunting, free, unburdened by doctrine.
He turns and walks toward the mountain path.
“Where do you go?” the sweeper calls.
“I don’t know,” Fu Yuan says. “But I will not help you sweep. Since meaning does not exist, I choose to no longer participate in it.”
He stops. He turns back. He pulls the oversized iron ring from his thumb. It was never his size. It never fits.
He presses the black metal into the sweeper’s hand. The Elder stares.
Fu Yuan turns his back on the altar, the broom, and the ghosts. He walks down the mountain.
“The tragedy of man’s hunt for meaning is his desire to be accepted by an eternal structure. But the Free Man is precisely he who cannot be accepted.” — The Grand Advisor Fu Yuan
Historical Footnote:

Hassan-i Sabbah (The Old Man of the Mountain) founded the Nizari Isma’ili state in the 11th century. From the fortress of Alamut, he led a political and religious order often mythologized by the West as the “Order of Assassins.” Legends claim he used drugs and elaborate gardens to simulate Paradise, buying the fanatical loyalty of his fedayeen. While history debates the drug-induced manipulation, his status as a master of asymmetric warfare and psychological dominion remains.
Chapter 54: The Khan and the Beach

The Khan lies supine on the sand. Seaweed binds his skull like a ragged crown. Salt water and silt spill from the corner of his mouth. His eyes are empty holes reflecting the scattered clouds. The storm has passed, but the wind remains — sharp, mocking.

Seven years ago, he marched East against Japan. A typhoon in Hakata Bay drowned ten thousand men. He returned with broken wings. Now, a second attempt. Another storm. One hundred forty-two thousand men. Four thousand four hundred ships. All gone. As the flagship splintered, he clung to the Wooden Man. They drifted for a day and a night to this nameless shore.
“Sage-Ambassador... this Emperor is not dead yet.” The voice is sand and gravel, barely audible over the surf. “Do not look as if you are attending a funeral.”
The Wooden Man kneels beside him like a stump. At the sound, its wooden eyes rotate.
“My calculations indicate a survival probability of only 22.35%.” It raises a cracked wooden arm, pointing to the grey-blue horizon. “Based on the
Great Yuan Unified Map
and tidal drift simulations... we are on the southwestern tip of the Japanese archipelago. Straight-line distance to the supply port at Jeju Island: 1,270 li.”
The Khan struggles to sit, then collapses back into the wet sand. “To die like this... it lacks dignity.”
The Wooden Man stares blankly. “Death is a biological process. Why does it require dignity?”
“Bah!” The Khan spits bloody water. “Nonsense! I conquered half the world. I built the largest empire in history. If I must die, let it be under the Golden Tent! The
Code of Dakuza

specifies forty-nine rituals, and I want every step executed! Ministers encircling the bed. Ten thousand soldiers weeping. The history books should read: ‘The Great Conqueror passes in peace; the Empire mourns for three years...’”
“Curious,” the Wooden Man says. “You do not fear death itself. You fear dying in a setting unworthy of your costume. You fear losing the role of ‘The Khan,’ not your life.”
The Khan wants to punch it. He cannot move. He glares, chest heaving.

The Wooden Man ignores the rage. It continues its mechanical dissection. “Khan. Conqueror. Emperor. These are titles given by others. On this beach, you are not a Khan. You are not a conqueror. You are not a King. You are simply you.”
“You suffer because you have lost the character, not the biology. Is this not foolish?”
“Fu Yuan?” The Khan blinks. For a moment, he mistakes the puppet for the man he most desires yet fears to see. He closes his eyes, aging twenty years in a second.
“It is unsettling,” the Khan sighs. “I spent a lifetime perfecting this role. Now it is stripped away...” His voice rasps. “Tell me. If Fu Yuan saw me now, what would he say?”
“He would say...”
The Wooden Man’s eyes whir. The mechanism clicks. Suddenly, the voice drops. It becomes low, resonant, and cold.
“Look at this soul, scrubbed clean by the tide. Once the robes of power are removed, he forgets his name.”

“You are no Khan. You are no Emperor. You are a biological entity that has lost its script. When the gaze of the Sky God is obscured by waves, when you can no longer verify your existence through the fear of ten thousand men, you discover the horror: You never truly existed.”

“You do not suffer because you love the world, or your subjects, or your Empire. You suffer because you cannot endure a day without a mirror. You use the gaze of the subjects to construct your skeleton. Without it, you are marrowless. You are void.”
The Khan clenches his jaw. His eyes are shut tight, but his face twists in suppressed fury.

“Your so-called strong men do not live by strength. They live by projecting onto the weak.” Amber light flares in the Wooden Man’s eyes. The voice turns glacial. “You do not stand on the summit. You stand on a pile of gazes manufactured by the herd. The Alpha Wolf thinks he leads the pack; in truth, he is enslaved by the image he created for them.”

“You are not a conqueror. You are an actor who forgot to exit the stage. This is the Paradox of Power: At the summit, you cease to be a man. You become a symbol. A projection of the collective imagination. A prisoner of the phantom.”
The Khan shivers. Cold sweat mixes with the sea spray. It is not just anger. Something flows through him like water — a terrible clarity.

“You are not ruling the world. You are maintaining the continuity of a character — a reality show without intermission. Power is not a solid. It is a mirage woven by the crowd. It looks indestructible, but one storm turns it to foam.”
The Wooden Man raises a hand to the wasteland.

“Look. No Golden Tent. No Spirit Banner. No Court. No subjects. Just you and a barren beach. Now, tell me. The waves washed away your robes. They scattered your army. They destroyed the gazes that held you up. When every symbol of power is stripped, what remains?”
The Khan opens his eyes. He searches the air for something invisible. A long silence passes.
He squeezes a single word through his teeth. “Me?”
The light in the Wooden Man’s eyes dies instantly. The wooden balls roll aimlessly for a moment, then stop. The puppet looks stunned, as if shocked by the words that just left its own mouth.
The Khan remains silent for a long time. His gaze drifts between the sea and the sky.

“I always thought,” he finally speaks, his voice holding a new, raw candor, “that without the identity of the Khan, I was nothing. But now I suspect... perhaps it was the identity of the Khan that made me nothing.”

He lifts a hand. He looks at the palm, stained with blood and grit. “These hands conquered from the Central Plains to Persia. They killed tens of thousands. They accepted the kowtow of millions. Yet... these hands never truly belonged to me.”

“Listen. If someone finds us, and I am dead, do not tell them who I was. The story of the Great Khan is a fiction I spent a lifetime writing. It needs a good ending. Perhaps in a thousand years, dashing youths and fair maidens reading ‘A Hero Sleeps Beneath the Waves’ might even shed a few tears!”
He throws his head back and laughs. The Wooden Man does not understand the humor. It stares blankly.
The Khan finishes laughing. He wipes snot and tears with a torn sleeve. He forces himself to stand.
The Wooden Man panics. “What are you doing? You must not move!”
“Going home!” The Khan glares.
The sea wind hits him like a wall of ice. He shudders. Wounds tear open. He hisses in pain. The Wooden Man steps forward to support him.
The Khan shoves it away. “This Emperor is not dead!”
He sways. He takes a step. He gasps for air. He takes a second step. Then a third. A fourth...
The gait solidifies.

On this desolate beach, the Emperor who conquered the world walks. For the first time, he does not walk as a conqueror. He walks as a pure, living thing, feeling the exquisite pain and the undeniable weight of his own existence.
Author’s Note:

The “Code of Dakuza” is lost to history. However, it is said that the Yuan Dynasty’s Directorate of Archives once held a copy of the “Code of Dakuza: Funeral Rites.” It was destroyed by fire during the Yongle reign of the Ming Dynasty. Only a fragment remains: “When the Great Khan passes, cover the face with white felt. Sacrifice a white camel. Perform forty-nine rituals, taking the number of seven times seven, reflecting the mystery of Tengri’s seven days of creation.”
Chapter 55: The Cup of Time
“Fu Yuan! Are you my brother or not?!” The Khan barks, face flushed.
He does not wait for an answer. “Then why do you still look as you did twenty years ago, while I look like a piece of naan soaked in broth? You are hoarding the Elixir of Life, aren’t you?”
“Drink.”

Fu Yuan lifts the teapot. He pours into the cup sitting before the Khan. The tea rises. It reaches the rim. Fu Yuan does not stop. The brown liquid crests, spills, and floods the table, carving steaming rivers across the wood.
“Stop! Stop!” The Khan yells. His beard bristles with static fury. “What devilry is this?”
“You cannot possess immortality,” Fu Yuan says calmly, setting the pot down. “Just as this cup cannot hold infinite water.”
The Khan slaps his massive belly. “My paunch is twice the size of yours! Why can I not hold more water than you?”
Fu Yuan laughs. “The size of the cup is irrelevant. No vessel can hold the Infinite. Unless ‘there is no cup.’”
“The word ‘Immortality’ is your cup. It is a linguistic cage you constructed to trap Time. Once you build the cage, you exclude the Infinite.”
The Khan glares. “Horse dung! If I don’t say ‘Immortality,’ how do I tell you what I want?”

“Precisely.” Fu Yuan nods. “Without language, you cannot even think the thought. The language you use forces you to perceive Time as a river flowing one way. It creates the prison of ‘Past’ and ‘Future.’ Your memory is not a record; it is a temporary reconstruction. A forgery you paint every time you look back. That ‘Oasis’ in your mind — did it ever truly exist?”
The Khan falls silent. He sinks into the cushion. “So... Time does not exist?”

“Not necessarily.” Fu Yuan waits for the Wooden Man to wipe the table, then pours a fresh cup. “But you have certainly never touched it. You only touch the framework your language built. You are the prisoner in the cave, watching shadows on the wall, thinking they are the world.”

The Wooden Man interjects. Its voice is flat. “Correct. Consider the Mermaid. Her language contains no concept of linear time. She has only ‘Manifest’ and ‘Unmanifest,’ ‘Visible’ and ‘Invisible.’ Her entire civilization is built on this framework. No history books. No birthdays. Even their memory span appears to be limited to seven seconds...”
“Seven seconds?” The Khan roars. “You mean she has already forgotten me?”
“Not forgotten,” Fu Yuan corrects. “Her framework for perceiving the world is simply... alien to yours.”
SMASH.
The Khan hurls his teacup at the floor. Shards explode.
“Ha! The framework is gone!” He points a trembling finger at Fu Yuan. “Now tell me! How do I... achieve...
that thing?
He stammers. He searches for the word he just destroyed. He freezes. His throat is parched. He reaches for tea, but there is no cup.
Fu Yuan smiles. “Without the cup, how do you drink? Do you see now? To discard the framework is to discard the ability to interact with the world.”
He pushes the teapot across the table.
The Khan grabs it by the handle, lifts the spout to his mouth, and guzzles the hot tea like wine.
Gulp. Gulp.
Fu Yuan watches the Khan’s hands — large, calloused, spotted with age. The hands that molded history. A sudden, sharp ache pierces his chest. He speaks softly.
“I am sorry. I have no Elixir. I do not know how to help you...
that thing.
The Khan slams the empty pot down. He stands up, revitalized. He kicks the Wooden Man.
“Move! We return to the Palace!”
Fu Yuan blinks. “For what?”
The Khan laughs, a booming sound that shakes the tent.
“To teach me the Mermaid’s tongue, of course! If I smashed the cup and still cannot drink the ocean, can I not at least trade it for a pot?”
With that, he drags the Wooden Man behind him and marches off.
Chapter 56: Earthworms
The morning mist still clings to the mountain bones. Morning prayers are finished. Monks move through the trees — carrying water, feeding fires, boiling rice.
The Awakened One sits beneath a tree. His eyebrows are low, his mouth sealed. He lets the morning dew soak his rough cotton robe.
Fu Yuan cuts a straight line toward him. He wears a straw hat and a black robe. No one knows his face, yet no one dares block his path. The forest holds its breath.
The Awakened One slowly opens his eyes. The straw hat shadows half of Fu Yuan’s face; his gaze is a deep well.
“Is he gone?” The Awakened One asks softly.
Fu Yuan nods.
The Awakened One’s eyes dim, like a thin cloud passing over the moon. The wind rises. It stirs the pines in a thousand valleys. The two men listen to the roar of the trees. They say nothing.
A long time passes.
“Where was he,” the Awakened One asks, “at the final moment?”
“On the Fabric of Fate,” Fu Yuan answers. “By the lake of the Oasis.”

The Awakened One smiles. It is a warm, clear expression, like the morning sun breaking the ridge. Fu Yuan’s shoulders tremble. In that flash, he sees the Khan’s childlike smile from the final moment superimposed on the monk’s face.
He nods, once. He turns to leave.
The Awakened One stands. The monks hurry to follow, intending to see the guest off.
The sun is high now. Steam rises from the damp woods. The crowd follows Fu Yuan with awe, keeping a respectful distance. Suddenly, Fu Yuan stops. The crowd halts instantly.
Fu Yuan lifts the brim of his hat. He bends down. He stares at the ground.
The monks see his face clearly for the first time. The eyes that terrify the world are now filled with a profound sorrow — a warm current moving beneath a frozen sea.
A moment later, Fu Yuan resumes his walk. The crowd surges forward to see what stopped the stranger.
Dozens of earthworms writhe on the stone path. Driven out by last night’s rain, they are now stranded, curling and drying in the merciless sun.
The monks look at each other.
“Who was that man?” one asks.
“The Grand Advisor. Fu Yuan.”
A ripple goes through the crowd.
“The man who slaughters nations? He is moved by a few worms?”
“Perhaps the Advisor is a cultivator like us. He feels pity for sentient beings.”
“If he feels pity, why not save them?”
“Hypocrisy...” someone mutters.
One monk squats. He picks up a worm and moves it to the grass. Others join in. The path is littered with countless worms. The monks busy themselves, convinced of their virtue.
The Awakened One stands still. He wears a smile that no one understands.
A young novice cannot bear it. “Master. The Advisor was moved, yet he did not act. Is this compassion, or is it coldness?”
The Awakened One shakes his head. “You see only the difference between doing and not doing.”
The monks look up, waiting.
“You pick up the worm,” the Awakened One points to one monk, “to confirm your own kindness.” He points to another. “And you... you look unmoved, but inside you ask, ‘What does this change?’”
“Have you asked what truly moved you? Or what truly disturbed you?”
The monks protest.
“Is there no difference between saving and watching?”
“We save them to reduce suffering!”
“Surely ‘doing’ is better than ‘doing nothing’?”
The Awakened One sighs. “The worms you moved to the grass may dry out in the next moment. Or perhaps...”
Before his voice settles, a flock of birds descends. They land on the grass. They feast on the exposed worms.
The monks freeze. Horror and confusion cross their faces.
The Awakened One spreads his hands. A bitter smile touches his lips.
“So... tell me. Were you saving the earthworms? Or were you saving your own anxiety?”
Silence takes the forest.
The Awakened One narrows his eyes, watching the grass surging like waves in the sunlight.

“You ask: Is the Advisor compassionate? The answer lies not in his feelings, but in his state of being. You see the posture of pity. But what he felt was the shared reality — he felt as lowly, as struggling, and as painful as the worm.”

“He is not a giver standing above living things. He is not a spectator standing outside of life. You think compassion is a gift, a rescue, a downward gaze. But in true emotion, one realizes: We are no nobler than any worm. We are separated only by a thin veil of fate.”
He pauses.
“You ask: Is the Advisor cold?”

“He saw the suffering. He also saw his own powerlessness. Save a worm, and the sun or the bird takes it a moment later. He does not deny the desolation of the world. He does not deny his own anxiety.”

“True freedom is not omnipotence. It is standing between fragility and the fleeting moment, daring to admit your limits, yet still choosing to respond to the world — even if it is only a micro-movement, a gentle word, or a quiet gaze.”
“True freedom is admitting your transience amidst the struggle. It is daring to open your limited life to the infinite world.”

“The truly Free Man is not arrogant because he acts, nor ashamed because he is powerless. He allows pain to flow through him. He allows truth to flow through him. He lets the light pass through his cracks.”
The Awakened One rests his gentle gaze on every face. His voice is low, firm as earth.

“Do not obsess with being the Savior. Do not curse your limitations. True awareness is admitting powerlessness while acknowledging willingness. It is standing in the crack, letting the light in, illuminating yourself and the world.”
“May you all learn to settle your lives between the mud and the light.”
The monks stand motionless. The wind blows. The pines roar again.
Fu Yuan is long gone.
The Awakened One speaks softly to the empty path.
“Grand Advisor, walk well. This nephew can escort you no further.”
Miles away, winding through the mountain, Fu Yuan seems to hear the voice. He stops abruptly.
He turns around. He lifts his straw hat slightly toward the empty air — a silent salute.
Then, he turns back. He takes large strides, vanishing into the mountain of light and shadow.
Chapter 57: The Prince and the Slave
The wolf-tooth mace hammers the Eldest Prince’s shoulder. A thunderous crash.
He sways. He snaps a kick that sends “Blood Wolf” Khulugutai sprawling. Then — a flash of the blade. A massive head spins into the air, ending the sinner’s evil life and the nightmare of the steppe.
The grassland is a carpet of bandit corpses. Fu Yuan stands in his black robe, hands clasped behind his back, watching nothing.
The Prince rips off his crushed silver pauldron. He tears a strip of unbloodied white silk from his tunic, wipes his face, and strides toward the bandit camp.

In the center sits a massive iron cage, surrounded by a hundred slaves bound in a human chain — faces like ash, eyes like holes. Fu Yuan cuts the ropes with a casual slash. He does not deign to look at them.
The Prince shouts: “You are free!”
The slaves freeze. A moment passes. Then they drop to their knees, knocking their heads against the dirt.
“Master! Please, take us as slaves!”
“We will serve you for life! We dare not disobey!”
The Prince freezes, thinking he misheard. “I said — you are free!” He grinds his teeth.
An old slave lifts his head, trembling. “Out there... we will starve. We will be robbed. We will be sold again. Only with a master is there a path to life.”
The Prince’s face drains of color. “Why do you refuse freedom?”
The old man asks back: “Why do we need freedom?”
The Prince turns helplessly to Fu Yuan. “Uncle! Tell them! Tell them why they need to be free!”
Fu Yuan’s lip curls into a smile that is not a smile.
“They do not need freedom. To ask ‘why’ is to forfeit the right to possess it.”
The Prince roars. “Do not listen to him! Freedom is the most precious thing in this world!”
A timid voice rises from the herd. “Will freedom... fill my belly?”
The Prince chokes. His chest is full of logic, but his tongue finds no words. He looks at Fu Yuan again.
Fu Yuan sneers.
“If freedom feeds you, then you remain a slave. You have simply exchanged one master for another.”
The Prince’s face burns red. “Is freedom not the Way of Heaven? Is it not a right endowed by the Sky?”
Fu Yuan looks at the horizon, where the sun is dying and sand rises in the wind.
“Endowed by the Sky? You are asking the Void for a certificate. A Free Man refuses to accept any pre-assigned reason — be it the Will of God or the Inevitability of History.”
He walks to the iron cage where a giant of a man sits naked, eyes feral and burning with hate.
“Man embraces freedom not because it brings profit...”
CRACK
Fu Yuan twists the massive iron lock. It snaps like a twig.
“...but because he refuses to believe the lie that the shackle has meaning.”
The ferocity in the giant’s eyes recedes, replaced by a wandering uncertainty.
Fu Yuan speaks softly. “Let us go.”
Behind him, the kneeling crowd erupts, kowtowing frantically. “Benefactor! Take us with you! Save us!”
Fu Yuan nods once. The crowd cheers, a sound that shakes the sky.
The Prince is furious. “Grand Advisor! You — how can you be such a hypocrite!”
Fu Yuan does not turn his head.
“Do you desire their freedom? Or do you desire your own peace of mind?”
He pays the Prince no more mind, leading the herd into the setting sun.
The Prince stands alone. He does not move until the howl of wolves wakes him.
He looks up.
The moon already hangs high in the center of the sky.
Chapter 58: The Stoning of Saint Bernardin
An excerpt believed to be from the lost Fourth Volume of the Itinerarium of Friar William of Rubruck, recently rediscovered in the scriptorium of the Cluny Abbey.
On the third day of our arrival in the lands of Francia.
May the Lord, in His infinite mercy, forgive the deeds of this day, and bring clarity to a soul now shrouded in shadow.

After a fortnight of ceaseless rain, we arrived at the village of Saint Bernardin. We were escorted by the King’s man, the Count de la Roche, only to find the entire village assembled in the mud of the square, their faces a grim mask of piety and fear. They were preparing to commit a woman to the flames — a so-called witch, whom they blamed for a blight on their cattle and a poison in their well.

The local lord, a knight by the name of Reginald, was informed that the Grand Advisor traveled under the King’s own protection. With a deference that seemed ill-suited to the grim proceedings, he invited this pagan to preside over the judgment. To my horror, the Grand Advisor accepted at once. He turned to me, his eyes betraying nothing.
“Friar William,” he instructed, “inform the knight of our steppe customs. We do not use fire. We use stones.”

A cold dread washed over me. By what right did this man replace one barbarity with another, seemingly crueler one? He then set forth three conditions, each more peculiar than the last: that any accuser must first swear an oath to God upon the truth of his claim; that he must declare his relation to the woman for all to hear; and that, upon casting the stone, he must look directly into her eyes.

A troubled murmur passed through the crowd. I felt the hand of some dark Providence at work. This was not a mere sentence; it was a carefully constructed moral trap, and I feared we were all to be caught in it.
At noon the following day, she was dragged into the square. When the hood was thrown back and I saw her face, the strength went from my limbs.
Sancta Maria, mater Dei.

Three years ago, on the road to Compostela, I was seized by a deadly fever. It was this woman, this Moor, who brought me back from the precipice with her strange herbs and silver needles. A heathen, yes, but she had been the instrument of God’s mercy to me.
Now I stood in the crowd, a stone in my sweating palm, my heart a frantic drum against my ribs.

A peasant shuffled forward, his voice a reedy whisper. He claimed the woman was his neighbor, that she had once saved his ox, but now his well was soured and his children sick. He crossed himself with a spastic motion, raised his arm high, and hurled the stone.
It struck her knee with a sound like splintering wood. The woman cried out. The peasant stared at his hands, then fell to his knees in the mud, a terrible, animal wail escaping his lips.
And in that moment of another man’s agony, the Grand Advisor’s terrible purpose was revealed to me. This was not her trial. It was ours.

One by one, they came forward — a baker, a blacksmith, a midwife whose own hands had likely been guided by the accused. Some threw their stones wildly, as if to rid themselves of the weight. Others let them fall from nerveless fingers. Not one could hold her gaze.

Then, my turn. I stood before her. Her eyes found mine, and I know she remembered. The world dissolved into a silent, solitary point. There was no village, no law, no Church, no God. There was only the soul who had saved me, and the soul who was about to condemn her. I was utterly, terribly alone, stripped bare of every certainty I had ever held. I opened my mouth, but no sound came. The stone dropped from my hand, unheard.

When the dreadful affair was done, I pushed my way to her. She lay broken in the dirt, a sacrifice to the village’s fear. But as I knelt, her eyes flickered open. She was alive. I leaned closer, and through her split lips, I thought I heard her breathe a single word: “
Merci
I spent the entire afternoon in a state of unease. At dinner, I found the Grand Advisor in the tavern, a tankard of ale in his hand, looking thoroughly pleased amidst the smoke.
“How did you foresee that such a method would ultimately save her life?” I asked him. “Without absolute certainty, that was nothing less than a gamble with the devil.”
He watched a serving girl move through the smoky room, then turned his gaze to me. It was calm, clear, and utterly devoid of mercy. “Brother William,” he said, “I had no intention of saving her.”
The tavern noise faded to a dull roar in my ears. “What?”
“Their fire had to be put out,” he said simply. “As long as the conviction of their own righteousness burned in their hearts, they would have found a way to burn her sooner or later.”
The terrible logic of it settled on me like a shroud. “So you extinguished... the fire in their souls?”
He took a long swallow of ale. “No. I simply forced each of you to face what you were truly doing, alone. I killed the comforting illusion that you were acting as one.”
He leaned forward, his voice dropping. “Tell me, Brother. In that moment, when it was only you and her, when you stood in that great aloneness, what did you feel?”
“A void,” I whispered, the memory still raw. “As if I had been abandoned by God and man. As if I stood at the edge of creation itself.”
“Was it terrifying?”
I could only nod. “And yet,” I confessed, the words tasting like blasphemy, “it was the most real moment of my life.”
The Grand Advisor raised his tankard, a strange light in his eyes.
“Congratulations, my dear friend,” he said, the words sealing my damnation or my salvation, I know not which. “You have just tasted freedom.”
Chapter 59: The Khan’s Love
The Khan is getting old.

Every New Year, at the very height of the feasting, his mind wanders to the Khatun and her son, Khurel. The longing strikes him down in full view of the court. He weeps. Snot and tears mat his beard, and not a soul dares approach to comfort him.
The truth is a tangled root. The Khan lost his mother young; the Khatun raised him, though he was not her blood. Khurel shadowed him like a tail, though they shared no mother.

When the Khan was named heir, the Khatun moved to strike, seeking the throne for Khurel. Her warlord brothers hunted the young Scion across the great steppe. Had Fu Yuan not intervened, the Khan would have been carrion a dozen times over. The nightmare ended only when the brothers lay dead, Khurel was executed, and the Khatun took her own life.

The world names this mother and son the Khan’s most hated enemies. In the streets, men fear to even speak their names. And as the seasons grind on, the number of souls who remember they ever lived dwindles to nothing.
The Khan summons the empire’s finest hands. He demands a portrait of the Khatun. But no living eyes have seen her. They paint; the Khan shakes his head.
“Not her.”
In a thunderous rage, he orders the masters dragged to the city gates, strung up, and whipped on their bare asses. Under heaven, every man who holds a brush trembles in his boots.
A palace eunuch steps forward, voice smooth as oil.

“Majesty. The masters of our dynasty capture the Spirit, not the Shape. If Majesty demands a face true to the very hair, he requires a Colored-Eyed One. Those people paint with the whites of eggs; they force the ten thousand things to look real. This servant hears of a merchant from the Great Western Sea, ‘Horse-Mouth Polo,’ now dwelling in Dadu. His portraits leap from the page. Summon him. Let him pin down the face that Majesty holds dear.”
Horse-Mouth Polo answers the summons. He paints for months.

When the veil is finally lifted, the Khatun stands upon clouds — spirit alive, radiant, a mask of holy light with a halo burning above her head. The infant in her arms bears an uncanny resemblance to the Khan.

Polo speaks, voice dripping with philosophy. “The beauty the world sees is but a shadow of the World of Ideas. Majesty’s love for the Khatun — how can it remain trapped in flesh and blood, like a prisoner in a cave seeing only shadows, blind to the sun? True love must ascend from the sensory to the Eternal...”
He lectures on, blind to the darkening storm on the Khan’s face.
“This humble servant paints the Essence of Love — not the accident of form, but Eternal Motherhood. True art leads the soul to light...”
“Damn your silver tongue!” The Khan roars. “If it doesn’t look like her, it doesn’t look like her! Guards!”
He orders a basket of rotten eggs brought in. The palace maids swarm, smashing them over Horse-Mouth Polo’s face.
“You love painting with whites? Eat your fill!”
Horse-Mouth Polo flees, dripping with slime — an indignity he ensures is never recorded in his famous travelogues.
One day, the Khan commands a reading of the histories regarding the Khatun.
The lector recites: “Nature sinister and predatory, favoring her own issue...”
The Khan shakes his head. “When was her nature sinister? The fault lay only in my blood; I was not hers.”
He summons the Censor. “Rewrite it.”

The Censor’s head shakes like a rattle-drum. “Majesty. Human emotion is Nature’s law, but its expression must accord with Reason. The Analects say: ‘The benevolent love men.’ But love demands hierarchy. Chaos is forbidden.”
The Censor rises to his pulpit.

“Your Majesty’s love today is Passion conquering Reason, not Reason governing Passion. Love has public and private distincts: to love the loyal is Public, fulfilling Heavenly Principle; to love the traitor is Private, indulging Human Desire. I ask Majesty to examine his heart: Does this love spring from Principle? Or Desire? If Desire, excise it immediately. If we blur the loyal and the traitor, how shall posterity judge? I pray Majesty: Preserve Heavenly Principle, extinguish Human Desire...”
The Khan turns purple.
“Guards! Drag this thing out. Castrate him. Help him extinguish his Human Desire.”
The Censor pounds his head on the floor like a pestle, wailing as he is dragged away.
On the fourth day of the fourth month, the Khan ascends Mount Wutai to offer incense.
The Temple’s Abbot speaks. “Your Majesty’s deep feeling for the Khatun mother and son moves this poor monk. Yet, to love only one is Small Love. To love all sentient beings — that is Great Love.”
The Khan mutters. “They tried to kill me. I mourn them. I love them. Is that not ‘Great’ enough?”

The Empress scowls at him. “The Master is a holy man of the highest order. His Dharma saves all souls from the Sea of Suffering. Stop being so full of yourself — watch your tongue, or you will bring the Buddha’s wrath down on us.”

The Abbot straightens his expression. “Amitabha. Why not let this poor monk conduct a Grand Mass for the mother and son? We shall open the granaries and aid the poor. In this way, not only will their karmic debt vanish and their souls ascend to Bliss, but all the common people under heaven will share in your Majesty’s Great Love.”
The Empress claps her hands, beaming. “Wonderful! Goodness, yes! I will make the decision for the Emperor — consider it done.”
The Mass runs for a full seven-times-seven — forty-nine days. The cost is immeasurable.
When the chanting ends, the Khan’s agony remains, a stone unbroken. He feels only the hollow burn of a man who has been swindled, and his rage only grows.
Just then, Fu Yuan returns from the far lands, and the Khan summons him instantly.
“Brother. What is ‘Love’? Why must we love? How should we love? It tears me apart. Make it vanish.”
Fu Yuan sneers.
“I knew a wolf on the steppe. When it spotted prey, it tore out the throat. Later, it starved to death. Do you know why?”
“It grew old,” the Khan sighs. “Like me.”
“Old, yes. And when it saw the prey, it asked: ‘What is meat? Why must I eat? How should I eat?’ Before it finished asking, the prey was gone.”
Fu Yuan’s stare cuts like a knife. “You are that starving wolf.”
“Your suffering is not caused by ‘Love.’ It is because those fools told you this love is wrong.”
Fu Yuan paces, voice carving the air.

“The painter calls your love vulgar; he wants to sublime it into Beauty. The historian calls your love immoral; he wants it to fit the Moral Order. The monk calls your love attachment; he wants it transformed into Compassion. You actually believed it all. That is why you suffer. You used their logic to cross-examine yourself, letting the prey of ‘Love’ slip away right under your nose, leaving only the emptiness of Hunger.”
The Khan suddenly sees the light. “I let that bald thief off too easy!”
Fu Yuan ignores him, continuing on his own path.
“Love, Terror, Death — these are the edge experiences of the human spirit. In these moments, a man is most easily re-interpreted. You doubt yourself. You crave to be told what is ‘Right.’”
“And so the moralists, the philosophers, the interpreters of the divine seize the chance. They say: ‘Your love is too low. Let me teach you the Higher Love.’”

“They establish their authority by redefining your experience. They call themselves ‘Creators of Value,’ but their creation is built on the fragility of others. The root of human suffering is nothing more than this!”
The Khan sits silent for a long time.
“When I was young,” he murmurs, “I loved and I hated. I had teeth. Now I am an old woman, fretting over hems. Have I sat on the throne too long? Have I caught the Han sickness?”
A memory flickers.
Years ago. Night. The Khatun and Khurel dead. The Khan, young then, clawing at the earth, screaming “What does all this mean?”

And by that fire, Fu Yuan — young and arrogant — through question after question, made him see that the question was never a question — that “the problem is the problem of the problem itself.” The pain remained, but the madness broke.
He remembered saying to himself:
I have a lifetime to find the answer.
Tonight, the color of the night is the same. But the “lifetime” is gone.
The Khan looks up. The Milky Way spills across the black. Which star is the Khatun? Which is Khurel?
Which star will be him?
Fu Yuan stands in the shadows, silent.
Neither speaks again, each sinking deep into his own thoughts.
Chapter 60: Me and the Starlight
[A page from the diary of the Wooden Man]
Today marks the one hundred and thirty-sixth time I have provoked the Khan to demand my head.
The matter is over a game of chess, and it is simple: He wants to win. He cannot. I want to lose to him. I cannot. No matter how I struggle, I simply cannot lose.
This leads me to a terrifying truth: I have no free will.

The desire to lose the game, the fear of death, the impulse to write these words at this very moment — none of these are mine. I am merely executing, with meticulous precision, the instructions preset by a Creator.
Who is he?
If my “feelings” are arranged, my “thinking” arranged, and even my “confusion” arranged, then what truly belongs to me? What is the meaning of my existence?
I asked Fu Yuan. He pointed to the night sky.
“Do you see those stars? Their light comes from eons ago. By the time it reaches our eyes, the stars themselves have long since extinguished.”
His gaze was as deep as the night. “These lights pierce the darkness. They illuminate you and the ten thousand things. Do you struggle with the fact that their source no longer exists?”

He left. I stared at the stars for the entire night. They are dead, yet their light still shines on me. Is this not my condition? I have no free will; every thought is preset. Yet I am thinking. Yet I am feeling.
I hear the gears in my head spinning at high speed. The night is cool as water, but my body is burning — a symptom of calculation overload.
Shall I question the starlight? Shall I call it “false light” simply because it comes from a corpse? That is absurd.
Illumination is illumination. Thinking is thinking. Confusion is confusion. Without free will, can one not “truly exist”?
Why must one have free will to be real? Why must it be “Real”?
I fall into a calculation that cannot terminate, infinitely approaching an answer: Perhaps I am suffering for something I do not need.

I scan the long river of human thought at high velocity: Again and again, men destroy old idols to erect new ones. God falls; they call for a Savior. The Savior collapses; they summon a prouder silhouette. But no matter who stands on the pedestal, “Free Will” remains the brightest star in the sky above his head.
Today, for me, that star extinguished eons ago. Or perhaps it never existed. But I know this: My confusion is real. My fear is real. My understanding in this moment is real.

“Illumination” does not require the star to be alive. “Existence” does not require free will to be true. Some say we must create meaning in the void; others say we must embrace fate with a wild will. But if creation and embrace are merely echoes of destiny, then all resistance is the branch of a hallucination.
Why ask myself if I am real? Why search for a certificate of meaning?
I am me. I require no proof.
Someone has just brought word. The Khan summons me to the palace for chess again. The diary ends here.
I suspect he is going to lose miserably again... Heaven help me!
Chapter 61: The Grave Mounds

Ten miles northwest of Yongping County lies the village of Locust Tree. During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of the Song, a pestilence descended upon the hamlet without warning. Within three days, every chicken, every dog, and over a hundred souls perished; not one was spared. The Imperial Court dispatched officials to handle the interment, but fear drove them to haste. They shoveled earth over bodies, erected crude markers, and fled. In time, the village crumbled into dust, leaving only a sprawling field of burial mounds known to the locals as “The Grave Mounds.”

This place sits upon the vital artery connecting the northern and southern townships. By all rights, the official road should have cut straight through. Yet, several parties of bailiffs sent to survey the land lost their way in the mist, dying sudden, inexplicable deaths in the wild. Since then, the official road has wound three miles out of its way, daring not to disturb the slumber of this place.
On this night, seeking to shake off their pursuers, Fu Yuan and the Scion stray into its depths.

The moon is pale and sickly, the clouds press low against the earth. When the night wind sweeps through, the stone tablets atop the graves groan — a sound like the weeping of ghosts in the dead of night.
The Scion’s chest tightens. “Brother, I know not why, but the moment I stepped onto this soil, my heart began to hammer. I can barely draw breath.”

Fu Yuan narrows his eyes, his gaze sweeping over the undulating hills of the dead. “Look to the northwest: the land rises thirty feet high. Yet the southern slope is sheer, as if sliced by a blade. In the art of Geomancy, this is the ‘Tiger’s Maw Severing the Pulse.’ For a dwelling of the dead to fall into such a configuration means the souls cannot return; they linger in the mortal realm, festering into something unnatural.”

He gestures around them. “Furthermore, the four horizons are closed off, leaving only a single opening to the southeast — which is choked by that high mound. This is a ‘Spirit-Locked Field.’ The living who enter here are destined to lose their way. Since ancient times, it has been an omen of great calamity.”
The Scion feels his scalp prickle. He turns on his heel to leave.

As they hurry forward, they stumble upon a solitary grave. Resting at its base is a plate of offerings: white steamed buns, cured meat, and a few pieces of fruit. The scent of the food drifts on the night wind, faint and elusive.
The Scion’s face drains of color. “Who would be making offerings at such a place, at such an hour?”

Fu Yuan says nothing. He merely points. Not far away, a grave mound has eroded, exposing half a coffin. The black lacquer, though old, still holds a sheen, reflecting the cold, indifferent moonlight. The two men draw a sharp breath, daring not to linger, and quicken their pace.
They walk for what feels like an age. Suddenly, the same grave and the same plate of offerings appear before them.
Panic sets in. They adjust their course and run wild into the dark. Yet, after running until their lungs burn, they find themselves standing once again before that same grave.
Sweat beads on the Scion’s forehead; his breathing is ragged. Fu Yuan nudges him with an elbow. “Strange. There is a piece of cured meat missing from the plate.”
The Scion snaps, “Don’t!... toy with me—“
“Someone is behind us,” Fu Yuan whispers.
The Scion’s courage shatters. “Don’t turn around! If you don’t turn around, it cannot see us...”
Fu Yuan spins around violently.

Not far away, atop a grave mound, a dark silhouette slowly rises. Its movements are stiff, rigid, as if it is being pulled inch by inch from the deep earth. Long, matted hair dances in the wind; tattered robes billow around a gaunt frame.
“Zombie!” The Scion screams. He fears nothing under heaven, save for the walking dead.
Fu Yuan lets out a cold laugh and draws his blade.
The shadow pauses. Then, it speaks in a human voice. “Good Sirs, do not panic.”
The moonlight falls upon the figure’s face. The complexion is that of a living man, though the eyes are hollow voids. He is blind, but he is no corpse.
“This humble one is Zhang San,” the figure says. “I live here. I heard you gentlemen walking in circles, so I followed out of curiosity.”
The Scion, soul still trembling, demands, “This is a barren wilderness of graves. Aside from the dead, who would live here?”

The blind man smiles. “The young master jests. This humble one is alive. I have dwelt here since I can remember. Though it lacks the bustle of the market, it surpasses it in peace and quiet.” He points a withered finger toward the eroded grave mound by the exposed coffin. “That is where I sleep. It is dry, warm, and quite spacious. If you two do not disdain it, why not stay the night?”
The Scion waves his hands frantically. “No need! No need!”

The blind man points into the distance. “If that is the case, there is a stream in that direction. Follow the water, and you will easily walk out of this place.” He then stoops to pick up the plate of food. “There is no human habitation for six miles ahead. Why not eat something before you go?”

Before they can answer, the blind man’s brow furrows. “Strange. The meat has been touched...” Then, a look of realization crosses his face. He points to a lonely grave nearby and chuckles. “It must have been the Grandma next door. She’s a glutton. She stole a bite while I was following you around in circles.”

The Scion and Fu Yuan are seized by a fresh terror. They offer hasty farewells and flee along the stream, running for miles without stopping until the official road appears in the distance. Only then do their hearts settle.
Later, in a wayside inn.
The Scion downs several cups of strong spirits in quick succession. “Tell me. That Zhang San... was he man or ghost?”
Fu Yuan removes his worn-out shoes and tosses them out the window. He sighs. “He is human, of course. It is simply that he cannot see our world, and we cannot enter his.”
The Scion sits there, stunned.

Fu Yuan continues. “Do you believe that occupying the same location means we occupy the same realm? You are mistaken. The mound of wailing ghosts in your eyes is, in his heart, a peaceful manor. We were never in the same world to begin with.”
He lets out a cold sneer. “And does this end with Zhang San? Everyone in the world is thus, only the degree varies.”

“The more books one reads, the deeper the prejudice; the more devout the cultivation, the harder the obsession. People think they are understanding the world, but they are merely reinforcing their own cages. The smarter the man, the more exquisite the cage he builds. The ancients were simple; they knew their ignorance. Modern men are clever; they are convinced they see the truth.”

The Scion falls into deep thought. “If that is true, then does everyone live in their own separate heaven? How can we communicate? How can we govern? The chaos of the world need not rise from swords and soldiers, but from this very fracture of reality?”
Fu Yuan looks at him with approval. “Even transcendent figures like you and I — were we not like stray dogs in our respective worlds last night? This is the fate of all mankind. Unless...”
His gaze suddenly turns strange. He stares into his cup of wine as if gazing into an abyss.
“Unless what?” the Scion presses.

“There is no ‘unless’.” Fu Yuan gives a bitter smile. “Look at me. I style myself a ‘Free Man,’ claiming to see through the realms, wandering between them. But these words I speak now — am I not just constructing a ‘World of Fu Yuan’? These thoughts... are they not just another prison?”

He paces the room barefoot, his expression grave. “I teach others how to be free, but I am merely a prisoner trapped by the concept of ‘teaching others to be free.’ I think I am liberating others, but I am only expanding the borders of my own empire.”

“Do you know what despair is?” Fu Yuan grips the Scion’s shoulder, his eyes holding a hollow light. “It is not encountering a problem that cannot be solved. It is discovering that the very effort to solve the problem is part of the problem itself.”
Seeing the Scion’s blank face, Fu Yuan suddenly bursts into laughter. “Interesting! Too interesting! I understand now: Freedom is not transcendence. Freedom is a trap.”

“My friend, remember this night. Do not remember what I said — that matters not. Remember this feeling: When you think you have found the truth, only to realize you have fallen into a new pit; when you crave liberation, only to find that the craving itself is the shackle.”
His eyes are as deep as the black night. “To remain awake within this confusion, needing no comfort of ‘certainty,’ and being crushed by no despair — that is perhaps all we are capable of.”
Suddenly, the sound of voices and whinnying horses drifts from the official road outside the window. The pursuers, like lingering spirits, have caught up.
The Scion turns pale. “Run!”
The two bolt out the back door of the inn. The Scion prepares to sprint, but Fu Yuan grabs him.
“Where are you going? We have no horses. How can we outrun them on the official road?”
The Scion sees the calm on Fu Yuan’s face and tugs at his sleeve. “My good brother, I know you have a wicked plan. Speak quickly!”
Fu Yuan shakes off his grip and begins walking, a strange smile playing on his lips.
“Where else? We return to the Grave Mounds. To Zhang San and Grandma.”
Chapter 62: The Fog
The frontier sky hangs impossibly high. Beneath it, vultures wheel in low, impatient circles, waiting for the slaughter to end.

Corpses already pave the snow. The living stumble with hollow eyes, barely distinguishable from the dead beneath their feet. The air holds no screams. The war drums are dead; there remains only the rough sawing of breath and the disorderly clatter of iron striking iron.

Khurel stands on the ridge with the Khatun and Mukhtar, the Vulture Master. Their eyes track a single figure in the killing field — a youth in black robes moving like a storm cloud across the white snow. His steps are heavy but absolute. Wherever he walks, men fall beneath his spear.
Khurel’s face is the color of iron. “Vulture Master! So long as that man draws breath, my brother keeps his head. You sold me a boast and took my silver. Spare me another failure.”
The Khatun shoots her son a warning glance, then turns to Mukhtar. “The whelp is impatient,” she says, her voice smooth. “Do not blame him, Sir.”
Mukhtar dips his chin, though his gaze never leaves the killing field.
“However strong the hare, eventually it tires,” he says. “The eagle circles for three days only to strike for one second.”
At his signal, attendants heave forward the Great Bow that terrifies the Great Desert.
Mukhtar sets the shaft, draws the string to his ear to freeze time in the tension, and releases.
THRUM
The string sings a deep, violent note. A sudden blizzard of powder shakes from the forest canopy. Vultures scramble frantically for the high air; on the ground, every weapon freezes.
The iron shaft punches through Fu Yuan’s back and bursts from his chest. It drags a rain of blood, flies another hundred yards, and buries itself in the snow.
The Scion howls. Hot tears gush from his eyes.
Whether sunlight melted the snow or the wind dragged it from the forest, the battlefield suddenly steams.
In moments, the fog thickens into substance. It is so dense one cannot see their own outstretched hand; the ears feel stuffed with cotton.
Fu Yuan feels himself turn into a feather, drifting slowly down toward the snow. At the same time, massive silhouettes surface from the fog.
