Elephants emerge like beasts from the primordial flood, ghost-walking through the war. The riders are giants encased in matte black armor, faceless, absorbing the light.
The beasts tread the snow. Low thunder rolls beneath their feet, yet they leave no prints. Around them, men still hack and run, blind to the monsters above.
One leviathan halts before Fu Yuan. A trunk uncurls from the mist — gentle, immense — lifting him to place him upon the broad back.
A fatigue beyond resistance takes him. Consciousness dissolves into black water.

In the thick fog, the battle cannot continue. Mukhtar orders the horn; Khurel’s forces follow the sound and retreat. The Scion crawls through the slush, clawing at the earth in a stubborn search for the body, until his men forcibly drag him away.
Fu Yuan wakes in a pool of liquid gold, warm, smooth, like honey. His skin — no hole, no scar. Velvet.

The air is heavy with an otherworldly fragrance — blooming flowers, rotting fruit, a scent of unbearable temptation. A lethargy, deep and luxurious, soaks into his marrow, making him want to moan with the pleasure of it.
Tall, flawless beings lift him from the pool, anointing him with oils and dressing him in white silk and gold.
A man towers over the crowd as he approaches. He pulls Fu Yuan into an embrace, kisses his forehead, then turns to the assembly.
“He has walked the dust. He has sought in the cracks of time. He has walked alone under the silent dome. He is my seed left in the mortal coil, the echo of my blood, the reflection of my will.”
The giant looks down, eyes vast and tender. “My beloved son. The trial is over. You shall sit with gods. You shall drink the wine of immortality until the stars burn out and the oceans turn to dust.”
Days pass. Or eons. It is the purest joy Fu Yuan has ever known — a spray of foam falling back into the sea, a star returning to the night sky, a wandering ghost entering a mother’s womb.

Feasts. Music. They ask for stories of the dirt world. As he speaks, the memories feel distant, like scenes from a play about a stranger. When he says the name “Fu Yuan,” it is so alien it makes his heart seize.
One twilight, Fu Yuan finds his father by the sea. Seeing him approach, his father’s eyes fill with a smile, and he opens his arms.
“I am leaving,” Fu Yuan says.
His father gazes deep into him. The waves beat the sand in the rhythm of his breathing. “Why, my son? Are you not happy?”

“I have never been so happy,” Fu Yuan says, “and happy so purely, so completely. I cannot even describe it — I only know that I have entered an ultimate order capable of containing everything, an eternal structure where the ‘I’ is wholly accepted.”

His father laughs, and the stars and sea behind him glow with a soft light. “This is precisely the deepest longing of all living beings — to be understood, to be contained by a grand order that transcends the individual. My child, Heaven has never been a time, nor a place; Heaven is your state of perfection.”
Fu Yuan looks into the golden eyes. “Yes. So this is not Heaven, is it?”
His father pauses. The waves stop in mid-air, as if forgetting to beat the shore.
“Before I came here,” Fu Yuan says, “I was coal. Dark. Rough. Here, I am a diamond. Flawless. Eternal.”
“Coal is rough, yet it can burn. Diamond is flawless, yet it can only reflect. A seemingly perfect state is precisely what ends the possibility of ‘becoming.’ Is such ‘perfection’ still perfection?”

“People always think the destination of the quest is to find an ultimate order where everything can be placed. But if all questions are dissolved, the ‘I’ is no longer ‘I’; it will only halt, congealing into a link in this eternal whole. The reason ‘I’ am ‘I’ lies not in what is completed, but in what is unfinished.”
“This is an infinite process of ‘becoming,’ not a ‘result’ defined by others.”
His father’s eyes deepen. “So you would rather flow away in the finite than rest in the eternal?”
“Yes.” Fu Yuan’s voice holds a lucid resolution. “I choose to grow in the cracks, rather than be erased in the completeness.”
Silence stretches. Then, his father’s face shifts — pride, understanding, benediction. He touches Fu Yuan’s head.

“I understand,” the voice rumbles, a low vibration in the floor of the world. “What you seek is not an answer, but an eternal questioning. Not a destination, but an eternal departure. You refuse to become a perfect ‘masterpiece’ because you have chosen to become a ‘creator’ who never stops. This courage to refuse being sheltered by perfection and eternity... even the gods are shaken.”
“Go, my proud son. Embrace your limits. May you forge an infinity of your own in your forever-incomplete journey.”
The waves crash down with a thunderous boom, echoing through the universe.
“The freedom you can truly guard lies in everything you refuse!”
Violent pain and chill surge back into Fu Yuan’s body like a tide. He is in such pain he almost moans aloud.
He finds himself still stumbling through the dense fog, his body heavy and real.
The cold wind is like an icy rope. It threads in through the hole in his chest and out through his back, tying him tightly to this world.
Chapter 63: The King of Dingnan

Nanjiang, the Southern Frontier, is damp and hot; in summer, the heat becomes an ordeal. Inside the official residence, the King of Lingzhou reclines half-naked on a bamboo chair, his body slick with sweat, his gaze glazed and stagnant.

Following his failed political experiment, he was transferred from Lingzhou to this southern border and given the title “Grand General of the South.” Yet this barbarous land possesses nothing but a surplus of corrupt officials and unruly mobs; it is ungovernable. He has exhausted every method, yet achieved nothing. He watches the Great Khan’s birthday draw near, yet the holy edict summoning him back to the palace is late in coming.
He casts his eyes toward the crumpled sheet on the table — the Khan’s last handwritten letter, received a full year ago. It seems that in his father’s heart, this son was abandoned long ago.

Just as his thoughts drift into wild speculation, a sudden gale lashes the house, rattling the doors and windows with a sharp clatter. The room’s swelter vanishes instantly, invaded by a chill; thunder rumbles faintly at the sky’s edge.

A page boy rushes in, incoherent with excitement. It turns out the Indian mystic, Chhayanath, is performing a rain ritual in the south city. In less than the time it takes to burn a stick of incense, rain the size of soybeans began smashing down. The citizens are kneeling in awe throughout the streets and alleys. The storm began in the south and is sweeping this way.
Before his voice falls, the courtyard is drowning in a torrential downpour.
The King of Lingzhou looks out the window, seeing only a vast, watery blur. His spirit jolts awake. He barks: “Go! Invite that monk to me!”
Under the lamp, Fu Yuan studies the passage in the Prince’s letter:

“Master, I think I finally grasp your Way of the Free Man. Since all meaning is a human construct, and moral orthodoxy merely a historical accident, why should I be held hostage by these phantoms? As you utilize philosophy, I shall utilize religion; where you create teachings, I establish order.”

“A tool is just a tool; whence comes the sacred or the profane? In the eyes of foolish men and women, the likes of Chhayanath are gods, and religious temples are salvation. To us free men, they are but convenient instruments.”

Fu Yuan sinks into deep thought. Only when the attendant reminds him that the messenger is still waiting in the hall does he wake as if from a dream. He rolls up his sleeves, saturates the brush in thick ink, and writes four characters:
Keep Your Distance
He tosses it to the attendant.

Meanwhile, with the “Grand General’s” full support, Chhayanath establishes a new sect and manifests miracles. Within months, believers saturate the Southern Frontier; pilgrims travel from neighboring states to pay homage. The incense burns with unparalleled intensity.

And because they fear divine retribution, the once unruly mobs and corrupt officials actually turn self-disciplined. Theft vanishes; administration clarifies. Another half-year passes, and the Southern Frontier presents a scene of flourishing prosperity. The court and the commons alike marvel at the transformation. On this day, the imperial edict arrives: it heaps praise upon the “Grand General,” investing him as the “King of Dingnan” — the Pacifier of the South — and bestowing a thousand taels of gold and endless silks.
The King of Dingnan accepts the edict and thanks the Grace, but he does not return to his manor immediately. Instead, he travels with Chhayanath to the city outskirts.

The setting sun is blood; the green mountains are a sea. The two men stand at the peak, looking down shoulder-to-shoulder at the city under the twilight — orderly, yet teeming with the smoke and fire of human life.

The King of Dingnan exhales a long breath, breaking the silence. “Who could have imagined that fear and hope are such cheap yet efficient construction materials? They are enough to rapidly erect a temple of order upon the ruins of the human heart. The Grand Advisor once criticized this King for being too idealistic, for beautifying humanity, for harboring unrealistic fantasies. Now it seems his words were not empty.”

Strange light shoots from Chhayanath’s eyes. His voice is low, vibrating with a peculiar texture, like metal striking stone. “Does Your Highness not feel it? This scene is a painting. The ink is still wet.”
“A painting?” The King pauses.

“Yes.” A vague, elusive smile hooks the corner of Chhayanath’s mouth. “Fear. Hope. Piety. Greed. These are not bricks, Your Highness. They are pigments. The ten thousand people are a chaotic canvas. No good. No evil. No beauty. The Master of the Brush uses his Will as the stylus. He dips into the heavy ink of the human soul. He paints the Heaven and Earth of his own heart.”
The King of Dingnan trembles all over. “The Master means that so-called governance is, in essence, a creation?”

“It is the only creation.” Chhayanath’s lip curls; in his eyes, even the emperors of history are dust. “But the true Master does not merely paint. He must make the figures inside the scroll believe. Believe that the ink is breath. That they were born within the painting. And that outside the frame lies only the Abyss. When they believe this, the painting becomes the only tangible reality. And the Painter... tell me, how is he different from God?”

The two exchange a smile, everything understood in silence. Their gazes move in unison past the walls of the Southern Frontier, cast toward the distant North. In that vaster land, a larger canvas waits for their ink to splash.

In the days that follow, the King of Dingnan distills his governance of the south into a complete theory, naming it the “Nan Jiang Model.” Suddenly, politicians flock to the south to make the pilgrimage to this font of wisdom. Not only does the King rapidly rise as the most dazzling political star, but Chhayanath’s doctrine rides this updraft to spread across the great rivers, north and south, implicitly rivaling Buddhism and Daoism.
A folk rhyme spreads among the people: “
The Emperor nurses the rot; Dingnan brings the cure.

A consensus forms high and low: The Great Khan is old. His method of endless tinkering is obsolete, unable to cure the Empire’s deep-seated rot. Only the King of Dingnan’s “Nan Jiang Model” is the future — the sole hope for the Empire to shed its bones and be reborn.
“The Mandate is in the South.” Many already view the King of Dingnan as the natural successor; sycophants wear down his threshold.

The King is certain he has found the correct path. More than that, he believes he has spiritually surpassed the aging father, withering away in the distant capital. He submits a memorial to the Khan, requesting that Chhayanath be named Imperial Preceptor and his sect declared the State Religion. To him, this is not a request, but a declaration — a successful son displaying his final result to his father.
The Khan does not reply with ink; he replies with three thousand iron cavalry.

Iron hooves crush the deep dreams of the southern frontier. In less than two hours, Chhayanath and his core disciples are dead, their heads severed from their bodies. The King of Dingnan is personally escorted back to the capital by Fu Yuan.
The journey is silence. The King stares at the sky, eyes hollow, muttering to himself. Fu Yuan leaves him be.
Mountains rise and fall behind them. The air turns sharp and cool. High in the vast autumn sky, wild geese cry out, piercing the clouds, driving south.

The King of Dingnan shakes his head. A bitter smile twists his face. “The geese fly south. I head north. All these years, this nephew looked forward to the day he would return to the Capital. I never thought the day would come like this. Now, I would rather be one of those geese, flying as far as possible, rather than riding this prison cart home. Is this not irony?”

“Tell me, where did I err? You enjoined me to ‘keep distance,’ and I did. I kept my distance from Chhayanath, and from the religion I built with my own hands. To me, it was a tool from beginning to end. I never believed its doctrine for a moment. I remained awake.”
Fu Yuan watches the formation of geese. “Little Prince, do you know why the wild geese must fly south in formation, never leaving their positions for an instant?”

The King freezes. No one has called him “Little Prince” for many, many years, nor does he know why the Grand Advisor asks this, but he answers nonetheless, in a low voice: “I read the books of Natural Philosophy in my childhood. The head goose breaks the wind; it is the most toil. The flock follows close behind, borrowing its force to save strength. Only thus can they fly ten thousand miles.”
“Precisely.” Fu Yuan nods, though his tone holds no praise. “But do you know that the head goose also tires?”

Without waiting for the Prince’s answer, he continues: “When it tires, it retreats into the formation, and another goose takes its place. The entire flock is constantly rotating into that most arduous, yet most glorious position.”
Fu Yuan slowly retracts his gaze. Finally, he looks directly at the King of Dingnan, his eyes sharp as knives.

“The goose flies ten thousand miles not because of its bravery. It flies because it knows when to advance, and when to retreat. It knows it is the Head Goose. And it knows that in the next moment, it will not be the Head Goose. It maintains a lucid distance from that identity.”
“But you, King of Dingnan, fell in love with the feeling of being that Head Goose.”

“You mistook the roles of ‘King of Dingnan’ and ‘Pioneer of the New Model’ for your true self. You enjoyed the adoration of the ten thousand people; you became drunk on the power to reshape heaven and earth. You forgot that you, too, are just a solitary goose between heaven and earth. You forgot that you, too, need to retreat — that you need to keep your distance from that self wrapped in success and power.”

“Ordinary men mistake their Will for their Existence. They mistake the deeds forged by that Will for the Self. The Free Man is different. He observes his ambition as a passing cloud. He examines his success as a reflection in water. He experiences it. He refuses to become it.”
“This. This is the true meaning of ‘Keep Your Distance.’”

Beneath the vast dome of the sky, the prison cart grinds across the earth, cutting two shallow ruts. Moments later, the wind and sand smooth them over; the feet of strangers trample them into nothing. It is the merciless cycle of history: one actor exits the stage, another enters, and the “immortal deeds” they leave behind are nothing but tracks in the sand, waiting to be erased.
Chapter 64: Panic in the Inner Palace
The Great Khan, barely seated on the throne, has already discarded the saber for the charcoal stylus.
For half a month, he has been sprawled over the blueprints of the New Capital, clutching an iron ruler. He wields the stylus like a blade, and every stroke draws blood.
Winding alleys? Crooked lanes? What kind of system is this? Cut.
Noisy markets? Chaotic sprawl? An insult to the national prestige. Cut.

The Minister of Works stands by, unable to intervene, offering only breathless praise: “The beauty of the World must be thus — straight, square, impeccable. A fraction too much is excess; a fraction too little is a slant. Only absolute precision avoids blaspheming the Order.”

On this day, the Khan slashes the final cut. The Minister cradles the blueprints like a newborn heir and trots back to his bureau. The Khan rubs his stiff neck. He realizes it has been some time since he visited the Inner Palace. The Empress is likely displeased. He decides to take a turn, perhaps let the newly inducted Consort Jade press the ache from his shoulders.
He sets one foot into the palace.
He screams and scrambles out of the door, backwards.
The guards rush in to save the Emperor from the assumed assassins. Then they look, and every man sucks in a breath of cold air.
Assassins? None. Female specters? A whole army.

The concubines stand in a rigid formation. Their faces — plastered white walls; their brows — black roof tiles. Their lips are painted bloody red like bulls-eyes. Their hair is piled so high it scrapes the clouds.
They wear identical makeups and clothes, and they move their hands and feet in perfect unison.
One beauty steps forward from the formation. She lets out a musical laugh, yet somehow it makes the Khan’s skin crawl.
“Are you... my Queen?”
The Empress feigns hurt. “Has His Majesty been gone so long he no longer recognizes his own wife?”
The Khan sweeps his eyes over the row of women again. He cannot even distinguish where Consort Jade stands. He scowls. “What is this sorcery? What are you playing at?”

The Empress smiles coldly. “Does His Majesty not find his sisters beautiful? We hear the Emperor has spent half a month planning the New Capital, proclaiming that the Beauty of the World lies in uniformity and order. This Concubine manages the Inner Palace. Though I cannot set an example for the Empire, I certainly cannot allow the Harem to fall behind.”
Clap. Clap. Clap.
Applause breaks the silence. A man enters with a slow, deliberate gait. Long hair, black robes, eyes like deep pools. Who else but Fu Yuan?
The Khan realizes the trap. “I knew it! You two conspired to mock this Old Man!”
He looks again at the crowd of beauties, transformed into specters of geometry. A sudden realization strikes him. He barks: “Go! Wash those faces! Now!”
The women dissolve into giggles and scatter.
The Khan turns on Fu Yuan. “If you have words, speak. If you have gas, release it.”
Fu Yuan’s gaze cuts him like a knife, yielding not an inch.

“Shackle Beauty to a Standard, and you breed nothing but Ugliness. The world is blind to the woman you hold in your heart, but they see the city you demand. Finish your Capital of perfect uniformity, and every city under Heaven will become a mirror of this Harem.”
The Khan falls silent.
Fu Yuan tosses a scroll onto the floor. It is a memorial written by the Grand Guardian, Liu Bingzhong.
“Architecture is the Countenance of the State. If the Countenance is orthodox, the State is orthodox; if the Countenance is chaotic, the State is in peril.”

“Your Servant observes the rise and fall of ages: Great Beauty requires Unity; Minor Beauty breeds Disorder. The Ultimate Beauty of the Tao lies in harmonious unification, without a hairsbreadth of error. This aligns with the Will of Heaven above and the hearts of the people below. It is the Unchanging Truth of ten thousand generations.”
Fu Yuan sneers. “Rotten scholar. Empires need cornerstones, yes. But how can Beauty — a thing of mist and void — bear the weight of stone?”

“Beauty is the spontaneity that arrives unbidden. It is the sudden shock of a flower blooming around a bend; it is the wind unbinding your hair. It has no substance. It is merely the shadow of Existence. Try to grasp it, try to name it, and it flees. Think you have captured it? Open your hand. You hold only a corpse.”
The Khan drifts into a trance. A memory surfaces from the deep past.

He was a Scion then, a fugitive on the grassland with a young Fu Yuan. They had just shaken off their pursuers and reached a small barbarian tribe. And there he saw her — a girl surrounded by her people, heroic and bright, like a red sun rising from the horizon.
He snaps back to the present. He steals a glance at the Empress, her face still a mask of white plaster. He shudders.
Fu Yuan’s voice arrives, seemingly from far away, yet piercingly close.

“The common man fears freedom, so he establishes ‘Standards’ as shackles. He fears chaos, so he uses ‘Beauty’ as a shield. But if you ask ‘What is Beauty?’, it is like asking ‘What is Meaning?’ I tell you: It is nothing. It is neither profound nor holy.”
“If Beauty becomes Philosophy, it is the colonization of the mind.”
“If Beauty becomes Divinity, it is the strangulation of humanity.”
“If Beauty is granted Power, it becomes the worst of all tyrannies.”
The Khan sinks into silence. Fu Yuan speaks no more. The Empress lowers her brows in thought. Three people, three hearts, drifting in their own currents.
Suddenly, the cry of a swallow pierces the hall.
A single swallow lifts from the eaves. It arcs over the layered walls of the palace, heading for the distance.
The Khan watches the bird disappear into the timeline of history. He murmurs:
“The swallows who saw gilded halls and ancient kings...”
Fu Yuan finishes the line, his voice bridging the dust of a thousand years:
“...Now brush the eaves of ten thousand homes with their wings.”
They look at each other. Simultaneously, they burst into loud laughter.

In the spring wind, the swallow flies higher, further. It clears the rigid geometry of the palace; it crosses the straight lines of the streets; it soars over the square blocks of the market. Finally, it dives, dissolving into the smoke, the fire, and the messy, chaotic life of the ten thousand homes in the gathering dusk.
Chapter 65: The Pebble
The spring river flows forever east. Its surface holds the reflection of white clouds, yet the clouds do not drift with the current.
Fu Yuan lies on a riverside rock, his robe falling open at the chest, loose and unkempt. Across the river’s face, a monk walks toward him, treading upon the water.
Fu Yuan roars with laughter. “Boatless, you bald rogue! Still playing at your supernatural theatrics?”
Buzhou the Boatless steps onto the stony bank. Seeing that his insteps and the hem of his robe are slightly damp, he shakes out the fabric and chuckles. “Shameful. Truly shameful.”

He sits before Fu Yuan, studying him intently, then beams with delight. “One look at your eyes, Brother Fu, and I see your cultivation has deepened. The illusion of the Self and the illusion of the Other have vanished. You have reached the realm of the Great Bodhisattva — dwelling in no form, attached to no law. A joy! A triumph!”
Fu Yuan scoffs. “I practice no cultivation; whence comes this ‘depth’? And don’t think you can hustle me for a donation to that little temple of yours!”

Buzhou the Boatless turns solemn. “The Grand Advisor is mistaken. What is cultivation but the ceaseless stripping away of obsession, the shedding of fixed concepts? Is this not the very heart of your philosophy of the Free Man? You stand now at the terminus countless ascetics dream of reaching. Why feign modesty? Are you a millionaire begging in the streets? Or a beauty who knows not that she can topple kingdoms?”
He pauses, then sudden understanding washes over him. He claps his hands and laughs. “I see it now! Brother Fu walks the Supreme Path: the Cultivation of Non-Cultivation! This monk is too narrow.”
Fu Yuan offers no further argument. He picks up a pebble. With a flick of his wrist, he sends it skipping across the river’s skin, leaping toward the far bank in a spray of white water.
Through some unseen sleight, the stone reaches the far shore only to arc around and skip back across the surface. Fu Yuan opens his palm and snatches it from the air.
The Boatless Monk’s eyes go round. Before he can speak, Fu Yuan tosses the pebble into the woods behind them.

Buzhou holds his breath, waiting. A spring breeze drifts by, carrying the scent of grass and wildflowers, but the stone does not return. Astonished, he looks at Fu Yuan. The latter merely shrugs and spreads his empty hands.
The shock in the monk’s eyes flickers and dies, replaced by a dawning clarity.
“You understand,” says Fu Yuan.
Buzhou muses, “The monk can cross the river; the pebble can cross the river.”
Approval shines in Fu Yuan’s eyes.
Buzhou continues: “The pebble goes, never to return, yet the monk remains, waiting in expectation. The two acts appear the same, but the truth is divergent.”
Fu Yuan nods. “I am that pebble...”
He looks toward the woods where the stone has vanished.

“It has no target. It answers to no trajectory. It bears no heavy burden of spiritual rank. Its existence is not a story that demands to be told, nor a masterpiece that must be finished. It may cross the river to the far shore, or it may vanish into the woods on this side. No destination holds the power to define it.”
“And so, I am not a cultivator in the strict sense.” Fu Yuan slides both feet into the water, savoring the warm, soft touch of the spring current.

“Cultivation, self-actualization — these are the Great Projects men build to defy the absurdity of existence. I choose to stand outside the Project. I do not fight it. I do not need to conquer it. I simply decline to participate.”
As they speak, a small boat drifts downriver to the bank. The boatman sees them and hails them respectfully. “Do you two gentlemen require passage?”
Fu Yuan clasps his hands in thanks but shakes his head. “Alas, this humble one carries no silver.”
The boatman glances at the monk. “With a Master of such standing present, this lowly one takes no coin.”
Before the monk can speak, Fu Yuan laughs aloud and leaps aboard first.
Chapter 66: Gautama
Samarkand — ancient city steeped in silk and spice, where countless dreams and destinies converge like rivers, only to dissipate into mist.

One year ago, Gautama appeared in the city, speaking with a strange accent. Like the many drifters washed in by the tides of war, his robes were crusted with dried salt. Yet one had only to look into his eyes to be ensnared — they were as clear as the meltwater at the source of the Zarafshan River, yet behind them lay a soul as old as time.

Gautama never spoke of his origins. People guessed he was a fallen noble, for his fingers were slender and his bearing elegant, yet he did not mind doing the lowly work of a caravan porter. Within two months, however, he was discovered by Rostam, the city’s wealthiest merchant, and elevated to partner.

Rostam loved him like a son. He told Gautama, “You need do nothing but walk with me. Conversation with you fills me with inspiration.” To others, he said, “Gautama possesses a strange power. No matter how tangled the problem, he sees the essence. He seizes the vital artery. He is a born chess player, never a piece on the board.”

Fate did not always favor Gautama — he faced the betrayal of trusted partners, caravans vanished into the desert sands, markets collapsed overnight. Yet he never fled. He faced disaster as one faces a transparent glass: seeing clearly the contents of the suffering, then dismantling it, layer by layer.

He did not drown in questions like “Why me?” nor did he blame heaven or men. He simply asked the silence: “What is the problem? Why do I ask this? What remains in this moment? What can be done? What must be left undone?”
Time and again, Gautama rose from the ruins, flying higher than before. His fame spread, yet he treated his own success with indifference.

One day, he met Garan — the most renowned courtesan of Samarkand. She was the city’s incarnation of desire; to climb into her bed was, for many men, the ritual confirmation of having reached the pinnacle of life.

At first, Gautama was besieged by passion and unease like any other. Garan, with elegant ritual and exquisite skill, pushed love and lust to their zenith, making men believe this was the only conquest over loneliness. But after the heat faded, Gautama would grow quiet, examining his own thirst.

He repeatedly dissected the waves in his heart. When attachment rose, he peeled back the silk cocoon to identify the shape of the pain. When desire turned to obsession, he sifted through it to find what was real enough to hold.

He reminded himself: all pain and all pleasure can be deconstructed until only “immediate reality” and “available action” remain. Thus, he could immerse himself fully, yet leave without looking back; he could appreciate everything without the obsession of possession. He did not rely on Garan to confirm his existence, but calmly enjoyed every honest encounter. He did not deny desire, nor was he enslaved by it. He simply practiced freedom in every passing moment.
The wind from the great steppe brings the first snow to Samarkand, and with it, the iron rhythm of the Mongol Khan’s western expedition.
Rostam speaks to Gautama: “I am old. I have no faith I will survive these chaotic times. Sara is my only daughter, my pearl. I give her to you. Promise me — love her, protect her.”

He continues: “I have convinced the city nobles to surrender to the Mongol Khan. A messenger brought the Khan’s decree yesterday: the city shall be spared. The Mongol army arrives in three days. We will open the gates to welcome the Khan, and on that same day, hold your wedding. I have some friendship with General Subutai under the Khan’s tent; he will send a representative to attend.”

The grandeur of the wedding stuns all of Samarkand. Old Rostam embraces Gautama tightly, placing the signet ring of family authority into his hand. That night, nameless Mongol officers take turns toasting him. Gautama drinks much wine. As he staggers toward the bridal chamber, he feels, for the first time, that his life has reached the far shore of worldly perfection.
He pushes the door. It does not move. Then comes a woman’s scream.
He kicks the door open. The sight before him drives him mad — his new wife pinned to the bed by drunken Mongol soldiers, while others stand by, laughing wildly, unbuckling their belts.
Old Rostam rushes in from nowhere, roaring as he throws himself at them, only to be kicked to the floor. He struggles briefly, then moves no more.
Gautama loses all reason. He seizes a heavy silver candlestick and smashes a soldier’s skull into ruin. Another lunges at him, only to have his face crushed into a bloody pulp.

The remaining soldiers swarm him, pinning him to the ground. Fists and boots fall upon him like rain. In his ears, only Sara’s desperate wailing remains. Through a haze of blood, he sees a soldier mount his wife and draw a dagger—
BANG!
A massive firework explodes in the black sky, blooming scarlet as Sara’s blood.
Time loses its meaning. Gautama wakes in a space of total darkness. A tall figure with long hair and black robes stands with his back to him, hands clasped behind him — an apparition of Death.
“Gautama.” The man’s voice makes him shudder inexplicably.
“Am I dead?” Gautama asks.
Silence.
“Why?” Tears stream down Gautama’s face. “If I am dead, why is there still such pain? Is death not release? Is it not the void?”
The man slowly shakes his head. “It is not you who has died. It is the ‘smart’ Gautama.”
Gautama freezes.
“The Gautama who used philosophy to dismantle everything,” the man says. “The one who would say — ‘This is but an illusion,’ ‘This is but attachment,’ ‘This is merely a narrative.’”
Gautama clutches his head, rocking in agony. “I cannot do it. I cannot view this as illusion, attachment, or narrative. I do not want to!”
The man nods. “Because that Gautama is dead.”
Gautama falls to his knees, embracing the man’s legs, weeping uncontrollably. “Please. Let me die too.”
“It hurts, does it not?” the man asks softly, like a dear friend returning after a long separation.
Gautama nods frantically.
“Then, congratulations,” the man says.
Gautama throws his head back, glaring at him. “You are insane!”
The man leans down to gaze at Gautama. Gautama cannot see his face, only the burning intensity of his eyes.
“Tell me, Gautama. This moment — is it real?”

Gautama clutches his heart, groaning. “I am dead, speaking to a god of death. This should not be real. Yet I have never felt such reality. It is a reality that offers no excuses, no place to hide...” He trembles. “It is as real as standing naked in the snow.”
He meets the man’s gaze. “So, you congratulate me on feeling a ‘truer existence’?”

“An existence unwrapped by philosophy. An existence unexplained by wisdom. An existence unpromised by gods.” The man lifts Gautama’s chin, as if inspecting him. “Only the pain itself. Pure, inescapable pain that makes you doubt everything.”
“But I cannot bear it,” Gautama whispers, tears flowing unchecked.
The man remains silent.
“What should I do?” Gautama asks.
“You must stop asking ‘what should I do.’”

The man crouches, looking into Gautama’s eyes. “Listen, dear Gautama. I am not here to give you answers. I am not here to tell you how to walk out of pain. And I am certainly not here to take you to paradise. I am only here to tell you one thing.”

“Pain is the chisel the universe uses to carve you. It cannot be evaded. I am merely here to invite you to see: that story named ‘Suffering’ is a cage you wove for yourself to escape the reality of ‘Pain.’ If you fear pain, seek a doctor, take medicine. If you want a promise of deliverance from the sea of bitterness, seek faith.”
“Freedom is not a solution. Freedom is lucidity.”
Gautama asks, “Then what must I do?”

The man stands and turns to leave. “Do whatever you wish. If you want to cry, cry. If you want to hate, hate. If you want to despair, despair. Do not attempt to transcend them. Do not attempt to deconstruct them. Let them carve their marks into you, as wind and rain carve the stone.”
Gautama’s heart hammers against his ribs. “If this goes on, what will I become?”
The man walks into the darkness. “You will become You. A unique, destiny-carved You.”
“Wait!” Gautama shouts.
The man stops.
“Who are you?” Gautama asks. “I feel I know you.”
The man replies, “I am you.”
As the words fall —
BOOM!
The memories lost for nearly a year sweep back like a breaking dam, carrying the grit and fury of the steppe winds.

Gautama lets out a feral roar. He opens his eyes. He lies on a cold, damp floor, covered in blood and filth. His hands and feet are bound with iron chains; his body is a map of wounds. Intense agony storms through him, momentarily eclipsing the ache in his soul.
He sits up. The two clear pools of water in his eyes fade, darkening into an abyss with no bottom.

He snaps the iron chains with a casual twist of his wrists. He kicks the cell door off its hinges and walks out. Several Mongol soldiers rush at him, but meet his gaze and stumble backward, paralyzed by instinctual terror.
Snow still drifts from the sky. The city of Samarkand lies in a dead silence, as if the only sound between heaven and earth is the rhythm of his footsteps.
He arrives at the Great Khan’s command tent. The guards stand ready for battle, shouting, “Who goes there?” yet not one dares to take a step forward.
Gautama speaks.
“Tell your master...”
“Fu Yuan — has returned.”
Chapter 67: The Comet
A colossal comet cuts across the night sky. A folk rhyme quietly spreads through the streets:
The Emperor departs, the Empress holds sway,
The Crown Prince stands powerless, the Sacred Way in decay.
A Broom Star hangs high for ninety days,
Laborers and Eaters part their ways.

By the river, in a tavern, a man in a gray robe sits alone by the window. He is in the prime of his life, yet dust coats his shoulders and the weary lines of the world mark his brow. On his table, there is only a pot of wine and a plate of beef. Nothing else.
Thump, thump, thump.
A group of wilderness bravos storms up the stairs, bringing noise and chaos. After a few cups of wine, their talk turns to the state of the nation.

One man slams the table and stands. “The Laborer toils all day and never fills his belly; the Eater never sheds a drop of sweat yet feasts behind vermilion gates! Is there any justice in this world?!”
The crowd roars in agreement:
“The division between Laborer and Eater is truly the root of all human suffering!”
“Crown Prince Zheng’s theory is thunderous — it awakens the deaf! It has won the hearts of the people. We must follow him to the death!”
A scholar in a grime-stained long gown shakes his head, swaying rhythmically. “Do you gentlemen know why Crown Prince Zheng’s ‘Society of the Sacred Way’ was banned by the Regent Empress?”
The crowd eagerly asks for the reason.

The scholar says, “Wealth is Original Sin. You and I are penniless, so what sin do we have? The wealth is all in the hands of the ‘Eaters’ represented by the Empress. They are the most immoral breed on earth! Think about it — would she allow the Society of the Sacred Way to exist?”
The crowd nods, sudden realization dawning on their faces.
From the window, the gray-robed man lets out a cold laugh. It is soft, yet in the clamor of the tavern, it pierces the air like a needle.

The crowd turns, ready to explode in anger, but an elder at the head table stops them. He sees that although the gray-robed guest sits casually, his presence is as still as a deep pool and as imposing as a mountain. This is no ordinary man.
The elder clasps his hands. “This friend seems to have a different view. Why not drink a cup with us and offer some instruction?”
The man pauses, then rises. He walks over, leans his long sword against a pillar, and sits.

Before the wine touches his lips, the scholar interjects: “The gentleman understands justice; the small man understands profit. He who abandons justice to chase profit is a small man. Surely you do not disagree?”
The gray-robed guest drains his cup in one motion. “Good wine.”
He sweeps his gaze across the crowd and asks, “Does air have morality?”
The men look at each other, perplexed.
The elder muses, “Air is an object; it is everywhere. It seems to have no morality to speak of... What is your point?”
The gray-robed guest smiles. “If air has no morality, from where does wealth derive its morality? You and I breathe the air — what sin is there in that?”

“Wealth is the bedrock of the world’s rotation, just as man cannot survive without air. Yet why does no one discuss whether air has morality or original sin, while people discuss the morality of wealth with such relish?”
The crowd sits frozen. The tavern falls silent.
The gray-robed guest watches their expressions, amused. He remembers hearing these exact words not long ago, and his reaction then was no different from theirs...
That day, the autumn wind scoured the great steppe. In the twilight, the comet burned brighter than it ever appeared in the Central Plains.
Bonfires roared; songs drifted on the wind. A disciple presented a wine pot. Fu Yuan poured a cup, then passed the pot to Fu Ce.

Fu Ce took the pot and immediately began speaking of the situation in the Central Plains: Emperor Ling had died; the Empress and her faction held total power; the world believed Crown Prince Zheng was finished. But the Crown Prince had released a manifesto:
Birds and beasts know equality; only the human world has injustice, and this is the sin of wealth. Birds and beasts divide only by male and female; humans divide by Laborers and Eaters.
It continued:

The Laborer is born to be exploited by the Eater; this is an irreconcilable contradiction. The former creates wealth, the latter seizes it — does this accord with Heaven’s Principle? When I ascend the throne, I swear to level the rich and poor, to end the division of Laborer and Eater, and restore the Righteous Path to the world.

This theory was deafening. It not only won the support of the common people but attracted countless men of conscience. And the comet in the sky — was it not the harbinger of great change? Months ago, the Crown Prince had sent word to recruit him. Fu Ce thought to himself: Life is short. To shed one’s blood for a noble ideal — that would not be a wasted life.
Fu Yuan pointed to the pot in Fu Ce’s hand. “Drink.”
Fu Ce had spoken so much his mouth was dry. He lifted the pot and drank greedily.
Fu Yuan looked up at the comet in the sky. He watched it with total focus. Fu Ce followed his gaze.

Fu Yuan seemed to speak to himself. “That is nothing but a clump of ice and rubble. It is no messenger of Heaven. It is merely pushed by the solar wind, wandering the cosmos, flying toward the depths of the dark.”
Fu Ce forgot to drink.

“People believe they represent light, but in truth, they are rushing toward darkness,” Fu Yuan said, staring at Fu Ce with burning eyes. “They think they are creating history, but they are merely the dust of history.”

Fu Ce dared not meet Fu Yuan’s gaze. He lowered his head. “Has your nephew fallen into the trap of the mob again? But...” He looked lost. “The Crown Prince says human injustice is the sin of wealth. This sounds like truth.”
The wind blew past, carrying the scent of roasted meat and milk tea. Fu Yuan inhaled deeply. “Smells good.”
Then, he asked, seemingly at random, “Ce, tell me: does air have morality?”
Fu Ce wakes from the memory. The crowd is still staring at him, waiting for the answer.
“It is simple,” he continues. “Because air cannot be controlled, it yields no power. But whoever controls the moral definition of wealth controls immense social power.”

“The Empress and her kind say wealth is a gift from Heaven. The Crown Prince says wealth is the evidence of the Eater’s crime against the Laborer. They seem like fire and water, incompatible. But in truth, every political program, every religion in human history, is attempting to slap a label of ‘Good’ or ‘Evil’ onto a substance that has no moral attributes.”
“If you accept that label, you hand the authority to judge yourself over to them.”
Fu Ce picks up the long sword by the table and rises to leave. The elder’s eyes fall upon the sword. He notes its ancient, austere design, and his heart skips a beat.
“Dare I ask... what relation is the Grand Advisor, Fu Yuan, to you?”
Fu Ce answers, “He is my uncle.”

The tavern explodes into chaos. Chairs clatter, benches overturn. Some bow, some kneel. After a flurry of humble gestures, the crowd asks: “Is the Grand Advisor well? Did he leave any words for us? Where should we go? What should we do?”
Fu Ce shakes his head and offers a bitter smile. “He never gives direct answers. His last words to me were a question: ‘If the foundation is a lie, is the temple built upon it worthy of belief?’”
The crowd looks at one another — the Grand Advisor’s subtle meaning is too deep to grasp in a moment.

Outside the window, the colossal comet still hangs high. The world does not know that it is merely a clump of ice and rubble, being pushed through the void. It brings no light. It points no direction.
The only thing it can do is return, once every few hundred years.
Chapter 68: Brothers
The blizzard intensifies. The hoofbeats grow frantic.
“Your Highness!” a rider shouts against the gale. “The light is failing. We will be blind in this whiteout! Better we camp and continue at first light?”
The Scion throws his head back and laughs. “This man bled for this grassland! Do not doubt he can find his way with eyes gouged out!”
He points his whip. “There! My brother waits! Make haste! Or else the cold takes him!”
The thought pulls him back a year: He presses the bronze seal of Yanling — the City of Goose Quill — into Khurel’s hands. They embrace. The boy’s eyes burn with heat.
Rest assured, brother. I will guard this for you, and when you return, Yanling will be the anvil on which you and I forge the world!
The memory warms the Scion’s chest. He lashes his horse’s flank hard.
The wall of Goose-Quill City rises in the distance, emerging from the dark. A single grain of firelight flickers at the gate, fragile against the storm.
“Look!” the Scion shouts. “Khurel is holding the torch for us! Faster! Faster!”
They thunder to the gate in a frenzy, breathless.
But they meet no Khurel, just a lieutenant of the guards. Khurel is detained, explains the lieutenant, entertaining an envoy from Persia and cannot be disturbed.
The Scion clicks his tongue, impressed. “Persian envoys? Already? My brother really is something else!”
He strides toward the gate then suddenly pulls back, craning his neck to look at the two giant characters carved above the archway.
“These two... they seem different from when I left.”
The lieutenant bows his head, a flicker of mockery flashing in his eyes. “The Young Lord said the name ‘Goose-Quill’ was too petty. Three months ago, he ordered it renamed ‘Hero’s Pass.’”
Awkwardness hangs in the air.
“Good! Good!” The Scion forces the words through his teeth. “Dominating... real dominating!”
He slashes his hand through the air. “Get us drunk! Warm us up! And don’t you dare disturb your master’s business!”
The Scion sleeps until the dead of night.
Then Khurel barges in.

He sports a short, new beard and has thickened in the shoulders. His face is flushed with drink, his eyes manic. Still in armor, he crashes down onto the edge of the bed and unleashes a monologue, boasting of his achievements over the past year. He talks for a solid hour.
The Scion can barely wedge a word in. His eyes water from exhaustion.
Then Khurel says, “And another thing. I contacted the three eastern chieftains for you. I told them to pledge loyalty to you now, ahead of time.”
He looks pleased with himself. “Since you’re back, I’ll find a day for them to come kneel to you.”
The Scion jerks awake. “What?!”
“What is it?” Khurel frowns. “By the time the Kurultai gathers, you’ll have three tribes in your pocket. Is that bad?”
“Father Khan is barely cold! The steppe is a tinderbox waiting for a spark,” the Scion snaps, sleep vanishing. “How can you be so reckless?!”

Khurel’s expression hardens. “When Father was alive, you preached this same caution. You held me back, and I never got the chance to show him my true worth. Now he is dead, and you still try to block me?”
“Father didn’t just have us two sons,” the Scion says, his voice rising in desperation. “If we show our hand too soon—“
“Enough!”
Khurel springs to his feet — the sudden motion sends the teapot and cups crashing to the floor. The Scion’s guards burst in, hands on hilts, looking confused.
Khurel jams a finger toward the Scion’s nose.

“Do you take me for a child still sucking milk? Do you really think those tribes are coming for you? Let me be clear: Everything you have, and I mean everything, is within my grasp. If I wanted to crush you, it would be as easy as pinching an ant!”
He sweeps the room with a vicious glare, kicks the door wide, and storms out.
The men stand in stunned silence.
The Scion sits dazed for a moment, then can’t help but start weeping. After a long while, he wipes a handful of snot.
“Let’s... just go.”
Zara opens his eyes from meditation.
Khurel strides in. The candle flame dances wildly, making the deep wrinkles on the Persian prophet’s face writhe like a snake’s nest.
“You have done it,” Zara says.
“Ha! You should have seen my brother’s face!” Khurel laughs.
“Khurel! Oh, Khurel! What did I witness tonight?” Zara asks.
“What?” Khurel pauses.
“I saw a Lion!” Zara paces the room, his eyes burning with fanatical fire. “A Lion that finally shredded the rope from its neck!”
He spins around, his voice booming like thunder.
“Hark! The stars sing for you! They cry out: ‘Behold! A new Will is born! The footfalls of the one who dares trample the old world are ringing out!’”
“Your brother is the Sun of Yesterday. He has already set! But you... you are the Fire of Tomorrow, ready to burn across the grassland! But—“
He halts. His eyes blaze like torches.
“Lightning severed in mid-strike is more terrible than the dark! The Lion halted in mid-leap will be dragged back to the cave by Yesterday’s ghosts!”
“Answer me, Khurel! Do you choose to be the Divine, perfect and total? OR do you choose to be the Mortal, flawed and inadequate?”
“Will you allow the Sun of Tomorrow to rise from your blood? Or will you let the Shadows of the Past consume your Sky once more?”
His voice climbs higher still, shrilling.
“O Great Soul! Pulverize the final fetter! Let your thunder rouse the slumber of this vast land! Let the proclamation be heard by all men and women — a new Sovereign is born!”
Khurel stands dumbstruck. He trembles at the thought. “You mean...”
The door curtain lifts. The lieutenant walks in. “Reporting, Your Highness. The Scion and his party have left the residence. They should be out of the city by now.”
Khurel grits his teeth.
“Pursue them!”
“You know the rest of the story.” The Scion sounds exhausted. “I jumped off that cliff and crashed into your wagon.”
Days later, beneath a canopy of stars on the snowfield, the Scion huddles in furs, leaning against a rock, watching Fu Yuan feed the campfire.
“Ever heard of Icarus?” Fu Yuan doesn’t look up.
The Scion shakes his head.

“Someone gave him a pair of wings. But warned him: do not fly too high.” Fu Yuan rubs his hands over the fire. “But the moment the boy took flight, he forgot everything. He flew higher and higher. Closer and closer to the sun.”
“Like me handing Yanling to Khurel...” The Scion sighs. “The ending?”
“The sun melted the wax holding the wings together.” Fu Yuan turns. “So he fell. And drowned in the sea.”
The Scion stares into the fire.
Fu Yuan stands up, facing the snow-capped mountains on the horizon.
“There is a philosophy in this world. It prods you to use the Will to master Fate. Use the Will to conquer the World. Sounds magnificent, doesn’t it?”
A glint of contempt flashes in his eyes.
“But the disciples of this creed never turn their gaze inward. Their so-called ‘Will’ is nothing more than the engine of primitive instinct. It is as fragile as the wax on the wings of Icarus.”
“If the Will is the wing, then Unexamined Desire is its wax.”
“The Will to Power? Wax. The Vanity of the Ego? Wax. Even that desperate need to prove one’s existence? Nothing but wax!”
Fu Yuan leans in, his eyes cold.
“Think wings built of such frailty can ever bear the weight of true Self-Overcoming? Think again.”

“The believers of this creed delude themselves. They will believe their every Despicable Impulse possesses a Noble Meaning. But the truth is, their climb toward the sun is no ascent. It is but the prelude to falling — into madness, then ruin.”
The Scion stares, dumbfounded.

The young man before him stands in the snow with nothing to his name — yet he holds himself with a sovereignty that dwarfs the kings of this world. And the strangest part? It doesn’t make you hate him.

“Why save me?” The Scion’s voice is hollow. “I’m wiped clean. City’s gone. Brother’s gone. My men? Dead or scattered to the winds. The old man who covered for me is in the ground. All I have left is this title of ‘Scion’ — and right now, that’s just a target painted on my back. This head... it’s the only thing left worth a few coins.”
Fu Yuan studies the Scion’s head for a beat. He bursts out laughing.
“If I were after coin, why would I bother with that big head of yours?”
He looks up at the river of stars. His eyes burn with the same cold, distant fire.
“I saw a certain possibility. A possibility beyond all comparison.”
A tremor runs through the Scion. A strange premonition, electrifying his blood.
“Brother... are you saying you saw me taking the whole world?”
The corner of Fu Yuan’s mouth hooks up. Serious? Mocking? Impossible to say.
“Don’t flatter yourself. All you have done is shed a pair of wax wings. You are now — barely — qualified to contend for the world.”
That night, the Scion dreams.
He is back on the steppe. He and Khurel, riding hard, chasing the red sun. Khurel is the boy again — eyes feverish, shouting in pure joy, charging into the dying light.
Far in the distance, Mount Yanling stands shrouded in the sunset’s glow. It looks like a giant feather, dropped by an angel into the mortal world.
Chapter 69: The Audit of Freedom
The Khan has a nightmare. In the dream, Fu Yuan transforms into a goshawk and vanishes into the gray sky. The Khan wakes; the royal pillow is soaked through with cold sweat.
By afternoon, the Wooden Man sits across from him at the chessboard. Seeing the Emperor’s mind wandering elsewhere, the Wooden Man offers an observation.
“Your Majesty, the last two moves you played were mathematically unpredictable. They are also, objectively speaking, garbage.”

The Khan glares at the few pieces remaining on his side of the board. “If I had a few more Horses,” he grumbles, “I’d trample your algorithms into sawdust, no matter how fast that clockwork brain of yours spins.” He sighs, his shoulders slumping. “The Grand Advisor... he is like the Horse. He moves as he pleases, jumping over walls, defying straight lines. But he is too free. One day, he will simply ride off the board. And then what becomes of this Emperor?”

The Wooden Man’s painted wooden eyes roll in their sockets. “Your Majesty, your distress stems from an inability to control the variable known as ‘Freedom.’ The Grand Advisor’s high entropy comes from his unpredictability — precisely like the Horse’s trajectory.”
The Khan blows through his mustache. “Speak human!”

“To simplify,” the Wooden Man adjusts its vocal synthesis, “you fear his departure because you cannot quantify his freedom, nor do you know if there exists a redundant unit with equivalent specs to replace him. It is a classic problem of information asymmetry.”
The Khan pauses. The cold logic is strangely comforting. “So? What is the solution?”

The Wooden Man’s eyes glow with a sudden, brighter lumen. “To resolve your anxiety, we must transmute the abstract concept of ‘Freedom’ into observable, metrics-based data. I have analyzed every word and action of the Grand Advisor and successfully reverse-engineered a ‘Freedom Quantification & Assessment System.’ We shall call it: The Freedom Audit.”
From out of nowhere, the creature produces a scroll of sheepskin covered in dense, terrifying charts and formulas that no mortal mind could parse.

The Khan snatches it, staring blankly at the ink. He understands nothing, yet the weight of the nightmare lifts. He slams the table, dragon-face beaming. “Good! Excellent! You stiff-jointed blockhead, you’ve finally done something useful! I grant you the funds to establish the Ministry of the Freedom Audit. Scour the empire! Test every soul! Find me the horse with the highest score!”
The Wooden Man moves with terrifying efficiency. The public beta testing concludes swiftly, yielding fascinating data:
Subject A: The High Monk.
Famous for the Zen koan:
“Originally there is not a single thing, where can dust alight?”
Score: 58/100. System Analysis:
“Subject exhibits cognitive calcification regarding the concept of ‘non-attachment.’ He is attached to the idea of having no attachments. Recommendation: Deconstructionist re-education.”
Subject B: The Mad Poet.
Known for sprinting naked through the capital screaming,
“I have cast off all shackles!”
A favorite among bored court ladies. Score: 76/100. System Analysis:

“Subject’s behavioral loop is entirely dependent on the shock-feedback of external observers to validate his ‘free’ state. Diagnosed as Histrionic Performance Personality. The shackles have not been cast off; they have merely been made transparent.”
Subject C: General Zhang the Fearless.
Style name “No-Taboo,” alias “Self-Forgetting.” Score: A Dismal 35/100. System Analysis:

“Subject’s courage is a structural dependency relies entirely on the existence of an ‘Enemy’ to be conquered. ‘Fearlessness’ is merely a rebranding of aggression. If the external enemy is removed, subject’s existential framework faces immediate collapse.”
Then, the day arrives. The Wooden Man rushes into the hall, gears grinding with excitement. A perfect specimen has been found.
“Score: Ninety-nine,” the Wooden Man announces. “The missing point is only because the algorithm briefly struggles to classify him as a carbon-based lifeform.”
The Khan, delighted, demands the transcript.
Q:
Do you know who I am?
A:
Don’t know. Don’t want to know. If I knew, what of it? If I don’t, what of it? If you give, I take. If you don’t, I don’t hate you.
The Khan looks skeptical. He glances at the Wooden Man, who continues reading the transcript with reverence.
Q:
Do you want this alms?
A:
Naturally.
Q:
Will you kowtow for it?
A:
Giving or not giving is your liberty. Accepting or refusing is mine. I do not force your hand; you need not force my knees. We each keep our own gravity. That is all.
Q:
Do you know what suffering is?
A:
Hungry, I want food. Cold, I want warmth. Lonely, I want a voice. Not getting these things — that is suffering.
Q:
So, are you suffering now?
A:

To say ‘no’ would be a lie. But I have found this: when I am in pain, and I think about how to stop being in pain, the pain doubles. But if I simply starve, simply freeze, simply sit in the silence... it is manageable.
Q:
If someone told you your life is meaningless and you should just die, how would you answer?
A:

I would tell him that ‘meaning’ is his need, not mine. If he feels pointlessness, that is his sensation. If I feel amusement, that is mine. I do not force him to understand me; he should not force me to accept him.
“Enough!” The Khan yawns, waving a hand. “Bring him for the Palace Exam tomorrow.”
The next day, inside the Golden Hall.
“Summon the ‘Freedom Laureate,’ Wang the Dog!” the eunuch shrieks.
Slap, slap, slap.

The sound of bare, calloused feet on marble. Wang the Dog enters, guided by the Wooden Man. Before he even reaches the throne, the stench of him hits the court officials like a physical blow. He is a ruin of a man — hair matted into felt, eyes white and blind. Someone whispers that he is a beggar from the South Market, known by the nickname “Take-It-Or-Leave-It.”
The beggar stands with a vacant expression. The Wooden Man tugs at his rags, signaling him to kneel. He does not move.
The Khan frowns, then a cunning light flickers in his eyes.

“I am weary,” the Emperor announces. “Sage-Ambassador, take our Laureate to rest. Bathe him. Clothe him in silk. Feed him the liver of dragons and the wine of immortals. Let him sleep on down. Bring him back in a few days.”
The Khan waves his sleeve, ending the court session. The officials exhale, grateful to escape the smell.
Half a month passes before the Khan remembers Wang the Dog. He summons him.

When the Dog appears this time, the matted hair is shorn, the beard trimmed. He looks surprisingly delicate. He wears robes of embroidered satin, and his waist has thickened with the bloat of sudden gluttony.

He is led before the Khan. Knowing now that the presence radiating heat from the throne is the Son of Heaven, Wang the Dog collapses. He prostrates himself, shivering, forehead pressed to the cold stone.
“Laureate Wang,” the Khan rumbles. “Have you enjoyed the last fortnight?”
The beggar’s teeth chatter. It takes him a long moment to squeeze out a single syllable: “Yes...”
“I heard you dislike kneeling,” the Khan says softly. “Why not stand and speak to Us man to man?”
“This small citizen dares not! Dares not!” Wang trembles violently.

“I heard your nickname is ‘Take-It-Or-Leave-It,’” the Khan continues, his voice dropping to a dangerous purr. “I am thinking of granting you a lifetime of this wealth, this glory. Will you give me a ‘Take-It-Or-Leave-It’ now?”
Wang the Dog dissolves into terror, weeping into the floor tiles. “Dog dares not! Dog dares not! Mercy, Your Majesty!”
The Khan watches him for a moment, then waves his hand. “Get him out.”

As the beggar is dragged away, the Khan turns to the Wooden Man, who stands frozen, processors looping in confusion. “Sage-Ambassador,” the Khan smiles grimly. “In this game of chess... who won this time?”
The Emperor stands up, his playfulness vanishing. His voice becomes thunder.

“The Free Man I know,” the Khan points a finger at the empty space beside him, “is a man who pits his singular will against ten thousand armies! He is a man who can fall from the pinnacle of heaven to the depths of hell in a single night, and wake up the next morning ready to chew iron! He is a man who possesses nothing, yet looks down on Emperors with pity! He is the bastard who treats my Imperial Edicts like toilet paper!”
“The scars on his body and soul are more numerous than the patches on that beggar’s coat!”
“In the eyes of the world, he is a god. He possesses everything. Yet he chooses to live as if he has nothing.”
The Khan stops. He looks at the Wooden Man. “Do you understand, Sage-Ambassador?”
But he does not wait for an answer from the stunned contraption. He turns his gaze outward, past the pillars, to the sky.

It is deep autumn. The air over the capital is crystalline, the clouds piled high like mountains of white jade. The Khan narrows his eyes, searching the blue expanse. Sparrows and swallows chatter as they flit by.
But there is no eagle.
“Such a man...” the Khan whispers, his voice losing its thunder, leaving only the ache of a lonely king. “How many can you find in this world?”
Chapter 70: The Departure

The Eldest Prince changes into cloth robes. He slings a simple bundle over his shoulder and slips out of the Imperial Palace as the night deepens. His steps are firm; he carries a wish in his heart, vast and tender.
When he reaches the city gate, he sees a familiar silhouette. A figure in black stands at the end of the empty street, still as a ferryman waiting on a quiet shore.
Fu Yuan does not turn, yet the Prince stops. He knew the road would be hard, but he did not expect the first step to weigh so much.

He speaks first: “Grand Advisor, I see the world drowning in a sea of bitterness. Birth, aging, sickness, death. Loves parting, enemies meeting. It never ends. I want to find a Way — a path to end all suffering for every living soul.”
Fu Yuan turns. The moonlight traces his outline. There is no praise in his eyes, only a coolness, clear as the moon itself.
“If you are moved by your own mercy,” Fu Yuan says softly, “you are already disqualified from taking the first step.”
The Prince stands still.

“You are not moved by the suffering of the masses. You are moved by the grand narrative of ‘The Savior’ that you are writing for yourself. This pity in your heart? It is a medal you are pinning to your own chest.”
“Those drunk on the role of Savior cannot see true pain. Go back. Until you smash this golden idol you have built for yourself, you can go nowhere.”
The Prince stands frozen in the moonlight. He looks at his shadow on the ground and sees the halo of a false god. Silently, he turns. He walks back into the golden birdcage.
The second departure happens in midsummer.

The golden peaks of the palace shimmer under the distant night sky. The heavy, sweet scent of agarwood and roses has finally drifted away, replaced by a breeze carrying the fresh, raw smell of river grass and mud.
He comes to the riverbank outside the city. On a large stone sits a man — Fu Yuan again. He is barefoot, his feet dipping in the stream, kicking the water with a lazy rhythm.
The Prince sighs and sits beside him. “I have come again.”
Fu Yuan hums, unsurprised.

“You were right,” the Prince whispers. “That ‘grand narrative’ of saving the world... it was just me, intoxicated by my own nobility. That story is gone now. But I have found that the pain is even more real.”

He looks at the broken starlight floating on the water. “I see my Father aging, the frost creeping into his hair. I see the palace flowers open at dawn and fall by dusk; splendor fades in the blink of an eye. Nothing is permanent; life is suffering. I fear loss. I fear getting old. I fear the end.”
“This time, I leave not for others. I seek only my own release. I want to escape this endless pain.”
“So, you are not running from the palace...” Fu Yuan pulls his feet from the water, dries them, and slips on his shoes. “You are running from ‘Suffering.’”

“What you are chasing, under the pretty name of ‘Liberation,’ is actually ‘Joy.’ How is that different from eating when hungry, or seeking warmth when cold? It is just a more refined, metaphysical way of chasing comfort.”

Fu Yuan stands, dusting grass from his robes. “All the world’s prayers and practices, no matter how beautiful the words, are built on this simple instinct. If you cannot see through this, you are only trading one cage for a more comfortable one.”
The Prince falls silent. The water murmurs. The far bank looks enchanting under the moon, but he knows he is not yet ready to cross.
Deep Autumn.

The Prince has crossed the capital, crossed the fields, climbed a small hill, and arrived at a quiet town. He enters a tavern. Before he can speak, the waiter leads him to the best room upstairs. He pushes the door open. A table is set. Fu Yuan sits there, watching him with a smile.
He raises his cup to toast the Prince. “To your arrival.”
The Prince slumps into a chair, smiling bitterly. “Grand Advisor, how do you always know?”
“As long as you are thinking about ‘leaving,’ you can never leave.” Fu Yuan pours him a full cup.

The Prince holds the cup. “This time, I think I am ready. I have purged that self-righteous intoxication of ‘saving all beings,’ and I have seen through the hypocrisy of ‘fleeing pain to find joy’ — I see it as the source of all desire. This journey is not for the masses, not to avoid pain, not to find pleasure, not even for myself.”
“I only seek the Truth that stands above it all.”
Fu Yuan raises a hand. “Drink first.”
The Prince lifts the cup to his lips. It is empty. He does not know when Fu Yuan emptied it.
“’The Truth that stands above it all’?” Fu Yuan plays with the wine pot in his hand. “Before you go looking for it... are you ready for the possibility that it does not exist at all?”
The Prince stares at the empty cup, and shakes his head bitterly. Fu Yuan fills his cup again and laughs. “Then let us eat and drink.”
By the side of a stream.
The Awakened One takes off his shoes, rolls up his trousers, and hoists the Little Novice onto his shoulders to wade across the riverbed.
They are halfway across when the Little Novice can no longer wait. “Awakened One, what happened next? Did the Prince ever actually leave?”
The Awakened One couldn’t help laughing. In that laughter, there is relief, there is memory, and there is a trace of warm compassion for the self of years past.
Did he leave? Of course he left. Because you are riding on his shoulders.
He — is right over here.
Chapter 71: The Khan’s Glass Orb (II)

Two men, stripped to the waist and corded with muscle, slash their way through the rainforest. Their faces are masks of tangled beard and wild hair, yet nothing can obscure the feverish fire burning in their eyes. Who could imagine that the Khan and his Grand Advisor, whose names make the whole of Central Asia tremble, would appear here — like this?

Night claims the jungle. They find a high terrace by the riverbank where the earth is hard enough for camp. Fu Yuan gathers banana leaves to pitch a shelter and spread sleeping mats, then lies back to observe the stars — fixated on a delicate, crucifix-shaped constellation he has never seen before, one that leaves him utterly entranced.
The Khan gets a fire going and leans against a tree. Unable to wait another second, he draws out the glowing glass orb.

The way he cradles the orb betrays no hint of the emperor clutching a crown. Instead, he looks like a rough plainsman holding his firstborn son — a mixture of bear-like tenderness and nervous fragility.
He lifts the orb to his ear. Faintly, from within, comes the sound of a holy choir — hymns sung for him by the people of that tiny world. As he listens, tears well in his eyes.

For days, he has been their attentive deity. If they pray for light, he holds the orb under the sun to let it bask. If they beg for rain, he dips his finger in the dew and flicks it gently against their “sky.”

The world inside the glass transforms. Greed, cruelty, and oppression evaporate. Its people know that an Almighty Eye is watching them. They raise cathedrals to worship him; they create art to glorify him. He even feels that when they look up at their firmament, they are looking directly at him.
He drifts into sleep, the orb curled against his chest.
Morning brings disaster. The orb is gone.

They turn the jungle upside down for three days before finding it in a clearing. A monkey sits upon a rock, clutching the orb, accepting the adoration of its troop. The Khan, burning with rage, strides up and knocks the Monkey King dead with a single swing of his stick. The troop scatters in a screeching panic.
They lean in to look at the glass, and their jaws drop.
The cathedrals are ruins. Upon the rubble, factories rise, vomiting thick, black smoke. The people have smashed their idols to bow before the altar of the Machine.
The Khan holds the orb close, straining to hear. “They actually believe they evolved from monkeys!” He looks at Fu Yuan. “You can’t be serious!”
Sleep eludes them both that night.
Fu Yuan lies on his banana leaves, the starlight pooled in his eyes. The Khan edges closer and lies down beside him. “What the hell are you seeing?”
“What cannot be seen,” Fu Yuan says, a trace of melancholy in his voice.
“And what the hell is that?”

“A certain possibility,” Fu Yuan sighs. “Once, when the people in the orb looked at the sky, they might have met the gaze of a God. Now, they see only a silent cosmos of floating rocks and burning gas. The moment they believed they came from monkeys and turned to worship the machine, that first possibility vanished from their reality.”
The Khan stares at the sky, shaking his head. “I don’t see a God staring back at me, either. But surely there’s more up there than just rocks and fire?”
“Actually, there are other possibilities, aren’t there?”
Fu Yuan shakes his head. “When a fundamental cognition like ‘What is existence?’ solidifies into a collective consensus, all other possibilities of ‘being’ are erased from the eyes of men.”
He sits up, crosses his legs, and closes his eyes.
“What devilry are you up to now?” the Khan grumbles.
“I am going to look inside myself,” Fu Yuan says. “There is a world there, too. It needs my gaze.”
The Khan snickers. “I hope they don’t think they evolved from monkeys.”
Fu Yuan pays him no mind.
Bored, the Khan picks up the orb again. The “physical” barrier formed by their belief in rocks and fireballs seems to have thickened; his gaze can barely penetrate the haze.
“No...” he whispers — as if speaking to himself, yet also making a promise to the tiny souls inside. “A sky without life... a universe with only one possibility... that is no home for any world.”
He cradles the glass orb in his palm, closes his eyes, and begins to hum.
He has no idea what he is singing... He only wants them to hear something... different.
Chapter 72: The Three Hundred
The Scion has not yet lifted his cup when the milk tea begins to shudder and ripple. He stands abruptly, eyes alight.
“Finally.”
They rush from the tent. On the horizon, dust bleeds into the sky, turning the azure to a bruised yellow. The entire grassland vibrates beneath their boots.
The Scion grips Fu Yuan’s sleeve, his knuckles white. “Brother, our days of suffering are over.” Then, his face drains of color. “Do not falter now... please.”

The arrival is the Scion’s cousin, known to the Steppe as Gurban of the Three Hundred. He is named for the three hundred iron-clad wraiths he keeps at his back — a personal cavalry that follows him like a shadow. The Scion and Fu Yuan may have evaded the pursuit of Khurel, but with fewer than a thousand men, they are dust in the wind against the Khatun’s empire.
If they secure Gurban, the dead fish turns over; the impossible becomes inevitable.
But the wolf pack bows only to the alpha. Gurban does not come to submit. He comes to weigh his cousin’s soul — and, more importantly, to stare into the eyes of the young man named Fu Yuan.
When Gurban sees Fu Yuan, his eyes narrow, sparking with interest. He whispers to an aide. The aide departs.
The following four days are not merely time passed; they become the stuff of Steppe mythology. Between the feasting and the drinking, there are trials.

On the First Day: Fu Yuan lifts Bat’er, the Last Giant of the Grasslands, into the air. He holds him aloft as Hercules held the Titan. When the giant is finally cast down, the earth groans so violently that wild horses, drinking miles away, scatter in terror.

On the Second Day: Jebe the Divine Archer draws his ten-stone bow. He fires three arrows in a seamless, breathing rhythm. The first, Fu Yuan strikes down with his own shaft. The second, he snatches from the air with a bare hand. The third, he catches between his teeth.

On the Third Day: The Nineteenth Heir of Zhuge Liang lays a Stone Sentinel Maze. A hundred boulders form a geometry of madness; the wind within screams like weeping ghosts. No man dares approach. Fu Yuan steps inside, strolls through the howling void, and emerges on the other side before a cup of tea can cool. The Heir weeps, vomiting three pints of blood in his defeat.

On the Fourth Day: The Thrice-Crowned Champion of the Lhasa Sutra Debates — the Supreme Vajra of Wisdom — enters the tent. By the end of their discourse, the monk has bitten through his tongue and crushed his prayer beads to dust. He strips off his cassock, laughing wildly, and wanders naked into the wilderness, never to be seen again.
On the Fifth Day, Fu Yuan is pale, though his eyes remain unclouded. That night, the strange guests arrive.

They are a procession of white. Men and women alike wear robes the color of bone, their hair cascading to their waists. They wear no expressions. They radiate a glacial chill that pierces the summer heat, making teeth chatter in the firelight.
Their leader stands taller even than Fu Yuan. His eyes are singularities — black holes where the world ceases to exist. He offers Gurban a curt nod. No bow. No homage.
Gurban does not introduce him. He simply seats the stranger at his right hand, with terrifying reverence.
The Scion leans in, whispering, “This feels wrong, Brother. Watch yourself.”
Gurban raises a cup. “Master Fu is indeed a marvel of this age. I drink to you!”
The Scion cannot hide his smirk, but the Stranger in White finally turns his gaze to Fu Yuan. Fu Yuan offers a polite smile. The Stranger offers nothing.
“However...” Gurban swirls his wine. “Why, even now, does Master Fu hide his true capacity?”
“What true capacity?” the Scion asks, baffled.
The Stranger answers before Gurban can speak. “It is, of course, the insight into the ‘Truth of Existence’ regarding the Cosmos and Life.”
He has the voice of a grinding stone, scraping against the nerves.
The Scion bristles. “Teacher, you are mistaken. My brother here is worth ten thousand men. Is that not capacity enough?”

“Even with the strength of ten thousand,” the Stranger says cold, “what are you but an ant before the avalanche? Even if you live ten thousand years, what are you but a flickering spark against the eternal dark? If you do not pierce the veil of Existence, you remain common. You are merely fifty steps laughing at a hundred.”
The Scion is silenced. Gurban grins. “Perhaps the Immortal Master would enlighten us commoners on the secrets of the Universe?”
The Stranger’s gaze sweeps the tent like a physical weight. Every heart tightens, squeezed by an invisible hand.

“The Truth of the Universe and Life,” he intones. His voice is hard as inscriptions on a stone tablet, every word carrying the weight of a final, unquestionable judgment. “It is the eternal war and fusion of three Primordial Substances: Structure, Will, and Oblivion.”
He carves the air with his words:
“A stone is mostly Structure, therefore it is solid. It holds but a sliver of sleeping Will, therefore it is still. Yet, its essence is slowly devoured by Oblivion, destined for dust.”
“A flame has a core of high-density Will, therefore it is hot. It consumes sparse Structure as fuel, therefore it is formless. Every flicker is Will burning itself out within Oblivion.”

“And Man... Man is the most tragic alloy. Your bones and reason are forged of Structure. Your blood and desire are ignited by Will. But the space between your breaths, the pause between your heartbeats, and your deepest longing for peace? That is Oblivion manifesting within you.”

“The common man lets these three tear him apart, living in agony. The Sage uses supreme Will to reshape his Structure, until the moment of perfection — where he releases all, and returns in glory to the ultimate Oblivion.”
“This,” he declares, “is the only, absolute Truth.”
Silence drowns the tent. Even the Scion is struck dumb; the theory is so grand, so cold, so structurally perfect that it feels unassailable.
All eyes turn to Fu Yuan. He seems lost in a trance.
“Master Fu?” Gurban prompts.
Fu Yuan blinks, as if waking from a dream. “I share this view,” he says, his voice thick with sincerity. “My perspective aligns perfectly with the Immortal Master.”
The tent erupts in murmurs.
For the first time, the Stranger looks stunned. The Scion flushes red with embarrassment. Gurban throws his head back and laughs.
“Hahaha! I thought Brother Fu would offer some earth-shattering insight. It turns out he is merely a parrot.” Gurban shakes his head. “This Prince is disappointed.”
Fu Yuan feigns shock. “Does the Immortal Master not speak the only, absolute Truth?”
Gurban wipes a tear of mirth. “Naturally, he speaks the Absolute Truth. Do you not know who he is? Let me tell you—“
“Then,” Fu Yuan cuts in, his lips curling into a smile barely visible to the naked eye, “if it is already the ‘Only Absolute Truth,’ how could there be — and why should there be — a second voice?”
The room stills.

“You should be relieved that my view aligns with the Master’s. Why are you disappointed?” Fu Yuan leans forward. “Unless, deep in your heart, My Lord, you were hoping I would produce a different Truth to rival the ‘Absolute,’ thereby proving the Master wrong?”
Gurban’s smile freezes.
“Or perhaps,” Fu Yuan presses, giving him no air, “you do not actually believe the Master’s words are the Absolute Truth? And if you are unsure, what right have you to be ‘disappointed’?”
His voice sharpens into a blade. “By what standard do you judge which Truth is higher, if you do not possess the Truth yourself?”
Gurban’s face turns the color of raw liver. The Stranger’s eyes flicker, re-evaluating the young man before him.
The only sound is the crackle of the hearth and the uneven breathing of men.
Fu Yuan rises and bows deeply to the Stranger. “Immortal Master, your view on the essence of the Cosmos is enchanting.”
He turns to the room. “But in my mind, I have never reserved a seat for a ‘Final Answer.’ Because the search for a ‘Final Answer’ is, in itself, a trap.”
He pauses, letting the words settle.
“We must never stop seeking answers. But the moment any answer is worshipped as ‘The Only’ or ‘The Ultimate’ — it ceases to be liberation. It becomes a prison.”
CLATTER
The Stranger stands so abruptly he overturns the wine table. The tent gasps.
He signals to the white-robed figures. “We go.”
He leads the way out of the tent. The white-robed figures follow like phantoms, dissolving into the night. Gurban is about to explode in rage when the Stranger suddenly returns to the tent entrance.
