He bows low to Fu Yuan — a bow of peers.

“This humble one has spent a lifetime building what I thought was a perfect ‘Structure,’” the Stranger says softly. “Today, I realize I was merely constructing the most impregnable of jails, imprisoning myself as surely as my followers.”
He smiles, a human expression finally breaking the mask, and pats Fu Yuan on the shoulder. “If you ever have need of me, you know where to find me.”
“This trip was not in vain,” he sighs, shaking his head. He strides out of the tent into the dark, never casting a glance back at the others.
Fu Yuan sleeps until noon the next day. When he emerges, Gurban and the Cousin’s entourage are gone. The campsite is clean.
The Scion finds him and punches him hard in the chest. “You absolute madman! I have never seen my cousin so furious!”
Before Fu Yuan can speak, the Scion bursts into laughter.
“But he left them! The Three Hundred Iron Cavalry, and thousands of his finest troops. From this day forward, brother... they belong to us.”
Chapter 73: The Venerable

Fu Yuan has not set foot in the Central Plains for an age. On this day, seized by a sudden caprice to see what face the world now wears, he changes into a white robe, shears his hair short, and, accompanied by the Wooden Man, departs the Great Steppe.

Scarcely has he entered the Middle Kingdom when he senses a shift in the wind of the times. Along the road, he sees them everywhere: figures clad in flowing black robes, their hair worn long and loose, regardless of gender. In this sea of ink, his own white attire and cropped hair make him a jarring anomaly, a drop of milk in a dark ocean.
Stranger still are the slogans plastered upon the city walls.
One reads:
“Life is dear, love is dearer. But for the sake of Freedom, both can be cast aside.”
Another proclaims:
“Worship is a shackle, not a liberation. The abject soul who kneels before gods and gurus has no right to speak of Enlightenment!”
The Wooden Man starts. “That sounds like something you’ve said once.”
Fu Yuan nudges him. “Keep your voice down.”
The Wooden Man’s eyes swivel in their sockets, mesmerizing the children by the roadside.

A peddler tugs at the automaton’s sleeve. “Sir, you bear a striking resemblance to the ‘Wooden Venerable.’ This humble merchant has a few right here — freshly consecrated, carved from premium nanmu wood. Would you care to invite one into your home?”
He produces several wooden figurines. They are dead ringers for the Wooden Man, though smaller in stature and crafted from far superior timber. On their backs, stamped clearly:
Product of Freedom Hall
, accompanied by a motto that sounds suspiciously like Fu Yuan’s own rhetoric.

Fu Yuan, amused by the exquisite craftsmanship, pays for one. But this act of commerce summons a swarm of other vendors, pulling and dragging at him, an unbearable nuisance. He barely manages to shake them off when yet another hand tugs at his sleeve. He turned, ready to unleash his fury, only to find himself staring at a monk — an acquaintance, no less.
Fu Yuan bursts into laughter. “Buzhou the Boatless! You bald thief, you haven’t popped off to the Pure Land yet?”

Buzhou grins, his face a map of wrinkles. “This small monk’s cultivation is too shallow; I wouldn’t dare check out so casually. That the Grand Advisor hasn’t yet ascended to the skies — that is the true oddity.”

The two follow the monk to his small temple. It is a place of peeling paint and desolation. Yet, in the courtyard stands a colossal Winter Sweet — a Wax Plum tree — its yellow blossoms exploding in a riot of life.

Buzhou serves tea. “In this age, those who believe in gods and Buddhas grow fewer by the day. The incense smoke thins. I am fortunate to have this millennial plum tree; it still lures a few tourists through the gates.”
When Fu Yuan asks about the black-robed masses and the slogans, Buzhou looks at him with genuine surprise. “Brother Fu, do you truly not know? They are all your own spiritual brood!”

It appears that after years of relentless warfare, the belief systems of the old world have collapsed. As the gods fell silent, people were left drifting in a void, desperate for meaning. At some unknown point, Fu Yuan’s doctrine of the “Free Man” began to catch fire. It started in the intellectual circles, swept through high society, and then, fueled by merchants smelling profit, the black robes and wooden pendants became the height of fashion. Now, if one cannot recite a few catchphrases from the Analects of the Grand Advisor, one is viewed with disdain.
Buzhou produces a paperback volume:
Thus Spoke the Khan’s Grand Advisor
. “Look, even this poor monk has begun to study. A pity I am old and my memory fades; I often botch the catchphrases.”
As they spoke, a group of tourists enters the temple. Buzhou rushes to attend to them, and Fu Yuan, with the Wooden Man in tow, follows to watch the spectacle.

The group is composed of men and women in black robes, wooden pendants resting against their chests. They cluster around a tall, middle-aged man. With his shoulder-length hair, billowing black garments, unbridled expression, and piercing gaze, he looks more like Fu Yuan than Fu Yuan does.

The Wooden Man notes a detail: though all wear black, the silk ribbons tied at their waists differ in color. The young wear white. The elders wear green, blue, or red. The mentor himself appeared unadorned at first glance, but a closer look reveals a ribbon of pure black.
The crowd chatters around the plum tree. A young girl asks, “Old monk, I heard the Patriarch once stayed in this very temple. Have you met him?”
“More than met him,” Buzhou replies. “This poor monk and the Grand Advisor were bosom friends.”
The girl giggles. “You lying monk.”
Buzhou assumes a solemn face. “Monks do not utter falsehoods.”
A young man produces a silver ingot and presses it into Buzhou’s hand. “My junior sister loves stories of the Patriarch. Tell her one, quickly.”
Buzhou pockets the silver and leads the youths toward the tea room, but not before casting a sly, conspiratorial wink at Fu Yuan over his shoulder.
The black-robed man remains standing under the tree, hands clasped behind his back, letting the falling petals settle on his hair and shoulders.
A “Red Ribbon” asks softly, “Venerable One, the spring light is enchanting. Why do you seem touched by sorrow?”
The Venerable opens his mouth, his voice rich with magnetic resonance.
“Before me, I see not the ancients; after me, I see not the comers...”

He sighs. “The wisdom of the Patriarch, set against the ignorance of humanity, is like a comet tearing through the dark night. Blindingly brilliant, yet gone in an instant. This is what it means to say:
Before me, I see not the ancients.

“We, as Free Men, have broken the shackles of order, extinguished the bonfires of blind faith, and shattered the cage of absolute truth. Yet the masses still bob and sink in the sea of un-freedom, life after life. This is:
After me, I see not the comers.

He looks up at the plum blossoms. “This tree knows to wither and bloom, cycling through eternity. But the wisdom of the Free Man — if we do not strive to inherit and pass it on — fears extinction. The weight of this responsibility... it gives one pause.”
The disciples are moved deeply. Several Green and Blue Ribbons begin to sob quietly.
Fu Yuan cannot help himself. He bursts into loud laughter, drawing angry glares from the assembly.
The Venerable asks gently, “This brother appears not to be of our school. Do you have some enlightenment to share?”

Fu Yuan laughs a while longer before stopping. “I see that your Patriarch’s wisdom is rather limited. If his philosophy of the ‘Free Man’ were truly effective, how could it produce a brood like you — people who worship him as an idol, treat his words as dogma, and use colored ribbons to enforce a hierarchy?”
The Venerable pauses, then smiles — a forgiving, knowing smile. “Brother, it seems your understanding of freedom is limited. Which is understandable. Look at the sun.” He points to the sky.

“It is merely a ball of fire, yet it brings light and warmth to the world, so people respect and love it. The Patriarch is the same. He never taught men to idolize him, yet his thoughts dispelled the ignorance of the common man. We Free Men respect and love him for this. Is that wrong? If, to prove we do not worship, we deliberately feign indifference, is that not simply another form of attachment? A Free Man is exactly one who dares to love and dares to hate.”

“As for the Patriarch’s catchphrases, are they not the manifestation of wisdom? Years ago, by the river, the Patriarch taught Friar William the principle of the raft. Without the tool, one cannot cross the river. We Free Men have shattered the obsession with absolute truth, but are we so foolish as to reject the tools of wisdom?”
“And as for the ribbons...” He unties the black silk from his waist and drapes it over a low-hanging branch.

“You see? The ribbon is gone. Freedom is a process of constant letting go; it is never achieved in a single step. These ribbons serve to remind us that we have not yet reached the ultimate state of freedom. The unfree man sees only the ribbon. The Free Man holds no ribbon in his heart.”
This speech not only fills the crowd with joyous admiration, it sends the Wooden Man’s eyes spinning wildly. Even Fu Yuan is momentarily stunned into silence.

The “Red Ribbon” from before steps forward, pulling several cards from his robe. He hands them to Fu Yuan and the Wooden Man. “Our meeting today cannot be accidental. If one knows how to observe and utilize the raised threads on the fabric of fate, one can manufacture circumstances to rewrite fortune. If you two are interested, you might come and experience our course — the first session is free.”

Inside the meditation room, Buzhou listens to Fu Yuan’s recounting and roars with laughter. Seeing the two “experience cards” in danger of being crumpled in Fu Yuan’s grip, he snatches them away and tucks them into a drawer. “These are useless to you. Leave them for this poor monk to give away as gifts.”
Fu Yuan looks out the window.
It is late spring. A breeze sighs through the courtyard, and the petals of the Wax Plum drift down in a chaotic, beautiful cascade — a golden rain.
This coarse, blackened trunk has lived for a thousand years. It has birthed countless branches, and bloomed with countless flowers.
Which branch is the original? Which blossom is truly free?
Chapter 74: The Final Lesson
Night falls upon the Imperial Palace like a heavy curtain.

In the Emperor’s chamber, a solitary red candle burns down to a stubborn stump. The flame sputters and gasps, fighting the encroaching dark. A eunuch, moving with practiced silence, brings a fresh taper to replace it.
Emperor Ling, frail as a dried leaf, waves a trembling hand. “Let it be.”

As the second Emperor of the Great Yuan, Ling’s life — when measured against the towering shadow of his father, the Great Khan — lacks the thunder of conquest, yet it possesses a jagged, tragic geography all its own.
In his youth, his heart is a vessel of grand illusions. He dreams of being like his elder brother, born to the wild steppe, riding alongside their father to break the spine of the world.

In manhood, he seeks to build rather than destroy. In Lingzhou, he constructs a City of Ideals, only to watch it incinerated by the fires of human fanaticism. His “Southern Frontier Model” sweeps through the empire like a fresh wind, only to be strangled by his father’s own hand, leaving Ling to rot in a prison cell.

He lives a life of violent oscillation. He is the Crown Prince of the Empire, and then a nameless grunt in the snow. He is the favored son, watching the palace flowers bloom and fade in comfortable captivity; he is a banished shepherd, reading the text of the universe in the rolling clouds of the frontier.
“Your Majesty... Your Majesty?” The eunuch’s voice seems to drift in from a distant shore.
Ling opens his eyes, forcing his gaze to anchor on the dying candle flame.
“The Grand Advisor is here.”
At the sight of Fu Yuan, a spark returns to Ling’s eyes. He manages a weak smile. “You are just in time, Grand Advisor. This is my final lesson. But the teacher today... is neither you, nor I.”
Fu Yuan nods and sits silently at the bedside.

Ling studies the face of his mentor for a long moment. “Those who studied under you... my father, my brother... they were giants. They towered over me.” His voice is ravaged, sandpaper on stone, yet strangely calm.

“In my memory, triumph is always a fugitive spark, while failure is the eternal night. Even when I finally ascend the throne, I feel no joy, for my heart has long since let go of desire. And now, I face my final defeat. I am losing to life itself.”
He stares into Fu Yuan’s eyes, a child seeking a father’s validation, a flush of fever coloring his pale cheeks.
“Tell me truly. Am I a failed student?”
Fu Yuan’s eyes are deep pools, undisturbed by the wind. Slowly, deliberately, he nods.
Ling laughs, a dry, rattling sound. “You see? I seek one last comfort from you, and I fail even at that.”

He clears his throat, fighting for breath. “You taught me to be a Free Man. But I was dull-witted. I never quite grasped the lesson. Luckily, in life, one can fail and fail again; one can retreat and retreat again.”
“But look at me now. I have no ground left to yield. I have no time left to fail. I possess the world’s greatest teacher, yet I am the world’s worst student.”
“Though I am the Son of Heaven, I cannot command the tides of existence. Before the face of Death, I am but a man. Fu Yuan...”
He struggles to raise his head. “I will never leave this bed again. Tell me... am I free?”
Fu Yuan shakes his head.
Ling nods in agreement, his eyes closing slowly, a faint, paradoxical smile touching his lips. “And yet... and yet, somehow, I feel profoundly free.”
Fu Yuan repeats the words softly, weighing them like gold: “I am not free, but I am very free...”
“A contradiction, is it not?” Ling whispers.
Fu Yuan shakes his head. “Freedom is born in the cradle of constraint. To touch the boundless, one must first grasp the bars of the cage.”
“You were never my most brilliant student, Your Majesty. But you were always the most honest one.”

Fu Yuan leans in, his voice dropping to a resonant hum. “If a man refuses to admit his chains, he spends his life wrestling with ghosts. He mistakes freedom for escape, thinking it means outrunning his fate. But that is a lie. That ‘freedom’ only forges new irons — be they ideals, faith, or power.”
“True freedom lies not in the breaking, but in the knowing. It is the clarity of the waking mind — to know the terrible weight of fate, and to accept the necessity of the limit.”
Fu Yuan gazes down at the man who, though younger in years, looks far more ancient than himself. His eyes hold a profound, wordless emotion.
“When a man ceases to view Fate as an adversary, he has already surmounted it. When he stops looking for Liberty on the far shore, he finds he is already swimming in its waters.”
Ling lets out a long, satisfied sigh. “So that is the way of it.”
A sudden light, clear and sharp, floods his eyes. “If Fate is not my enemy, then how can I be defeated? Master... for this final class, let us yield the teacher’s chair to Fate.”
Dawn breaks over the capital.

Fu Yuan and his entourage stand on the ridge of the mountains outside Dadu. Once they crest this peak, the vast embrace of the steppe awaits them. The Wooden Man and the servants look back at the city, laid out below like a rigid chessboard, their eyes lingering with reluctance.
But when they turn around, Fu Yuan is already gone. He walks with a stride that holds no memory of the city behind him.
In the morning mist, his silhouette, clad in a rough straw raincoat, begins to blur into the landscape. The servants hurriedly shoulder their burdens and run to catch up.
As the poet sings:
With bamboo staff and straw sandals, lighter than a horseman, I tread.
In a cloak of rush, I walk life through mist and through rain, unburdened. I look back at that bleak and desolate path, where I came.
The wind, the rain, and the sun... they are one and the same.
Chapter 75: Silence

The second year of Emperor Ling’s reign is a season of abundance. The rains are punctual, the winds tempered, and the Great Yuan Empire basks in a newfound clarity of rule. But the era of the founders has ebbed away. The Great Khan is dead. Of the iron brotherhood that carved this realm from the earth, only Fu Yuan and a few brittle elders remain.

The imperial court is now a sea of young, hungry faces — the “New Guard.” They are men of ambition, restless and untested. When Fu Yuan occasionally deigns to attend the morning audience, he still occupies the station of honor. It is a sight that draws a cold tide of murmurs.
During this morning’s session, Zhao Wujiu, Grand Scholar of the Hanlin Academy, strikes.

“The Grand Advisor’s merits under the Late Emperor are eternal,” Zhao declares, his voice ringing through the hall. “But the world has turned. After great chaos comes the need for great order. As our spiritual lodestar, he should be a monument of virtue. Instead, he peddles this ‘Free Man’ heresy. It is a betrayal of the state.”
He flings his challenge like a gauntlet:
“If every soldier learns ‘freedom,’ who shall march to war?”
“If every merchant learns ‘freedom,’ who shall pay the tithe?”
“If every subject learns ‘freedom,’ who shall obey the law?”

Silence smothers the hall. The few surviving elders tremble with a silent, impotent rage, their god insulted before their eyes. The younger officials trade glances, their lips curled in triumph. Every eye in the palace fixes upon Fu Yuan.
Fu Yuan does not offer a rebuttal. Instead, he falls away from the room, surrendering to the images surfacing in his mind.

In his mind’s eye, the sky is the color of lead. A black fortress is wedged between two peaks like a jagged bone. A young Scion stands upon the ramparts, screaming into the gathering crowd below, his voice raw with the thrill of conquest:
“Does anyone on this great steppe dare look down upon us now?!”
The Scion gestures to Fu Yuan at his side:
“With my brother here, is there anything in this world we cannot achieve?!”
Though they number fewer than fifty, their roar drowns the howling north wind, shaking the very snow from the mountain crests:
“Nothing! Nothing!”
Fu Yuan’s silence unmoors Zhao Wujiu. The Scholar’s rehearsed tirades find no purchase; he is a man stabbing at a ghost. Confused, he turns to the throne.

Emperor Ling’s face is a mask of oceanic calm. He gazes at his teacher — this silent, isolated figure — and is suddenly pulled back to a midsummer night heavy with the scent of oil-lamps and blossoms.
In the imperial garden, Fu Yuan’s black robes had merged with the gloom, making him appear like a great, looming bat. His voice had been a low, warm vibration:
“What is the difference between a man who fears terror and a man who fears the dark?”
“Remember, little Prince — darkness is not your enemy. Only through fear do we recognize our limits. And only by knowing our limits can we hope to transcend them.”
In the present, the Emperor casts a long, chilling look at Zhao Wujiu. The Scholar instantly hits the floor. The Emperor stands and waves a dismissive sleeve.
“The court is dismissed.”
By afternoon, the capital is ablaze with the news. Zhao Wujiu’s words are copied and traded until paper in Luoyang becomes a luxury. He is the man of the hour, his fame reaching a fever pitch.
Inside the Imperial Study, the Emperor and Crown Prince Zheng sit over a go board. The black stones are in full retreat. The Emperor lifts a white piece, hesitates, and sets it down with a smile.
“Zheng’er, your skill is now peerless,” the Emperor says. “If you hold back this much, even my victory feels hollow.”

Zheng rises in a flurry of haste. “Father jests. Go is a minor path. Statecraft is the true game.” He pauses, his eyes bright with an irrepressible excitement. “Today, in the hall, there was a masterstroke played. Everyone thinks Zhao Wujiu won and the Grand Advisor lost, but I suspect it is not so simple. I wish for Father to resolve my confusion.”
“What is it you wish to ask?” the Emperor asks with a smile.

Zheng considers his words. “Of this world, who is a greater orator than the Grand Advisor? Why remain silent against such baseless slurs?” He can barely hide his excitement. “Is he baiting a trap? Perhaps he is waiting to strike with thunderous force and wipe out Zhao’s faction in a single blow?”
The Emperor snorts. “The Grand Advisor is a hero of the age, a man of light. He would never stoop to such shadows!”

Zheng drops to his knees in terror. The Emperor pulls him up, brushes the dust from the boy’s robes with a tender hand, and says, “Zheng’er, tell me: does the eagle soaring in the heavens explain the breadth of the sky to the sheep in the mud?”
The Prince stares, blank-faced.

“Silence is gold,” the Emperor says, his voice grounding the room. “What use is there in debating the meaning of the sky with those who only know the safety of the pen? If the Grand Advisor had spoken, he would have already lost.”
Zheng’s face lights up with realization, followed by a flush of indignation. “Then why not seize Zhao Wujiu for his insolence?”

“Seize him?” The Emperor looks into his son’s eyes, and the Prince hurriedly lowers his head. A cryptic smile touches the Emperor’s lips. “And then what? Shall I — or you — be the one to tell the world that Zhao is wrong?”
He picks up a black stone, rolling it between his fingers.

“Understand this: ninety-nine percent of the people in this world are sheep who need a fold, not eagles who crave the sky. Zhao Wujiu’s ‘truths’ are the walls of that fold. A ruler’s first duty — before the sheep have grown wings — is to protect them from the wolves. You reinforce the fence; you do not tell them ‘you should all fly.’ To tear down those walls now would not be a liberation. It would be a massacre.”
Zheng’s face turns from red to white. He is speechless.

“If I, as Emperor, declared the Grand Advisor’s teachings the sole truth,” the Emperor continues, shaking his head at his son’s shock, “then ‘Freedom’ would instantly become the new Imperial Decree. It would become a dogma, a subject for the civil service exams. His philosophy would become a tool for careerists — another cage, invisible but far stronger. If I had defended him, I would have been the one to destroy him.”
Zheng scratches his head, a rare moment of genuine confusion breaking through. “Then... is Zhao Wujiu right or wrong? I am utterly lost.”

The Emperor laughs. “His sophistry only scares children like you and those loyal, senile old men in the court. In the current order of the ‘sheepfold,’ a society of ‘Free Men’ cannot exist. And if a society truly evolved to be composed of Free Men, its very structure would have transcended the need for sheepfold rules.”
“Do you not see? Zhao Wujiu’s argument is nothing more than a ghost story told by a shepherd to scare himself,” he says, looking out the window.

Meanwhile, across the city, Fu Yuan also stands by a window. Behind him, his old comrades are finished with their grumbling, nursing their wine in bitter silence. He pushes the sash open, takes a deep breath of the cool air, and looks toward the palace in the distance.
He shakes his head and smiles.
Chapter 76: The Port of Genoa

News that the Grand Khan’s colossal armada has anchored at the Port of Genoa sweeps through the city in scarce half an hour. Under the gaze of thousands, Fu Yuan, clad in robes of black sable, treads slowly across a pontoon bridge strewn with fresh blossoms. The Doge, Baldassare, and the Bishop have been waiting upon the quay for a considerable time.
The feast is barely concluded when a report arrives: the Scourge of the Seas, a pirate hunted by every nation of the Mediterranean, has been taken.
The Doge is overjoyed. “God has manifested His power! The Envoy is truly blessed by the Heavens!”

It transpires that this wretch had raided a fleet bearing tribute to the Great Khan years ago, only to vanish without a trace. Who could have foreseen he would surface here today? After pawning a few priceless gems, he made for a tavern to gamble with abandon, where he was recognized by a brother-in-arms from his navy days. Together with the City Guard, they subdued him; all involved in the capture suffered minor wounds.
As they speak, the man is dragged forth. It is — to the shock of all — the “Monkey King,” Alexander.

His raiment is in tatters, his face a swollen mask of bruises. Meeting Fu Yuan’s burning gaze, he lowers his head in shame. The days cast adrift on Monkey Island bred in him a sickly terror of “solitude” — wherever a crowd gathers, he must press into the throng. Upon reaching the bustling ground of Genoa, he naturally could not restrain himself. Heeding no counsel, he stole away into the city for debauchery, courting his own ruin.

Fu Yuan says with pleasure: “On this journey, aside from paying respects to His Holiness the Pope, I was charged by the Great Khan to capture this beast and escort him back to Dadu. We have a saying in my land: ‘We scour the endless steppe for the wolf, only to find him sleeping by our own campfire.’ It seems Genoa is indeed a land under God’s favor. I shall make sure the Pope hears of this miracle.”
Baldassare is elated. He orders the prisoner immediately packed into the stoutest iron cage and conveyed to the Envoy’s ship, while lavishing rewards upon the informant.

Fu Yuan raises a hand. “On the steppe, we hold no sin viler than betraying a friend for glory. Yet, the Great Yuan’s laws are ever clear on rewards and punishments. Pray, Governor, reward the informant on my behalf, and then, in accordance with the rules of our grassland, have him publicly given thirty lashes with a horsewhip. I shall then have no need to drag him before the Great Khan.”

The assembly is stunned. But Baldassare reacts with swift wit, bowing low. “Your Excellency, rest assured. Pray report to the Great Khan on my behalf: this official shall personally pull down that fellow’s breeches!”
Fu Yuan rises from the table; the crowd hurriedly attends his departure.
Back upon the ship, Fu Yuan releases Alexander from the iron cage. The pirate forcefully kisses the hem of his robe.
Fu Yuan says only: “It is of no consequence.”

Friar William fetches salves to dress Alexander’s wounds, scolding him with a pained heart. “The Lord says He is with those who mourn. Dear Alexander, solitude is not dreadful; what is dreadful is that you flee the Lord’s grace out of fear. That silence is the very moment to hearken to the Lord’s voice — why must you use worldly revelry to escape it?”
Alexander is ashamed, making only submissive sounds.

Fu Yuan shakes his head. “The solitude he endured on Monkey Island was a calamity. It was physiological, bearing no relation to thought or faith. Consider: were a field mouse trapped upon that isle, would it suffer loneliness?”
William and Alexander freeze simultaneously.
“Therefore,” Fu Yuan spreads his hands, “that is merely because the species are different, bound by differing laws of survival.”

William asks earnestly: “Grand Advisor, you often use the solitary eagle to symbolize the Free Man, and the herding sheep the unfree. The former seems not only to fear no solitude but to love it. Could it be that your philosophy transcends the limits of species?”
Fu Yuan laughs. “Your God created the species, and endowed them with their various chains. If I could ignore these chains, would I not be more brilliant than God?”
He then grows serious. “You are confusing two wholly different vessels.”

He hands William an empty wine cup. “The first is this empty cup. It thirsts to be filled by external things — be it crowds, voices, the approval of others, or the expectations of society. It stems from lack.”

He fills the cup with red wine gifted by Baldassare, signaling Alexander to take a draught. “This is wine from the Abbey of Saint-Émilion. According to the Doge, even the Pope uncorks such a bottle only on Christmas Eve.”

Watching the pirate swallow the wine, he reveals a satisfied look. “The other is this cup, filled with fine wine. It is self-sufficient. It needs no external thing to prove itself; its state of existence depends entirely on itself. It is a ‘cognitive’ independence, a high degree of sobriety. It is not swept up by the collective hallucination, nor is it terrified of its own independence from the world.”
“The state of the Free Man is the latter — he does not flee the world, nor does he refuse the world. He simply refuses to be defined by the world.”

Alexander shows a look of fascination. “The state you speak of... I yearn for it greatly.” Then his eyes darken. “Though my limbs are sound, it seems now the defect in my heart is so vast even the Lord cannot aid me. Have I lost all title to be a ‘Free Man’?”
Fu Yuan laughs involuntarily. “By your logic, if a warrior loses an arm, is he no longer a brave man?”

His gaze searches the deep black surface of the sea. Suddenly, several silver arcs leap from the water, gliding through the air for a stretch before submerging again. It is a school of flying fish; they skim close to the hull, as if provoking the souls on board.
Fu Yuan’s eyes show a look of appreciation.
“Freedom is not the absence of limitation, but the choice to leap amidst limitation. Their wings can never fly high, but that brief flight is eternal freedom.”
“Whose heart bears no trauma? Whose life knows no limitation? The qualification for freedom lies only in whether you dare to leap out of the water.”
In the cabin, under the flickering candlelight, Friar William dips his quill in ink, recording what he has seen and heard this day:

Tonight, I once again witnessed the strange catechism of this Eastern sage. Facing Alexander’s soul, near collapse from extreme isolation, he did not, like the Gospel of my Lord, offer comfort or the hope of salvation.
He simply... coldly pointed out the reality. He attributed the pain that is enough to swallow the mind to a defect inherent in the creature known as “man,” rather than a sin or trial of the soul.

I have never heard a philosophy so “honest,” nor one so lacking in “hope.” It promises no cure, depicts no Further Shore, and does not even posit that a “better” state necessarily exists. It seems to demand only one thing: to see clearly where one stands, whether that place be Heaven or Hell, and admit it is true.
This confuses me, and stirs a faint unease. Is the price of truth that one must abandon all desire for “salvation”?

Laughter drifts in. He looks through the window toward the deck, only to see Fu Yuan and Alexander exchanging cups, chatting and laughing, evidently both slightly tipsy. Fu Yuan seems to know William is watching; he draws a new bottle of wine, uncorks it, and waves the vessel in this direction, signaling William to join.

William smiles bitterly and shakes his head, dropping the quill, and pushes the door open to go out. On the opposite shore, the entire city of Genoa has sunk into dreams. The clamor and vanity seem blown away by the summer night’s sea breeze; only the torches on the deck remain sleepless.
